writeup: Cholowsky’s father, Dan, played Minor League ball for eight years and has been a long-time scout for the Blue Jays, Padres and Reds. One of the best players in his area for 2023 is his son, a two-sport star who serves as Hamilton High School’s quarterback in the fall. Cholowsky showed off an intriguing combination of baseball IQ and tools over the summer, and though looks were a bit limited because of his commitment to football, he continued to impress during his senior season.As a younger player, Cholowsky looked like the type who would hit a ton but not be able to stay at shortstop. His profile has changed some as he’s matured, with more conviction on his defensive ability and some small questions about the impact of his bat. He has a knack for contact from the right side of the plate and doesn’t swing and miss much, but he’ll sometimes get out of his legs and is content with shooting the ball up the middle. He’s hit-over-power right now, though more impact could certainly come as he fills out his 6-foot-2 frame and learns how to drive the ball more consistently.Cholowsky plays shortstop kind of like how he quarterbacks, showing an ability to throw on the run. He’s very comfortable throwing from all angles and combines that with natural comfort, confident hands and plus instincts. While he’s not a pure burner, he is a plus runner and uses his speed well on both sides of the ball. Interested teams will have to contend with the potential of him playing two sports at UCLA, but he has a chance to go in the top few rounds, just like former Hamilton standout Cody Bellinger did in 2013.

Scouts have a difficult time finding any flaws in Emerson's game, with one noting the worst thing he can say about him is that he's not Bobby Witt Jr. Like Witt, Emerson is a rangy prep shortstop from the Dallas/Fort Worth metroplex with all-around tools, even if his aren't quite as loud. He transferred from Argyle HS to Fort Worth Christian HS to play his senior season under former big leaguer Rusty Greer, who also mentored Witt while he was growing up.Emerson's advanced skills stand out as much as his tools, starting with the way he stacks up quality at-bats, making good swing decisions and barreling balls to all fields from the left side of the plate. He generates impressive exit velocities with a pretty left-handed stroke, and while he doesn't sell out for power, his bat speed and projectable strength portend future 25-homer pop. He presently focuses on just making hard contact in game action but can launch balls in batting practice and finished second at the High School Home Run Derby during All-Star Week.Emerson is an average runner out of the batter's box but pairs solid speed underway with good instincts. The Texas recruit may be a bit underrated with the glove because he's such a fluid defender at shortstop that he makes difficult plays look routine. He has a strong arm, quick hands, sound fundamentals and a finely calibrated internal clock, so he's a lock to stay at short. His makeup is as impressive as the rest of his game.

A late bloomer as a Georgia high schooler, Lackey didn't receive any NCAA Division I offers until his senior year in 2023, and he didn't draw much attention from scouts because he attended few showcase events. Now he's the best catching prospect in the 2026 Draft and poised to join Jason Varitek, Matt Wieters, Joey Bart and Kevin Parada as first-round backstops out of Georgia Tech. He has dramatically improved each season with the Yellow Jackets, ranks fifth in Division I in OPS (1.307) entering NCAA tournament play and won't last longer than the top three or four picks.Lackey's right-handed swing can get a little busy, but that doesn't prevent him from making consistent contact to all fields. He's extremely patient, rarely misses fastballs and has the plus raw power to provide at least 20 homers on an annual basis. His flat stroke produces too much groundball contact but he's driving balls harder than ever and in the air a bit more often this spring.Extremely athletic for a 6-foot-2, 215-pound catcher, Lackey isn't quick out of the batter's box but can flash plus run times once he gets going. He's very agile behind the plate and should become a solid receiver and blocker once he improves his focus and consistency. His strong and accurate arm is another asset, and he looked capable at third base while playing 13 errorless games there in 2025.

Gulliver Prep south of Miami has had more than 20 players drafted over the years, but just two first-rounders. The most recent was George Lombard Jr., now an ascending star in the Yankees system, and now it’s his brother Jacob’s turn. The younger son of former big leaguer George Lombard, Jacob showed off an exciting combination of athleticism and baseball IQ on the summer showcase circuit, putting him in position to come off the board well before his brother did at No. 26 overall in 2023.A 6-foot-3 premium athlete at a premium position, Lombard is a right-handed hitter with plenty of juice in his bat. He has the acumen to be a plus hitter in the future, though he did have some swing-and-miss on fastballs up in the zone over the summer, with a bat path that wasn’t matching the velocity he was facing. He’s already added strength, and speed, of late, with potential plus power. His main weakness might be that he’s a people-pleaser and listened to too much advice on the showcase circuit, with most evaluators believing he’ll be just fine once he lets his athleticism play. He’s an easily plus runner who should be a threat to run at the next level.Lombard will be able to play shortstop for a very long time, with outstanding range, feet, hands and action. Some might have questions about his arm strength, but he has plenty when he needs it. All of this adds up to him being a potential top of the first round pick.

Dillon Tate was the No. 4 overall pick in the 2015 Draft out of Santa Barbara. Ten years later, USCB's Tyler Bremner was taken with the No. 2 pick in the 2025 Draft. Flora, who relieved as a freshman and then moved in to join Bremner in the Gauchos rotation in 2025, could be the next in line to be an early pick in the first round. After a solid sophomore season and even better junior campaign, it wouldn't be as much of a surprise as Bremner was.Flora's combination of size and arm strength should intrigue most teams. The 6-foot-5 right-hander already offers premium velocity, with a fastball that sits in the 96-97 mph range and regularly touches triple digits, with good shape, carry and armside ride to it. He'll employ two different sliders, both of which can flash plus. There's a slower 78-81 mph sweeper with a lot of horizontal depth and also a harder, more gyro-like traditional slider, thrown 86-89 mph. He has plus feel to spin and just needs to gain a little bit more consistency with shape. His hard changeup is coming along, a kick change with downer splitter depth that now flashes plus.A solid strike-thrower who maintains his velocity, Flora might not have the same feel to pitch Bremner had, but he's more physical than his predecessor. He's posting every week for the Gauchos and has separated himself more than any college arm in the class, so he should fly off the board early in the first round.

Eric Booth Sr. turned down the Blue Jays as a 34th-round pick in 1993 and became a record-setting kick returner at Southern Mississippi. His son has inherited his speed and athleticism and will use it to forge a future in baseball. He has added polish to his swing and defense this spring, and his combination of tools and skills should land him in the top 10 picks.Booth has an unconventional setup and pumps his hands away from his body, sometimes leaving him with a choppy-looking left-handed stroke. He nevertheless makes consistent contact and barrels balls while displaying a good sense of the strike zone. His bat speed and strength translate into impressive exit velocities and could result in 20-25 homers per year if he smooths out his stroke and launches balls in the air more regularly. He won the home run derby at the Perfect Game All-American Classic last July.One of the fastest runners in the 2026 high school class, Booth can get from home to first in a little over four seconds and ran the quickest 60-yard dash (6.33 seconds) at the East Coast Pro showcase in August. To make the most of his plus-plus speed, the Vanderbilt recruit will need to hone his basestealing instincts and improve his reads and routes in center field, and he's making strides in both areas. He has addressed the biggest weakness in his game, working to improve his arm strength to average.

After winning a Georgia 6-A state championship as a Houston County HS (Warner Robins) sophomore in 2021, Burress tied for the state prep lead and ranked fifth nationally with 17 homers last spring, then won another state title as a senior. One scout likened him to former Peach State prep first-rounder Michael Chavis with less power but more athleticism. If everything comes together at the plate, the Georgia Tech commit could have solid tools across the board. Burress has an aggressive mentality with an uphill right-handed stroke geared to launch balls to his pull side. His bat speed and strength give him plus raw power and he's willing to work counts, though he does come with some swing-and-miss concerns. He'll have to prove that his approach will work against quality pitching and that he can catch up to velocity up in the strike zone. While Burress is capable of playing all three outfield spots, he'll have to retain his solid speed to remain in center. He displays fine defensive instincts and has plus arm strength. He doesn't have much physical projection remaining at 5-foot-9, but his present package of tools is enticing and he plays with a lot of energy.

Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School in Florida has produced seven players who have at least touched the big leagues, with Red Sox phenom Roman Anthony the most recent. That list also includes slugger Anthony Rizzo and lefty Jesús Luzardo. Now there’s another elite-level southpaw coming from the program in the form of Rojas, who performed well at a host of summer showcase events like the East Coast Professional Showcase and MLB’s High School All-American Game, not to mention tossing 11 shutout innings for Team USA en route to a gold medal at the WBSC U-18 World Cup. Rojas has positioned himself as one of the top lefties, and best arms overall, available in the class, high school or college. He can crank his fastball up to 98 mph with outstanding riding life, commanding it to both sides of the plate. It’s a better pitch to his arm side, but can get it glove side when he needs to, missing bats when it’s up in the zone. He complements it with a very nasty three-quarters low-80s sweeping slider that is a true out pitch. He doesn’t use his changeup much, but there’s plenty of feel for it to imagine it being a third effective offering.Athletic with outstanding presence on the mound, Rojas can fill up the strike zone, though he occasionally can try to get downhill too quickly and open up his delivery too early, causing a little more scattered command, things that are easily correctable. The Miami recruit has all the makings of a frontline starter and is in line to be Stoneman Douglas’ first first-round pick.

Overshadowed by first-round picks Arjun Nimmala, George Lombard Jr. and Aidan Miller in Florida's high school shortstop class of 2023, Lebron was on scouting radars but went undrafted because he wouldn't have gone high enough to lure him away from an Alabama commitment. By early 2025 he had become the frontrunner to go No. 1 overall in the 2026 Draft, though he was passed by UCLA's Roch Cholowsky later that spring. He still has a chance of becoming the highest pick in Crimson Tide history, passing Joe Vitello (No. 7 in 1991).The only knock on Lebron is a tendency to chase pitches out of the zone, especially breaking balls. But he's also making strides with his approach and two-strike plan and could develop into an average hitter, which would make him an All-Star. He has added 15 pounds and significant bat speed in college, giving him well-above-average raw power from the right side of the plate, and he also has good feel for driving balls in the air.All the rest of Lebron's tools grade as plus, as does his makeup, and some evaluators think he's even better than that as a defender despite an uncharacteristically erratic spring. He has a quick first step that allows him to steal bases and cover plenty of ground to both sides at shortstop. He can make any throw needed from anywhere at short and would be an asset anywhere on the diamond.

Derek Hacopian won the Atlantic Coast Conference triple crown and Player of the Year Award at Maryland in 1992, and his sons Chris and Eddie teamed up with the Terrapins in 2024-25. After Chris slashed .375/.502/.656 with 14 homers and more than twice as many walks (40) as strikeouts (19), and his older brother graduated last spring, he transferred to Texas A&M. His hitting ability and strike-zone judgment rank among the best in the 2026 Draft, though he hasn’t been at his best while dealing with lower-back issues that cost him nearly a month this spring.Hacopian can make hitting look easy because he has advanced feel for the barrel and control of the strike zone. Though he has a busy setup, his right-handed stroke always seems to be on time and produces consistent hard contact. He almost never misses fastballs, hitting .423 with a five percent whiff rate against heaters as a sophomore. While his hitting prowess overshadows his power, the latter is at least average and could be more if he drove balls in the air more often.Hacopian played third base as a freshman before shifting to shortstop as a sophomore, and the Aggies originally committed to playing him there in 2026. He's a below-average runner with average arm strength and a lack of lateral range, so he wound up at second base. It's not a given that he can stay on the dirt at the next level, so he could wind up in left field.

Egg Harbor Township is a small town of less than 50,000 people not far from Atlantic City. Not surprisingly, it’s never had a player drafted from its high school -- that might change this July. Flukey, a long and lanky right-hander, pitched at Perfect Game National and threw well at the Area Code Games last summer. He came out of the gates hot once his season got started, not giving up a run over his first four starts in April, and his name has steadily climbed up Draft boards.Flukey stands at 6-foot-6 with long levers and a body that’s easy to see standing atop a big league mound. While he was up to about 93 mph at the Area Code Games, he’s been cranking it up to 96 mph this spring with some life up in the zone after adding around 20 pounds to a frame that could carry more weight, which could mean more consistent velocity in the future. He’s throwing his curve a bit harder now too, in the 80-82 mph range, and it could be an average pitch, but it also can get slurvy. There’s feel for a changeup, but it’s a bit of a firm 84-85 mph offering.While Flukey throws strikes with an over-the-top delivery, some see a little reliever risk because of the lack of present secondary stuff. But most give him a very good chance to start, given his frame and the upward trajectory of his stuff. That’s why the Coastal Carolina recruit’s name has floated at least into top-five-rounds conversations.

Scouting grades: Hit: 50 | Power: 40 | Run: 50 | Arm: 50 | Field: 55 | Overall: 45Orange Lutheran High School has produced three first-rounders over the years, starting with Gerrit Cole -- a first-rounder in 2008 who went on to UCLA and became the No. 1 overall pick in 2011 -- along with right-hander Cole Winn in 2018 and Mikey Romero in 2022. (Brewers outfielder Garrett Mitchell was a 14th-round pick in high school, then followed Cole’s path and was a Bruins first-rounder in 2020.) Curiel entered the summer showcase circuit looking like he might continue that line, and while he showed off some skills and played for Team USA, scouts didn’t think he had the kind of summer performance they expected from him, and he continued to get mixed results from evaluators this spring before removing his name from the Draft so he can head to Louisiana State.Tools-wise, Curiel has a package that reminds some of a Pete Crow-Armstrong or a Michael A. Taylor. A left-handed hitter, Curiel’s bat-to-ball skills are excellent, and he rarely strikes out. But while there is some gap power in there, it’s been softer contact than some hoped to see at this point. He hasn’t gotten much stronger or grown from when he first arrived on the high school scene and he’s yet to show he can add strength to his narrow frame or have impact at the plate.While Curiel is now mostly an average runner, he does have outstanding instincts that will let him play center field for a long time. That with his knack for finding the barrel gives him a pretty decent floor as a big leaguer, and there are some who think he might come out of LSU as a high floor college hitter. There might have been some prospect fatigue here as Curiel has been “famous” for a while, with the industry reminding itself that it took a player like Taylor a long time to fully develop.

Over the summer, Helfrick put his name on the map as an intriguing high school catching prospect, particularly after he won MVP honors at the Perfect Game All-American Classic at Chase Field in Phoenix. But while he has all the raw tools you want from the position, he’s struggled to replicate that performance this spring, leading some to think he’s likely headed to college.A right-handed hitter, Helfrick does have tremendous bat speed that can lead to impacting the baseball, like he did with his triple in the All-American Classic. But his swing gets disconnected and he doesn’t sync up his lower half. It’s power-over-hit now and he hasn’t shown good plate coverage, especially this spring, with a bit of a one-dimensional approach.Helfrick does run well, especially for a catcher, and that athleticism should help him become a better receiver. He has plenty of raw arm strength, but he needs to clean up his throwing mechanics to improve his accuracy to second base, something that should come with more instruction. It is an intriguing set of tools at a premium position that lacks depth in this Draft class, but it might be tough for teams to sign him away from his commitment to Arkansas.

Calvary Christian High School in Florida has a stacked pitching staff that has three Top 150 prospects. Peterson, a tall and projectable right-hander who offers some good now stuff with more in the tank to come, led that group heading into the spring after those ingredients were on display at summer showcase events like the PDP League, East Coast Pro Showcase, MLB’s High School All-American Game and Perfect Game’s All-American Classic, but a lack of consistency this spring has impacted his stock.It’s still easy to dream on what the 6-foot-5 Peterson can become when he’s fully matured and fills out that frame. His fastball still excites scouts, throwing it up to 96 mph with more consistent velocity to come. Over the summer, his 78-82 mph slider showed good lateral shape and break, featuring good depth when he stays on top of it. But he’s largely scrapped it this spring, throwing his softer 12-to-6 curve, but not consistently. He has feel for a changeup, thrown in the low 80s, but he doesn’t use it much.Like with many young high school arms, Peterson is more control than command, and his delivery can get out of sync at times. Added strength should allow him to repeat his delivery so he should be a solid strike-thrower in the future. The upside as a starting pitcher could still have the Florida recruit in top three round conversations.

One of the three big starters at Calvary Christian High School in Florida, Dietz looked like he might be the No. 2 guy on that stacked prep rotation. A big lefty, Dietz has transformed his body, and his effectiveness on the mound had improved as a result. After a solid tour of summer showcase events, he hasn’t taken the step forward as one of the best high school southpaws in the class many hoped to see this spring.Dietz still offers an intriguing combination of size at 6-foot-6, stuff and feel for pitching. The lefty throws his fastball typically in the low-90s and there’s hope that continued improvement with his condition that led to a better delivery over the summer will create more consistent velocity in the future, though scouts noted his arm speed hasn’t been as impressive this spring as it was last summer. His hard and short low-80s slider misses bats and could be an out pitch in the future. He has some feel for a changeup -- although he hasn’t used it much, there’s belief it will develop into at least an average offering.Despite his size, Dietz does an excellent job repeating his delivery and arm slot, showing an ability to use both sides of the plate and throw all of his pitches for strikes. The Arkansas recruit reminds some of a high school version of Oklahoma’s College World Series standout Jake Bennett, who was a second rounder in the 2022 Draft.

One of the top all-around athletes in the high school crop, Strosnider finished second at the 2023 Texas 3-A state track and field championships with a high jump of 6 feet, 7 inches and also earned all-region accolades in basketball as a junior. He also offers an intriguing combination of power and speed on the diamond, and he wowed observers with a batting-practice display at the Area Code Games last August that shattered a window of a dorm beyond the right-field fence. He could go as early as the third round to a team that believes enough in his hitting ability.Strosnider's most attractive attribute is his plus raw power, which he generates with a quick left-handed stroke, strength and a heavy emphasis on launching balls to his pull side. His aggressive approach and tendency to chase breaking balls out of the zone create serious swing-and-miss concerns, however. He'll need to make adjustments to make more contact and fully tap into his pop against more advanced pitching.While there are questions about Strosnider's bat, the rest of his tools are solid or better. He has the quickness to steal bases and possibly stay in center field, and he also has the arm strength to fire low-90s fastballs as a left-handed pitcher and handle right field if he has to shift to a corner. He turns 19 three weeks before the Draft, which takes him out of the mix for some age-focused teams and will make him Draft-eligible as a sophomore in 2026 if he attends Texas Christian.

A solid player during his New Jersey high school days, Gracia got off to a very good start at Duke, earning freshman All-American honors by finishing with a 1.000 OPS in 2024. Gracia got off to a very rough start in his sophomore year as he tried to incorporate a swing change, but he got hot once he abandoned that switch, allowing him to finish with 15 homers and an OPS of 1.007. He followed coach Chris Pollard to the University of Virginia and missed the fall due to an undisclosed illness but returned to have a productive junior season with the Cavaliers.When he's locked in, Gracia looks like one of the better college bats in the class. Swinging from the left side of the plate, he makes excellent swing decisions and shows off plus contact skills. His advanced approach led to a Blue Devils-record 57 walks as a sophomore, and he really limits his swing-and-miss. That gives more confidence that he should continue getting to his solid raw power at the next level, and there's definitely the chance he can keep adding strength to his 6-foot-3 frame.Though he's a fringy runner, scouts give Gracia a chance to stick in center field because of his excellent instincts and efficient routes. If a move to a corner is necessary, left would be a more likely landing spot because his arm is a little short for the prototypical right fielder. His continued consistent production in a year light on college bats hasn't hurt his stock.

A year ago, Trent Gringlinger was a well-regarded prep catcher out of Huntington Beach HS in California, one who went on to play college ball at Tennessee. Scouts in southern California were abuzz when Trent's younger brother, Jared, reclassified in February from 2027 to be a part of this year's Draft class. The 17-year-old is legitimate two-way player for the Oilers, and evaluators seem to prefer his left-handed ability on the mound.One of the youngest players in the class -- he just turned 17 in April -- Grindlinger is all about projection on the mound. His present stuff doesn't jump off the page, but it's very easy to dream on a lot more to come as he fills out his 6-foot-3 frame. His fastball has been clocked up to 95 mph at times -- touching 96 mph last summer -- but he typically sits around 90-93 mph, with good arm speed and strength that some scouts think points to a future plus heater. He's been using an 80-82 mph kick changeup lately, though some favored his feel for a traditional circle change he'd used previously, and he'll occasionally try to throw two brands of slider, with the short, bullet one better than the sweepier version. He tends to be around the plate with his stuff.As a hitter, Grindlinger has a contact-oriented swing, albeit without much impact now, though some feel there is power to come, which would help him fit a likely corner outfield or first base profile better. His name was rushing up Draft boards, but if somehow things didn't work out, he could get the chance to keep playing both ways with his brother at Tennessee next year.

There have been only two prep left-handed pitchers from Massachusetts to sign out of high school and make it to the big leagues: Bill Travers back in 1970, Hall of Famer Tom Glavine in 1984, while current Marlins prospect Thomas White went in 2023. Bumila hopes to join that select group as he was racing up Draft boards this spring. The 6-foot-9 lefty, who helped lead his Bishop Feehan basketball team to a state championship this winter, has the chance to be just the fourth prep arm from the Bay State to go in the top 40 picks.All discussions about Bumila start with his elite fastball. Not only can he crank it up to over 100 mph, but Bumila’s extension and low release height are outliers, creating 19-20 inches of inverted vertical break. In the Massachusetts high school ranks, he doesn’t need much more, though he does have a decent slider and changeup. He can vary the shape of his 88-89 mph breaker, and it should translate to pro ball, while he sells his upper-80s cambio with good arm speed, a pitch with some armside fade to it.Bumila’s already-premium stuff could take another leap forward when he gets pro instruction; he likes digging into metrics and pitch design, so access to professional technology should help. He throws strikes, though he’s definitely control over command at this point. Because of his overall athleticism, the Texas recruit does a nice job of keeping all of his large moving parts fairly coordinated in repeating his delivery. He did sprain his UCL in 2025 but returned this spring with no ill effects after having internal brace surgery last May.

For the second time in five years, Lincoln-Way East HS (Frankfort, Ill.) has a glove-first shortstop who's attracting scouts. Bell is more highly regarded at the same stage than Ryan Ritter, who turned down the Cubs as a 33rd-rounder in 2019 and emerged as a fourth-rounder out of Kentucky three years later. Bell hasn't performed well offensively as a senior, but his defensive prowess still could land him in the second or third round.One of the top shortstops in the 2024 high school class, Bell plays quicker than his average speed and covers ground to both sides. He's a smooth defender who can execute throws from any angle with solid arm strength. He also has a good internal clock and the athleticism to play all over the diamond if he becomes more of a utilityman than an everyday player.A switch-hitter, Bell has impressive bat speed and could develop 20-homer power if he makes projectable strength gains and improvements at the plate. But he didn't hit on the showcase circuit and has been too passive this spring, taking too many hittable pitches and not doing much damage. He'll turn 19 before the Draft, which will rule him out for some clubs, and he'll be a sophomore-eligible in 2026 if he attends Kentucky.

Reese began his college career at Houston in 2024 before transferring to Mississippi State and earning Southeastern Conference newcomer of the year honors last spring. He batted .352/.422/.718 with 21 homers while dealing with a loose bone fragment in his left big toe and has produced similar numbers as a junior. After ranking second in the SEC with 12 homers during league play, he should go somewhere in the back half of the first round.Reese's carrying tool is his plus power, which he generates with the strength in his 6-foot-4, 220-pound frame and the bat speed and leverage in his left-handed stroke. He can homer to any part of a ballpark and pounds both lefties and righties. He can get a bit aggressive at times but is making progress with his swing decisions and some evaluators think he could become a solid hitter with 25-30 homers per year.The biggest question with Reese is whether he can handle third base. He's a below-average athlete with limited quickness and range, and his arm is merely average. He has improved since fielding .889 at the hot corner as a sophomore but still could wind up at first base or possibly left field.

Etowah HS (Woodstock, Ga.) features three seniors drawing attention from scouts in Condon, right-hander Matthew Sharman and outfielder Deion Cole, who helped the Eagles win the state 6-A championship in 2024. Condon is the star attraction, drawing comparisons to Lenny Dykstra, Brett Gardner and a stronger version of Sal Frelick. He may have the best combination of hitting ability and speed in this high school crop.Though Condon has an unconventional left-handed stroke with an upright barrel, he makes good swing decisions and regular contact to all fields. He has enough bat speed and strength to get to average power, though it shows up more in batting practice than in games at this point. He hits balls hard but has yet to pull them in the air consistently.Condon has double-plus speed and likes to use it, playing with constant intensity as he tries to make things happen on the bases and runs down balls in the gaps. He's a quality center fielder who will stay there at the next level, with the added bonus of solid arm strength. A Tennessee recruit, he's reminiscent of former Volunteers star Drew Gilbert with his 5-foot-11 frame, tools and flair.

Bolemon didn't allow an earned run as junior while striking out 101 in 42 1/3 innings, winning Gatorade's South Carolina prep Player of the Year Award and leading Southside Christian HS (Simpsonville) to its fourth consecutive state 3-A title with a 19-strikeout no-hitter in the championship. Then he dominated on the showcase circuit as well and was the ace of the U.S. National Team that won the 18-and-under World Cup in Japan in September, fanning 17 in 11 scoreless frames. He entered 2026 as the consensus best pitching prospect in the Draft -- college or high school -- but hasn't quite lived up to that billing.Bolemon's stuff is beyond reproach, starting with a 91-94 mph fastball that reaches 96, and he can throw it to either side of the plate with carry or downhill plane. He has a pair of plus breaking balls, with evaluators slightly preferring his 78-82 mph downer curveball to his low-80s sweeper. He also does a nice job of killing spin on a mid-80s kick changeup that could become a solid offering with more use.There's much more to Bolemon than just pitch quality. The Wake Forest recruit has a projectable, athletic frame and a clean delivery that allows him to pound the zone with his main three offerings. Scouts love his makeup and mound presence as well. The only blemishes on his résumé are a partial elbow tear in eighth grade that required internal brace surgery and the fact that he'll turn 19 three months before the Draft, but neither is a concern.

The best high school players coming out of Pennsylvania the last few years have been hitters, from first rounder Cole Young from the Pittsburgh area and Benny Montgomery outside of Harrisburg. This year’s class is headlined by arms, with lefty David Shields representing the west side of the state and Kuhns, one of the more intriguing projectable right-handers in the class further east, putting his name on the map after a strong summer that included pitching for Team USA in international competition, though he was trending a bit in the wrong direction as the spring wore on.Kuhns is a wiry, lean and twitchy athlete on the mound with a ton of ceiling. At 6-foot-3 and 168 pounds, he’s the prototype for projection and he already has good stuff. His quick arm fires both a two- and four-seam fastball, throwing the former in the 89-91 mph range with plenty of run and sink and a 93-95 mph on the latter with ride up in the zone at his best. He has good feel to spin his breaking ball and though it can sometimes get slurvy, it’s easy to see a future plus slider in there. His splitter has good tumble to it as well.With three legitimate offerings, more to come in the tank and an athletic and repeatable delivery that allows him to fill up the strike zone, Kuhns has a lot of future starting pitcher traits. He’s committed to Tennessee, but even though his spring has been uneven, his upside could land him in the first couple of rounds come Draft time.

A product of Arizona high school powerhouse Corona Del Sol, Carlon stayed home to go to college, jumping right into the Arizona State bullpen as a freshman. He took a big step forward results-wise as a sophomore, also as a reliever, before pitching well in brief stops in the Cape Cod League and USA Baseball's Collegiate National Team Training Camp. A move into the Sun Devils' rotation this spring has helped cement him as top-two-round arm talent, even if the jury is still out about his long-term role.Considered to be the top college arm in the area, Carlon could have the chance to start thanks to a potential four-pitch mix coming from his strong 6-foot-5 frame. The lefty can run his fastball up to 98 mph, sitting around 94 mph, though it can get flat with scattered command. He's able to get back in line with his breaking stuff, starting with an 87-88 mph cutter-like short slider he throws as often as his heater that misses a ton of bats. He can manipulate it and make it a bit longer at times, and he also has an average 12-to-6 curve that offers a different look. His changeup is his fourth pitch, but it does have some fade to it.A solid athlete who hit .349 playing both ways in the Alaska Summer League in 2024, Carlon has struggled at times with finding the zone consistently, though he cut his walk rate down as a starter this year. There's some reliever risk, and he could move quickly in that role, but a team taking him in the top two rounds should give him every chance to stick in a rotation.

When New Jersey high school infielder Nick Becker was being evaluated for the 2025 Draft, there was some concern about signability because of his potential desire to play with his older brother, Eric, at the University of Virginia. Nick ended up signing with the Mariners as a second-rounder last July, and in 2026, it will be Eric's turn. And after two strong years with the Cavaliers in the ACC, he has a good chance to come off the board earlier than his brother did.A left-handed hitter, Becker has been a starter since he set foot on campus, and he's hit from day one, carrying a .366 average and an OPS north of 1.000. He's hit over power as of now, with a solid approach, excellent bat-to-ball skills and limited chase. There could be more impact in there, which could raise his profile, though right now he's content to hunt for base hits with a simple swing.A quiet leader with a slow internal clock, Becker is an average runner with an average arm. A team taking him might at least send him out as a shortstop and see if his excellent instincts allow him to stick there. A move to second or third might be in his future -- he manned the hot corner for Team USA over the summer -- and if he starts driving the ball more, he could profile well anywhere.

Townsend has been on the radar for a while now, having pitched for USA Baseball’s 18U World Cup Qualifying team back in November 2022 and showing off good stuff last summer at events like the Area Code Games. He continued to perform well, especially down the strength at the end of his spring season in California, giving him some helium as the Draft approached.The 6-foot-1 Townsend has an intriguing combination of stuff and projection. He’ll currently throw his fastball in the 90-94 mph range with decent life, but his best pitch currently is a knuckle-curve that pairs nicely with the heater. He throws it in the 79-80 mph range and it flashes plus, with good, late dive to it. That two-pitch combination is his foundation, but he does show a separate slider at times, along with some feel for a changeup.While Townsend’s repertoire is solid, he needs to refine his touch and feel for pitching overall. As good as that curve can be, he does have trouble landing it for strikes at times, and like with many prep pitchers, he’ll need to work on his overall command. He gets high marks for his willingness to learn and his makeup and there’s enough ceiling here for teams to consider taking the right-hander early enough to sign him away from his Ole Miss commitment and make him the first Santa Margarita High School product to sign since Cleveland took Milan Tolentino in the fourth round of the 2020 Draft.

Reddemann spent two years at the University of San Diego developing a reputation as a big-time strike-thrower with a real feel for pitching. A move up to UCLA for his junior year has helped him reach another gear completely. Establishing himself as the Friday night ace on the best team in the country, not to mention with top Draft prospect Roch Cholowsky, has allowed the right-hander to be seen by a lot of the scouting industry, where his improved stuff has helped catapult him to early-round status. His momentum was delayed with news of him missing time due to arm fatigue breaking at the end of April.A fall and offseason with the Bruins’ staff has helped Reddemann add strength and velocity, while all of his repertoire has improved. His fastball now sits around 95-96 mph, and he’s touched as high as 99 mph, getting plenty of swings and misses with the pitch. In the past, his best secondary offering had been his changeup, and the 84-85 mph pitch still has the chance to be a plus offering in the future. But his go-to this year has been a hard, 87-91 mph cutter, a pitch he’s shown he can throw to both sides of the plate against both lefties and righties, with an ability to bury it on the corners. He also unveiled a newer, sweepier low-80s slider, and he’ll use an 80-82 mph curve to get ahead early in counts. Even with the uptick in velocity, Reddemann has continued to fill up the strike zone with all of his offerings, and deception in his delivery helps all of it play up. He’s super-competitive on the mound, and scouts who saw him in San Diego have been impressed in the way he’s changed his breaking ball profile and stepped up his game in his Draft year.

Both of Sorrell's grandfathers played in the big leagues: Billy Sorrell, an outfielder who shared a 1966 Topps rookie card with Ferguson Jenkins, and Tom Griffin, who led the Majors in strikeouts per nine innings (9.6) as an Astros rookie in 1969. After failing to make much of an impression on the showcase circuit last summer, he put himself on scouting radars by homering three times and winning MVP honors at the Five Tool High School Festival in March. His all-around tools have made him a possible top-three-rounds pick.Sorrell packs a lot of strength and leverage in his 6-foot-3, 195-pound frame, and he looks to launch balls from the left side of the plate. His stroke can get uphill and long, so he does come with some swing-and-miss concerns, but he also has a knack for barreling balls. He produces high exit velocities yet still needs to prove he can produce against consistently good pitching. Very athletic for his size, Sorrell has plus speed out of the batter's box and can make an impact on the bases and in the outfield. A Texas A&M recruit, he shows fine instincts in center field and has a good chance to stick up the middle. If he has to move, his plus arm will fit nicely in right field.

Robbins' long track record of performance could land him in the late first round. A year ago, he led the Big East Conference in batting (.422) and on-base percentage (.547) at Seton Hall before topping the Cape Cod League in hitting (.307), slugging (.545) and OPS (.936). He has wielded one of the Southeastern Conference's most dangerous bats since transferring to Texas, more than tripling his previous career high with 19 homers entering NCAA tournament play.Becoming more aggressive has allowed Robbins to tap into increased power, as he's chasing pitches more often but also driving them in the air much more frequently and hitting them harder and farther than ever before. His quick right-handed swing allows him to catch up to and destroy fastballs, though his new mindset has left him vulnerable to breaking balls and changeups. He holds his hands and back elbow high at the plate and balances briefly on his back leg before unleashing an uppercut stroke, a nontraditional approach that has resulted in his strikeout rate jumping from 13 percent as a sophomore to 32 percent in the Cape League and 21 percent with the Longhorns.While Robbins produces lackluster run times out of the batter's box, he's an average runner with a knack for stealing and taking extra bases. A corner outfielder at Seton Hall and on the Cape, he has played a decent center field for Texas. He'll get a chance to man center in pro ball but likely will wind up back in left or right, and his average arm strength works at all three spots.

A veteran of Major League Baseball’s Dream Series, Rose started making a bigger name for himself on the Draft scene with solid performances at summer showcase events like the PDP League. The Chicago native moved to Florida to attend IMG Academy for his senior year to get a bigger challenge and more exposure, and he continued to show off some exciting tools, even if evaluators aren’t exactly sure where he’ll play at the next level.Rose has the chance to be an impact hitter in the future. He has excellent bat speed and plus raw power, especially to his pull side, though he doesn’t have the most natural swing -- a mechanical tweak that can be made at the next level. He has plus straight run speed and projects to be at least an above-average runner on the basepaths.Some of that projection is impacted by where he plays long-term defensively. Rose is agile behind the plate and some believe he could develop into a solid backstop, something he’s eager to prove, though he would invariably slow down. Others feel the Louisville recruit has too far to go as a catcher and his speed/athleticism would look better in center field. Wherever he plays, his leadership skills and makeup will be huge plusses for any organization that drafts him.

The cousin of Brewers outfielder Garrett Mitchell, Grahovac was a mainstay on the showcase circuit last summer and a member of USA Baseball’s gold medal-winning 18U team. A standout at Villa Park High School in California, a school that has produced big league talent like Aaron Boone and Mark Trumbo, Grahovac has continued to show some offensive potential this spring, albeit with continuing concerns about his future profile.Grahovac shows off a lot of strength from the right side of the plate, with plenty of present power that showed up at times over the summer and this spring. But it’s strength over bat speed, and there’s continued to be too much swing-and-miss, with scouts worrying about his hit tool. His approach and bat path are not conducive to high contact rates.There is also uncertainty over where Grahovac will play defensively. He’s a decent athlete who plays shortstop in high school, but the Texas A&M recruit won’t play there at the next level. Most see a move to third or left field as the best option, and he’s told teams he’s willing to catch. While he hasn’t seen much time back there, it could be the spot where he profiles best.

Prosek isn't as tooled up as fellow Mississippi prep prospects Eric Booth and Kevin Roberts Jr., but he's a better hitter than either of them and one of the most advanced bats in the entire high school class. Part of a Magnolia Heights (Senatobia, Miss.) program that has won seven consecutive Midsouth Association of Independent Schools 5-A championships, he homered twice in the 2025 title game. He also played well on the showcase circuit, winning MVP honors at the Perfect Game All-American Classic in July.The nephew of Brewers third-base coach Matt Erickson, Prosek has a quick, compact left-handed swing that always seems to be on time. He can chase pitches and get pull-happy on occasion, but his good feel for the barrel allows him to make quality contact. He has the hitting ability, bat speed and strength for average power, and some evaluators believe he can become a plus hitter with solid pop.Because he's a below-average runner and lacks lateral quickness, Prosek fits better at third base than in the middle infield. He has seen some action at catcher this spring, playing well enough that scouts believe it's a path that may be worth exploring in pro ball -- though it would add to his development time and take a toll on his offensive production. He has solid arm strength and has pushed his fastball to 93 mph on the mound. He'll be 19 on Draft day and eligible again as a sophomore in 2028 if he attends Mississippi.

Edwards is a Los Angeles area native who attended Palisades High School, earning back-to-back league pitcher of the year honors as well as consecutive first-team all-city nods in 2022 and 2023. After heading just a stone’s throw away to Southern Cal for college, the left-hander served as a swingman type for the Trojans, amassing just over 70 innings combined. A strong fall showing has been followed up by a solid spring leading the weekend rotation, which has kept the scouting industry’s interest piqued.In many ways, Edwards fits the prototype of a college lefty with a feel for pitching. He has a solid three-pitch mix and knows how to use it effectively. While his fastball typically sits in the 91-93 mph range, he can touch 95. There is some carry and “darkness” to his heater, leading to a 30-percent miss rate on the pitch in 2025, according to Synergy, a miss rate he maintained this spring. He’ll throw his slurvy breaking ball around 80 mph, and the curve flashes as a plus pitch with excellent spin. He has good feel for an above-average changeup with good fade that also can miss bats.Teams will like that Edwards will just be turning 21 immediately after the Draft. The fact that his strike-throwing has improved since he got to campus won’t hurt, either, as he showed excellent command during his work this fall. He has all the ingredients needed to be a solid starting pitcher and his ability to maintain his stuff this spring has floated him up into top-two-round consideration.

Borthwick entered the summer showcase circuit as a very large -- he’s 6-foot-6 and listed at 255 pounds -- and intriguing two-way high school player from the Florida panhandle. He showed off skills at the plate, hitting .300 for Team USA at the WSBC U-18 World Cup in Japan, but he really took off with his work on the mound. After pitching well at events like the East Coast Pro Showcase and tossing 10 shutout innings for Team USA, earning tournament MVP honors, it’s clear that his future as a pro will be as a right-handed hurler.Borthwick has the size, pitch mix and makeup to be a future workhorse starter near the top of a big league rotation one day. While he’ll sit 93-95 mph mostly with his fastball, he’s shown the ability to get the heater up to 97-98 mph with good life up in the zone and to both sides of the plate. His best secondary offering is a hard 84-85 mph slider that can flash plus and miss a ton of bats. He has a really good feel for spin, though at times he can try to do too much. There’s some feel for a changeup with some fading action, but he doesn’t use it much.While Borthwick can get a little east-west with his delivery, something very correctable, he shows the ability to pitch to all four quadrants effectively. He’s a power-hitting corner infielder who could get the chance to do both should he go on to Auburn, but he profiles as the kind of high-ceiling arm that goes early enough to forego heading to campus.

In 2024, The Stony Brook School from Long Island, New York, won the 2024 NYSAIS State Championship. In 2025, the school held its own by going 2-2 at USA Baseball's National High School Invitational. At the center of it all for the Bears was Ruiz, their shortstop, who then went on to perform at summer events like the East Coast Professional Showcase and Area Code Games. He also manned shortstop for Team USA, moving fellow Draft prospect Grady Emerson to third, and was named to the All-World team as the U.S. brought home gold in the WBSC U-18 World Cup.An undersized switch-hitter who is listed at 5-foot-10 and 168 pounds, Ruiz is arguably the best defensive shortstop in the class. He's an elite defender with a plus arm and range, great hands and outstanding footwork. Unlike with many young players at the premium position, Ruiz not only makes the spectacular, acrobatic plays, throwing on the run, he also slows the game down and makes the routine plays as well. It's future Gold Glove-caliber defense. An above-average runner whose instincts help him be a successful base-stealer, Ruiz's bat is behind his glove. He makes a ton of contact with an idea of what he's doing at the plate, excellent bat control and no fear, showing an ability to turn around premium velocity. But he's done it without too much damage or impact to date, and showing some ability to drive the ball could certainly help the Vanderbilt recruit's Draft stock.

The son of Hall of Famer Jim Thome, who's also an assistant coach for his Nazareth Academy (La Grange Park, Ill.) team, Landon is one of the best all-around hitters in the 2026 high school crop. Some scouts were more impressed with him last spring than his teammate Jaden Fauske, who signed with the White Sox for $2,997,500 as the top pick in the second round. Thome could go in the same range despite questions about his eventual defensive home.Thome turns his left-handed swing loose with maximum intent but has the pitch recognition and bat-to-ball skills to make a lot of hard contact. His stroke has loft geared to drive balls in the air and he has tapped into more power as he has improved his strength and bat speed. His pure hitting ability may stand out a little more than his pop, but both grade as at least solid.Thome is a below-average runner who could lose a step as he continues to fill out. He's a reliable defender with sure hands at shortstop, but his lack of quickness and fringy to average arm won't allow him to stay there at his next step, whether that be college at Florida State or pro ball. After working with Jake Cronenworth during the winter, he's moving and throwing better, giving him a better chance of playing second or third base. Scouts praise his baseball IQ and makeup.

Lowrance's hitting ability has generated a lot of buzz this spring, to the extent that he may not last until the second round. He has a similar offensive profile and better secondary tools than fellow South Carolina prep product P.J. Morlando, a Marlins first-rounder two years ago. The top prospect in Virginia's recruiting class, he's unlikely to make it to Charlottesville.Lowrance has a smooth left-handed stroke and a track record of delivering against quality competition on the showcase circuit. He doesn't try to do too much at the plate, making consistent contact to all fields despite naturally long levers with his 6-foot-5 frame. While he has a projectable build, he doesn't have explosive bat speed and doesn't lift and pull many pitches at this point, so he'll need to add strength and make some adjustments to get to 20-homer power.Though Lowrance moves well for his size, his average speed and quickness will dictate a move to third base at the next level. His solid arm strength will work at the hot corner, though there's some question about his ability to stick there in the long term. If he outgrows third base and slows a bit, he could wind up at first base.

Not to be confused with the former Georgia safety with a similar name and build, Jackson spent his first college season at Wofford, winning Southern Conference freshman of the year honors and helping the Terriers earn their first-ever NCAA Tournament victory in 2024. After splitting time between the outfield, catcher and DH for the Bulldogs as a sophomore, he became a full-time backstop and has exploded this spring. The first catcher in Division I history to total 25 homers and 25 steals in the same season, he earned the Southeastern Conference player of the year and tournament MVP awards and led the league in all three triple crown categories entering NCAA tournament play.Jackson is extremely strong and hits the ball extremely hard from the right side of the plate, looking like a taller version of Tyler O'Neill. He has demonstrated a selective approach throughout his college career and is doing a much better job this year of making contact, both against velocity and against non-fastballs. There's still some swing-and-miss to his game but he's getting to his well-above-average power more consistently and crushing homers from foul pole to foul pole.More athletic than most catchers, Jackson can flash plus speed and regularly posts average run times. He's still a work in progress behind the plate with the potential to become a fringy or perhaps average defender once he polishes his receiving and blocking. He features solid raw arm strength but throws with effort and needs to improve his technique and accuracy. If he can't stay behind the plate, he could handle the outfield corners in pro ball.

Mendes came out of the very successful Jesuit High School program in Tampa and began his career at Mississippi. After a year pitching almost exclusively out of the bullpen, Mendes returned to his home state to join Florida State’s rotation. It was an up-and-down sophomore season, though he did pitch eight strong innings in a Regional win to help send the Seminoles to a Super Regional for the second straight year. He’s taken a very nice step forward as FSU’s Friday night starter in 2026, launching him into early-round consideration.An athletic 6-foot-1 left-hander, Mendes will use a robust five-pitch mix to keep hitters guessing. While his fastball sits around 92 mph and tops out at 96, it does feature some life upstairs with some angle to his glove side. His 80-mph changeup is his best secondary, a plus cambio he sells well with arm speed, the kind of offspeed offering that seems to pause in mid-air with late arm side depth to it. His low-80s slider can show some good short glove side tilt and he can back foot it to right-handed hitters and he’s added a new, harder cutter, with better shape and control of it as the season has gone on. He’ll use his upper-70s curve to steal a strike early in counts.Mendes has improved his delivery as well as his ability to control his emotions on the mound, all allowing him to minimize damage and fill up the strike zone a lot more consistently than in the past. He can run into trouble deeper in outings, but it’s not anything teams viewing him as a future big league starter to select in the top couple of rounds are overly concerned about.

Scouts appreciated Rembert's bat when he was a Florida high school senior in 2024 but weren't sure about the rest of his game and didn't try to deter him from attending Auburn. After earning consensus Freshman All-America honors and posting the best batting average (.278 in five games) on the U.S. collegiate national team last year, he's poised to become one of the first sophomore-eligibles selected in 2026. He has proven that he can handle second base at the next level and earns comparisons to Howie Kendrick and Christian Moore.Rembert has some of the best bat speed in college baseball and an advanced approach, though his swing and patience have regressed a bit this spring. He manages the strike zone very well and focuses on drilling line drives from gap to gap. It's unclear if he'll provide more than 15 homers annually because he makes too much groundball contact, though he has the strength and bat speed to do more damage if he turns on more pitches and adds more loft to his compact right-handed stroke.Rembert is an average runner with arm strength to match, which limits his usefulness on the bases and in the field. Some scouts once wondered if he'd wind up in left field, but he's looking more comfortable at second base in college than he did at shortstop in high school. He has reliable hands and provides steady defense at the keystone.

Advincula established himself as one of college baseball's best contact hitters in two seasons at California, batting .334 with a 9 percent strikeout rate. He made an even bigger name for himself in the Cape Cod League, winning the batting title (.392) in 2024 and the top pro prospect award voted on by scouts last summer. Afterward, he transferred to Georgia Tech, where he ranks first in Division I in hits (103) and third in batting (.431) at the outset of NCAA tournament play and further helped his cause by hitting .477 in Atlantic Coast Conference games.Advincula is a master of reading defenses and using his short left-handed swing to slap balls through holes for hits. His bat-to-ball skills can work against him at times because he does chase pitches out of the zone, leading to suboptimal contact and cutting into his walk totals. He shows enough bat speed and strength to provide double-digit home run power, but he has gone deep just once in 205 at-bats in the wood-bat Cape League because he rarely turns on pitches or lifts them in the air.Though he can produce well-above-average home-to-first times as he dashes out of the batter's box, Advincula plays more as a plus runner and isn't a prolific basestealer. He has good infield actions and quickness but just fringy arm strength, making him best suited for second base, where he's a sure-handed defender. The Yellow Jackets tried him at shortstop in the fall, but his arm was stretched on the left side of the infield.

If teams like high school left-handed pitchers, this might be the Draft for them. Within the first round, they could have their choice of three from different areas of the country: Gio Rojas in South Florida, Carson Bolemon in South Carolina and Schmidt out west in California. Schmidt may have started the summer showcase circuit behind the other southpaws, but after performances at events like the Area Code Games and MLB’s All-American Game, he’s vaulted himself towards the top of the prep lefty list.An uptick in velocity this summer, particularly at the Area Code Games, certainly helped Schmidt’s stock rise. He was typically pitching at 93-94 mph with his fastball, but he was in the 94-97 mph range, and more of that consistently would certainly help him on Draft boards. There isn’t a ton of deception with the pitch right now, so it can get hit at times. He backs up the heater with a 78-81 mph slider that features three-quarters shape to it, a breaker that can disappear, miss a lot of bats and flashes plus. There’s feel for a changeup, too, which he sells well with arm speed out front.Schmidt has shown an ability to repeat his delivery well and fill up the strike zone with all three of his offerings, providing a very nice left-handed starting pitching kit. The LSU recruit reclassified to be done with high school early, so he’ll still be 17 at Draft time, something that will look good in a lot of team models.

The Blue Jays took a flier on Brunson in the 18th round of the 2023 Draft, but the California prep prospect couldn't be dissuaded from attending Texas Christian. He became an immediate starter for the Horned Frogs in center field, displaying average-to-solid tools across the board. His all-around ability should land him in the top two rounds, even if being a right-handed hitter without a true plus tool limits his ceiling.A steady if unspectacular hitter, Brunson reached base in his first 43 games in 2025, when he tripled his home run output from four as a freshman to 12 as a sophomore. He's an aggressive swinger but makes consistent contact and drills line drives from gap to gap. He translates the strength in his 6-foot-3, 200-pound frame into high-end exit velocities and 20-homer power that plays to all fields.Brunson has solid speed and will find spots to steal an occasional base. He doesn't have classic centerfield quickness but his instincts help him to track down balls in the gaps. If he can't remain up the middle in pro ball, his solid arm strength will fit nicely in right field.

Scouts rated Radel as South Dakota's top high school prospect in 2024, though he didn't draw enough interest to divert him from Notre Dame. He immediately became a weekend starter for the Fighting Irish and has improved steadily throughout his three years in South Bend. He has added a couple of ticks of velocity and picked up a cutter as a junior, allowing him to miss more bats than ever and giving him a chance to become Notre Dame's first pitching first-rounder since Aaron Heilman in 2001.Radel's riding fastball now sits at 93-95 mph and touches 98 while holding velocity deep into games. He's doing a better job of locating a mid-80s slider with depth that has become a solid offering, while his new upper-80s cutter elicits plenty of chases. He lands his 78-82 mph curveball for strikes and mixes in an upper-80s changeup with fade and sink against left-handers.Though Radel doesn't have a true plus offering and his pitch shapes are fairly ordinary, he succeeds because of his pitchability and competitiveness. Combining a high release point with good extension gives batters a different look and he pounds the strike zone. He's a high-floor starter who could fit into the middle of a rotation.

South Carolina features two high school position players who could factor in the top 50 selections, though scouts are split as to which is better. Some prefer Bo Lowrance's hitting ability, while other lean toward Marchand's superior all-around tools and defensive value. The latter helped James Island HS (Charleston) win the state 4-A title as a sophomore and the 5-A Division I championship as a junior, and he should become his school's first Draft pick since 1969 Seattle Pilots first-rounder Gorman Thomas.Thanks to Marchand's quick right-handed stroke and still-growing strength, balls jump off his bat when he times them right. He tends to lunge forward at the plate and hit off his front foot, though he manages to keeps his hands back and has a strong record of production on the showcase circuit. He could develop 25-homer power if he smooths out his swing and turns on more pitches.Marchand's hands work well in the field as well as at the plate, allowing him to get rid of balls quickly at shortstop. Clocked up to 92 mph on the mound, the Mississippi recruit has plus arm strength, good defensive instincts and a chance to remain at short. He has merely average speed and quickness, so he could end up at second or third base.

Hughes led Stetson in slugging (.515) as a freshman in 2024 before transferring to Texas Tech, where he batted .327/.411/.697 with nearly as many homers (19) as strikeouts (24) last spring. He continued to control the strike zone and hit the ball hard in the Cape Cod League, where he earned All-Star accolades and helped Bourne win its third title in four years. He's an offensive-minded left fielder who draws comparisons to Rusty Greer and Athletics 2025 second-round pick Devin Taylor.Hughes scores well analytically because he makes good swing decisions and a lot of contact against all types of pitches, producing a lot of hard line drives. He has a pretty left-handed swing that's compact and quick, and he possesses plus raw power. With his hitting ability, he should be able to make adjustments to allow him to launch balls to his pull side more frequently and get to 20-25 homers per season.He'll have to produce at the plate because Hughes doesn't offer much value elsewhere. He's a below-average runner with arm strength to match, which limits his defensive options. He can look shaky at times in left field, and at 5-foot-11, he lacks the ideal size for first base.

The nephew of long-time big leaguer Sean Rodriguez, Williamson has been a member of the starting outfield at Central Florida pretty much since the start of his freshman year in 2024. That first season was fairly nondescript, but an MVP turn in the wood bat Cal Ripken League that summer led to a big step forward as a sophomore with the Knights. He finished the 2025 season with an 1.110 OPS, 13 homers and 13 steals, putting him in position to join former UCF teammate Antonio Jimenez, a third-rounder of the Mets last year, as an early-round selection.Williamson is a strong and compact left-handed hitter who gives off some Alek Thomas or Nate McLouth type vibes. He has the chance to hit, with some power, showing a solid overall approach that led to small strikeout and high walk rates in '25. There’s a little hitch in his swing at the start, with a drop in his hands and a barrel tilt that can affect his timing, leading some to worry a little bit about his hit tool. But he has a quick swing with plenty of bat speed, and he’s shown he can get to his solid raw pop in games, particularly to his pull side.An above-average runner who didn’t get thrown out in 13 attempts to steal a year ago, Williamson played center as a freshman but moved to right field last year and is likely to stay in a corner in 2026. He has the arm to profile well there, but a team interested in taking him could very well give him the chance to play up the middle, which would raise his profile.

De La Salle High School in Northern California has produced just one big leaguer in left-hander Kyle Harrison and has never had a first-round pick come from its program. The latter will most certainly change in the form of Spangler, a big shortstop with impressive all-around tools that he showcased at events like MLB’s High School All-American Game and the Area Code Games after being named the Metro Player of the Year as a junior by the San Francisco Chronicle.Because of Spangler’s size – he’s at least 6-foot-3 – his instincts and throwback approach to the game, more veteran baseball people have mentioned he reminds them of a young Cal Ripken Jr. type and because he hits left-handed, there are the invariable Corey Seager comparisons. Spangler is more polish than flash with a simple, solid approach at the plate, finding the barrel consistently. While he can be susceptible at times for breaking stuff down, he has excellent zone awareness and shows a willingness to use the middle of the field or go the other way. He can get to his pull side power at times and more thump should come as he fills out his frame and if he starts committing to getting the ball in the air more. An average runner, Spangler’s instincts make him a good baserunner and also give him the chance to stick at shortstop. He has easy actions defensively, with good hands and while he lacks explosive range, he tends to always be in a good position to make all the plays. If he gets too big, third base is always an option for the Stanford recruit.

The Northern California high school scene hasn't produced a first-round arm since Matt Manning went No. 9 overall to the Tigers back in 2016. That dry stretch could end in 2026 with Hirschkorn. He didn't enter the summer that high on radars, but he certainly ended it firmly on the map, especially with outstanding performances at Perfect Game National and the Area Code Games, showing off a combination of size, athleticism and projection.Standing at 6-foot-7, Hirschkorn is an athletic right-hander with the chance to have a legitimate three-pitch mix. It's easy gas, up to 95-96 mph with very little effort, and it plays well at the top of the zone. There's a ton of room to get bigger and stronger, making it easy to dream of triple digits in the future. He backs it up with a very good low-80s slider that misses a ton of bats. There is some feel for a mid-80s changeup, but he hasn't thrown it a ton yet.Over the winter, scouts might check in to watch Hirschkorn's athleticism on the court during basketball season. That part of his game is apparent in his ability to repeat his delivery and fill up the zone, though some might quibble with a little high elbow. He's not done a lot of work in terms of pitch design, adding even more upside potential for the LSU recruit. He's reminded some of Noble Meyer, a 2023 high schooler from the Pacific Northwest who ended up going No. 10 overall in that Draft.

Christian Brothers HS (Memphis, Tenn.) has sent 11 players to the Majors, most notably All-Star catcher Tim McCarver. The Purple Wave now have the best prep catching prospect in the 2026 Draft after Brick reclassified from the 2027 class. He made that decision in October after starring for Team USA at the 18-and-under World Cup in Japan, batting .333 while providing quality defense behind the plate as the second-youngest player on the squad.Brick's best tool is his well-above-average arm strength, as he regularly records sub-1.9-second pop times and can throw out basestealers from his knees. He moves well behind the plate and shows the potential to become a solid receiver and blocker once he improves his one-knee technique. He didn't have any issues handling the premium stuff he saw with the U.S. 18-and-under team and scouts rave about his makeup.Brick's bat hasn't been as good as anticipated this spring. The Mississippi State recruit has a quick right-handed stroke but gets too passive at times and his long levers result in some in-zone swing-and-miss worries. He displays plus raw power in batting practice but focuses more on hitting line drives during game action.

Doug Waechter grew up in the Tampa-St. Pete area then went on to pitch for parts of six seasons in the big leagues, five of them with the hometown Rays after they took him in the third round of the 1999 Draft. His son, Kaden, has a chance to go earlier than that, a product of the same Jesuit High School in Tampa that produced future big league right-handers like Brad Radke and Lance McCullers Jr.Waechter offers an exciting combination of MLB family ties, projection in a 6-foot-3 frame, athleticism and feel for pitching. His fastball typically registers average velocities, sitting around 93 mph, though he can reach back for 95-96 mph, and there could be more in the tank to come. It plays up because it has good life to it, and he can command it to both sides of the plate. His top secondary offering right now is a slider that works best as an out pitch in the 84-86 mph range. He’ll crank it up a bit and turn the pitch into an upper-80s cutter, and he has some feel for a changeup, though he can drop his elbow on it at times.All of Waechter’s arsenal comes from an easy and repeatable delivery. He’s willing to throw all of his offerings at any point, pitching backwards when he needs to, filling up the zone consistently. Ceiling and pitchability don’t often come in one package, and that’s why scouts will be watching this Florida State recruit very carefully in the spring.

As a junior, Clark hit .411 hitting leadoff and playing shortstop for St. John Bosco high school in California, earning MVP honors in the ultra-competitive Trinity League. He continued to perform over the summer at events like the Area Code Games and also helped Team USA take home a Gold Medal at the WSBC U-18 World Cup in Japan. He didn’t play a ton over the fall, but that did little to disrupt his upward momentum, and a strong start to his spring didn't hurt, either. But some struggles as his senior season wore on impacted his stock a bit.Clark has an exciting combination of upside and instincts. He’s a left-handed hitter who consistently makes excellent swing decisions. He doesn’t chase and routinely squares up the baseball with plus bat speed. While he’s pure hit and approach over power right now, there is some conviction that his ability to drive the ball will continue to show up as he moves along in the game. He’s a plus runner, and that tool plays up even more because of his high baseball IQ.The only thing scouts aren’t sure about regarding Clark is his ability to stick at shortstop long term. Teams interested in him early will certainly give him the chance to play the premium position, where his instincts and accurate arm play well, but some think second or third base might be a better fit long-term. He’s now committed to Duke for college, where he would get the chance to keep playing with his twin brother, Miles.

After helping Liberty Christian Academy win a state title in Virginia as a junior in high school, Blair stayed put for college ball, immediately impacting the Liberty University pitching staff as a freshman reliever who earned Conference USA all-freshman team honors. He moved into the rotation as a sophomore in 2025 and was named to CUSA’s all-conference first team, and he’s upped his game even more in leading the Flames as their Friday night starter, vaulting him up Draft boards in the process.Blair offers an intriguing combination of funk, deception, stuff and command, with as many as five pitches to choose from. He’ll throw both a four-seam, with decent run to the glove side, and a two-seam with sink to the arm side, firing his fastball up to 97 mph. The sinker is a true bowling ball which was getting ground-ball outs around 50 percent of the time in 2026. He has a distinct slider, with bite, depth and occasional sweep, missing bats at around 83-84 mph, and more of an upper-80s cutter with short, late life. He doesn’t use his mid-80s changeup much, but some scouts think it could be solid average in time.All of Blair’s stuff plays up because of his unorthodox mechanics. His delivery features a low slot and cross-body arm action to go along with a leg kick. It looks like it should take a lot of effort, but he repeats it well and has become an extreme strike-thrower, creating a very uncomfortable at-bat. Not all teams will like the arm action, but his ability to take the ball every week this year with positive results should have him off the board in the top two rounds.

Brown comes from a Sulphur (La.) HS program that has sent four pitchers to the big leagues, headlined by John Thomson. A key starter for the U.S. national team that won the 18-and-under World Cup last September, he's one of the most polished high school arms in the Draft. But his stuff hasn't taken a step forward this spring, so it may be difficult for clubs to select Gatorade's state player of the year high enough to divert him from his Louisiana State commitment.  Brown's fastball typically ranges from 88-92 mph and peaks at 94, but it plays better than its velocity thanks to low release height and carry as well as his ability to command the heater. He can land his sweeping 78-82 mph slider for strikes or get hitters to chase it off the plate. He can fade or sink a changeup with similar velocity, and he has the faith to throw it in any count. Brown is also a quality athlete who doubles as a center fielder and one of the best prep hitters in Louisiana. He hides the ball well in his low-effort delivery and moves his pitches around the strike zone with ease. He might make a push for the first round with more velocity and there's definitely more projection remaining in his 6-foot-2 frame.

When scouts went to see 2024 Marlins second-rounder Carter Johnson at Oxford (Ala.) HS, they couldn't help but notice Maniscalco, then a freshman. He has reclassified into the 2026 Draft and will be one of the youngest players (17 years, two months) selected in years. He shares some similarities to Eli Willits, who reclassified into the 2025 Draft and went No. 1 overall, with Maniscalco possessing more power potential but less speed. The biggest difference is that Willits starred in his final high school season while Maniscalco looked overmatched at the plate during his before rallying some later in the spring.While Maniscalco had a reputation for having a very advanced approach for his age, he has looked too aggressive at times and too passive at others while struggling to make contact against Alabama high school pitching. Evaluators are willing to cut him some slack because of his youth, but they don't like the deep trigger in his swing. A switch-hitter, he has a markedly better stroke from the left side. Though there now are concerns about his hitting ability, most scouts believe he'll develop average or better power once he fills out his 6-foot-2 frame.Maniscalco is presently an average runner, with some scouts wondering if he might get a bit quicker as he adds strength and others speculating that he might lose a step. There's no question about the Mississippi State recruit's ability to remain at shortstop, where he has smooth actions and projects as a plus defender with arm strength to match. The son of Matt Maniscalco, who made it to Triple-A as a shortstop in the Rays system and runs his travel ball program, Rocco has developed a high baseball IQ while growing up around the game.

The son of former All-Star José Contreras, Joseph inherited his father's 6-foot-4 frame and aptitude for throwing a forkball. He helped Blessed Trinity HS (Roswell, Ga.) win the state 4-A title in 2025 while his velocity and prospect stock were making significant jumps. He could fit into the top two rounds as one of the higher-ceiling arms in the high school class, though teams will have to sign him away from a Vanderbilt commitment.After topping out at 91 mph in 2024, Contreras now works at 92-95 and peaks at 98 with his four-seam fastball, though it doesn't stand out in terms of life or shape. He did manage to jam Aaron Judge with a 94-mph heater and induce a broken-bat double play while pitching for Brazil during the World Baseball Classic. He still has room to pack plenty of muscle on his lanky frame, so he could add more power to his heater and his secondary offerings. His best pitch is a 77-81 mph forkball that dies at the plate, though both his 82-85 mph slider and 80-83 mph changeup with fade are solid weapons as well.Contreras has a clean arm action and delivers his pitches from a high three-quarters slot with short extension, creating some reliever risk. He throws his fastball for strikes and gets chases with the rest of his arsenal, though he still requires more polish. He needs to improve the quality and command with his heater and show that his forkball, slider and changeup will work against more discerning hitters.
