Only one player in Central Florida was named both an All-American and a Gold Glove winner by the American Baseball Coaches Association. That was Head, the top player in the Orlando area who helped lead his Windermere High School team to a regional championship its first appearance in the state Class 7A finals for the first time in school history. The Orange County male athlete of the year also caught the attention of scouts, who like his combination of tools and makeup.The first thing that might stand out with Head is his athletic 6-foot-3 frame. He’s a left-handed hitter and thrower with instincts on both sides of the ball. Right now, he’s more hit over power with a solid approach and a feel for making consistent contact. There is some question about how much power he’ll have, as he didn’t always show enough impact, but it’s not hard to dream on more pop to come as he physically matures. He’s an aggressive and savvy baserunner who can steal some bags.Right now, Head is athletic enough to play center field and was recognized for his glove work this spring. While his instincts should allow him to stay up the middle initially, there are some who feel he might continue to slow down and will have to shift to an outfield corner. That would put more pressure on that projectable power to show up on the regular. Teams considering him on Day 2 of the Draft will be buying the North Carolina State recruit’s upside, work ethic and overall baseball IQ.

The best Kansas prep position prospect since Bubba Starling went fifth overall to the Royals in 2011, Peterson won the High School Home Run Derby during All-Star Week and performed well at most showcase events last summer. He entered 2026 as one of the top power hitters in the high school class but isn't driving the ball with authority as much this spring. He would draw some interest as a pitching prospect if he didn't have so much offensive potential.Peterson is employing a selective approach, keeping his left-handed swing under control and utilizing the entire field. He still possesses plus raw power but is focusing on making line-drive contract. Once he learns to drive balls in the air more regularly, he could produce 20-25 homers per season.Peterson has slowed down as he has gotten stronger and is now a below-average runner. There are mixed opinions about his defense at third base, with some scouts believing he's fine there and others questioning his athleticism and envisioning him as more of a corner outfielder. His arm strength is beyond reproach because the Texas recruit has been clocked up to 94 mph on the mound, where he also has displayed an interesting low-80s slider.

Comeau climbed Draft boards with a strong finish to the showcase circuit, batting a combined .417 with four extra-base hits in 10 games between the Area Code Games and the World Wood Bat Association World Championship. In between, he participated in the Canadian Futures Showcase and finished second in the Home Run Derby at Rogers Centre. He won't turn 18 until a month after the 2026 Draft, adding to his appeal.Comeau is very advanced at the plate for his age, employing a quick and effortless left-handed stroke while making good swing decisions. He's underdeveloped physically at 6-foot-4 and 190 pounds, so he's only scratching the surface of his power potential. He already shows precocious feel for driving balls to the opposite field and could provide 20-25 homers on an annual basis once he adds needed strength and pulls more pitches.Because he's a below-average runner, Comeau has limited defensive options. The Texas A&M recruit won't stick at shortstop, with third base his best-case scenario and the outfield corners not much of an option unless he gets quicker as he gets stronger. He has been clocked up to 92 mph on the mound, so arm strength isn't an issue.

Bryant recorded one of the most dominant outings on the showcase circuit last summer, striking out all six batters he faced at East Coast Pro and concluding his day by catching likely early first-rounder Jacob Lombard looking at a 95-mph fastball. He also produced during the spring, helping Citizen Christian Academy (Douglas, Ga.) win the Georgia Independent School Association Class A championship by spinning five shutout innings in the first game of the finals. He's extremely projectable at 6-foot-5 and 180 pounds as well as the best pitcher in Clemson's recruiting class.His fastball already sits at 91-94 mph and tops out at 96 with ride and armside run, and Bryant should add more velocity as he gets stronger. He utilizes two different breaking balls, a sweeping low-80s slider with the potential to become a plus pitch and an upper-70s curveball. He also displays aptitude for a fading changeup, though it's a bit firm in the mid-80s.Bryant generates his solid arsenal with athleticism and arm speed. His stuff and control will fluctuate at times, which is typical of a lanky high schooler who's still developing physically, but he generally throws strikes. He has the ingredients to become a mid-rotation starter.

When several heavy hitters came to Texas in February to scout college tournaments and some high school games, Harris dazzled them by striking out 17 in six no-hit innings. That outing was the first step in surpassing several more famous names to emerge as the consensus top mound prospect among Lone Star State prepsters. While he doesn't have the loudest stuff, he has feel for four pitches and has dominated all spring.Harris operates at 90-94 mph and reaches 96 with his fastball, displaying the ability to create carry, sink or cut while commanding it well. It could develop into a plus offering once it adds more power, as could his low-80s slider with high spin rates and flashes of two-plane depth. He lands a mid-70s curveball for strikes and has begun work on a mid-80s changeup.Harris has a projectable 6-foot-3 frame and, as one of the youngest pitchers in the Draft, plenty of time to add strength. The Texas recruit is athletic and moves well down the mound, creating good extension while easily repeating his delivery and pounding the strike zone. Assuming he turns pro as a top-two-rounds talent, he should advance quickly for a high schooler.

Scouting grades: Fastball: 60 | Slider: 55 | Cutter: 50 | Splitter: 50 | Control: 50 | Overall: 50Known more as a slugging third baseman for much of his high school career, Brown has dramatically improved on the mound this spring. He has added significant strength as a senior, pushing his fastball to 98 mph as the highlight of a power four-pitch arsenal. He'll get the chance to play both ways if he attends Mississippi State but is more likely to focus on pitching after turning pro for a seven-figure bonus.Brown can work in the mid-90s with some armside run on his heater and maintain his velocity deep into starts, though his fastball gets hit more than it should and he'll need to tweak its shape to maximize its effectiveness. He has the makings of a plus low-80s slider with sweeping action and also utilizes an upper-80s cutter. He does a nice job of killing spin on a mid-80s splitter that he employs as a changeup.Brown has a strong 6-foot-4 frame and operates with little effort from a low arm slot and wide angle. He has the ingredients for average or better control once he gains more experience. Some evaluators worry that his long arm action and tendency to open up in his delivery pose reliever risk, while others believe he can clean that up as he focuses on pitching. He's just scratching the surface of his potential and could develop into a mid-rotation starter.

After helping his high school win an Oregon state title in 2023, Kleinschmit went the junior college route, spending a year and earning All-American status at Linn-Benton Community College. He made a seamless transition into Oregon State’s rotation in 2025, striking out 11.2 K/9 and holding hitters to a .194 batting average. More success pitching on the weekends for the Beavers could help him be one of the first college lefties to come off the board in July.During his first season in Corvallis, Kleinschmit showed the ability to fill up the strike zone with three pitches. While he won’t overwhelm anyone with pure stuff, he makes up for it with movement and feel for pitching. His fastball typically sat in the 90-93 mph range in 2025, topping out at 95 mph, with good tailing life and scouts wonder what an uptick in velocity might do for his profile, a la Red Sox lefty Connelly Early. He has good rotation and depth with his upper-70s slurvy slider to go along with decent feel and deception with his low-80s changeup.Early’s step forward stuff wise came with added strength and there’s the potential for Kleinschmit to go in that direction especially once he gets into pro ball. He has a solid starting pitcher’s foundation with his pitch mix and ability to be around the zone and if he manages to find more in the tank this spring, he’ll be on a more upward trajectory on Draft boards.

Bailey worked just 23 2/3 innings at McLennan (Texas) CC before tearing the anterior cruciate ligament and meniscus in his left knee on March 4, ending his sophomore season. But that was more than enough to affirm his status as the top junior college prospect in the 2026 Draft, and he still could go as high as the second round following knee surgery. After spending 2025 at Baylor, he initially committed to Texas A&M before joining the Highlanders.Bailey works with a 93-96 mph fastball that touches 98 with sink that elicits more grounders than swings and misses. His 82-85 mph breaking ball can fluctuate between a curveball and slider and is most effective when it has tighter break. Both are solid offerings with the potential to get better, as is his mid-80s changeup with fade and tumble.A quality athlete who gets down the mound well, Bailey has some effort in his delivery but improved his strike throwing before he got hurt. There's projection remaining in his 6-foot-3 frame, so he could add some more power to his pitches. If he turns pro, he should be ready to return to the mound around the start of next season. He missed his junior year in high school while recovering from Tommy John surgery.

There is a constant desire for baseball to attract more athletes to the game, and the scouting industry has the chance to do just that with Bowen. The JSerra Catholic standout entered the summer as a former football player with raw tools and finished it rushing up Draft boards because of more confidence in his ability to use those tools with outstanding performances at showcase events like the Area Code Games.Bowen is a veritable toolshed in a strong, athletic 6-foot-3 package. A football standout who is now focusing on baseball only, he’s a physical right-handed hitter who has always been able to punish the baseball. It might be 70-grade raw power, but the question in the past was if he would hit enough to get to it. There’s been in-zone swing-and-miss in the past, but he found the barrel a lot more consistently over the summer, leading evaluators to be more bullish on his hitability.With plus athleticism that plays on the basepaths and in the outfield, Bowen has the chance to stick in center field, though he has the plus arm to profile well in right field if needed. He also gets high marks for his makeup and high energy play on the field. If the hit tool keeps improving, the Oregon State recruit could easily skyrocket into the first round.

Back in the late 1990s, Howard Horn was a part of the same Princeton pitching staff that featured future big leaguer and current Rangers general manager Chris Young. Howard's son, Archer, has become one of the more intriguing prospects in Northern California, a two-way player who most think has a brighter future as a position player after displaying his skillset over the summer at events like Perfect Game National and the Area Code Games.Horn joins Tyler Spangler as left-handed-hitting NorCal prep shortstops getting a lot of attention. While Spangler is the more highly regarded prospect, Horn might be a bit more explosive, albeit less polished. At the plate, he shows off good bat speed and the ability to loft the baseball, providing some confidence that he’ll impact the ball with high exit velocities, especially as he adds strength. Though not everyone loves his pure hit tool, there could very well be more power to come in the future.Horn is an average runner but is a good athlete who has been his school’s point guard on the basketball court in the past. He’s 93-95 mph off the mound, and that arm really works from shortstop. Some see a move to third or second in the future given his lack of pure footspeed, but refinement and consistency on his angles and transfers could give him a chance to stick. Both he and Spangler are committed to Stanford for college ball.

Glenbrook North's (Northbrook, Ill.) best prospect since Jason Kipnis played for the Spartans 20 years ago, Bass has the potential for at least average tools across the board. He doesn't have the same offensive upside as fellow Illinois prep shortstop Landon Thome, but some scouts think it's close and Bass is a superior athlete and defender. He outperformed Thome on the showcase circuit and projects as a possible second-rounder.Bass is a solid to plus runner with athletic actions at shortstop, a position he should be able to handle at higher levels. He has a strong arm with the ability to make throws from a variety of angles, and he has been clocked up to 90 mph on the mound. The Wake Forest recruit has a lot of similarities to Marek Houston, who played shortstop for the Demon Deacons before the Twins drafted him 16th overall last July.While he stands out most with his defense, Bass also has a lot to offer at the plate. He has a quick right-handed stroke and continues to add strength to his 6-foot-2 frame, flashing some opposite-field power and the possibility of 15-20 homers on an annual basis. He displays a mature approach at the plate and his biggest backers believe he can become a solid hitter. Scouts love his makeup.

A majority of big league talent to be drafted out of British Columbia in Canada has been of the position player variety, with Tyler O’Neill and Brett Lawrie the most recent top three round success stories. The last time a high school pitcher went early from the province was back in 2002 when left-hander Adam Loewen was the No. 4 overall pick in the Draft. A fellow southpaw, Duncan looked like he had a very good chance to end that drought, first exciting scouts after throwing well at various showcase events last summer and fall, then standing out this spring during trips to Arizona in Florida. He walked off the mound with an elbow injury during a spring outing with Team Canada in the Dominican Republic, and though it was confirmed in early June that he would require Tommy John surgery, the industry was still bullish on him being drafted and signed.A projectable 6-foot-3 lefty, Duncan has shown steady progress stuff-wise while being in front of scouts a lot since age 14 pitching for Canada’s junior national team. He was sitting around 88-89 mph with his fastball then and kind hovered in that range, with scouts waiting for a leap forward. It’s possible that started to happen at the Futures Showcase, when he was up to 95 mph -- sitting 93 -- with his heater. He’s also shown the ability to show off at least above-average secondary stuff, really selling his deceptive changeup with excellent arm speed and snapping off very good breaking stuff when he stays on top of it.Not only does Duncan have a true three-pitch mix (it's not too difficult to dream about three above-average-to-plus offerings in the future), he’s shown a propensity for strike-throwing and understanding how to mix all of his pitches. He’s committed to Vanderbilt for college, but there was conviction he would still go in the first few rounds and rehab his elbow under pro instruction.

A high-leverage reliever at North Carolina State and with Team USA during his first two years in college, Dudan moved to the rotation this spring. The good news is that his fastball/slider combination remained dynamic in longer outings and he dramatically improved his control, making him a possible first-rounder. The bad news is that he blew out his elbow after throwing 116 pitches against Notre Dame on April 4 -- he hit 98 mph in the eighth inning -- and required Tommy John surgery.Dudan averaged 96 mph and touched 99 with his fastball as a junior, showing carry at times and sink at others, though his heater stands out more for its ability to generate groundouts than empty swings. His tight upper-80s slider is a legitimate wipeout pitch, producing a 41 percent chase rate and 48 percent swing-and-miss rate this spring. He also showed some feel for an upper-80s changeup with sink and fade, though he barely used it.A quality athlete, Dudan cut his walk rate from 15 percent in his first two seasons to 6 percent in 2026. While his aptitude for a changeup and improved control enhance his chances of succeeding as a starter, scouts believe his delivery with a long arm action and low slot makes him more likely to wind up as a reliever. He'll likely go in the second round even though he won't return to the mound until late 2027.

Kennedy may be the most improved player in NCAA Division I this season. After batting .209 as a freshman at Texas in 2024 and topping the Big 12 Conference with 79 strikeouts in 2025 at Kansas State, he had LASIK surgery. Better vision has led to increased frequency and quality of contact, allowing him to earn Cape Cod League all-star honors last summer and eclipse the Wildcats' single-season home run record 20 this spring.Now that he's seeing the ball so much better, Kennedy is chasing fewer pitches and controlling the strike zone. While the right-handed hitter still will fish for some curveballs out of the zone, he has gone from being helpless against curves and sliders to crushing them. He's pulling balls in the air much more frequently than in the past, hitting them harder and generating plus raw power seemingly with just a flick of his wrist.Kennedy offers plenty of athleticism to go with his improved offensive profile. He shows solid speed out of the batter's box and even more quickness once he accelerates. A second baseman as a freshman and third baseman as a sophomore, he has upgraded his actions and lateral range at shortstop, where he displays solid arm strength.

The Florida panhandle is often a difficult area to get scouted due to its remote location. But evaluators will be spending plenty of time there just to see the pitching staff at South Walton High School. It’s a group led by Coleman Borthwick, transfer Griffin Loy and Lord, a super-projectable right-hander whose size and stuff certainly garnered attention during the summer showcase circuit at events like the East Coast Professional Showcase and MLB’s High School All-American Game.Lord is a tall and lanky 6-foot-8 right-hander with serious projection. His fastball will sit around 94 mph, though he’s shown the ability to reach back for 97-98 mph in shorter stints, and he throws it with heavy sink. At times, he can really snap off a plus upper-70s curveball, though he can try to do too much with it and it becomes slurvy. He doesn’t need a changeup in high school, but he did show a low-80s one occasionally over the summer, though it’s more of a batting practice fastball than anything truly effective.All of Lord’s arsenal comes from a fast, easy arm and it’s easy to dream on more velocity to come as he fills out that frame and learns to sync up his delivery consistently, which should also help his command. He did have Tommy John surgery as a freshman but has shown no ill effects now. He switched his college commitment from Florida to Mississippi State and will play for Brian O’Connor should the Draft not go his way.

The best player to come out of Redwood High School in Visalia, California, has been infielder Aaron Hill, though he went on to spend three years at Louisiana State before starting his pro career. Hill was drafted out of high school in the sixth round and it looks like based on talent, Volchko could go much earlier than that after popping up in a big way this spring.Volchko is big and projectable at 6-foot-4, reminding some of a young Noah Syndergaard with a high-waisted frame, and he has the chance to have a legitimate four-pitch mix. Coming from a high overhead slot, he’s thrown his fastball in the 92-96 mph range. His upper-80s slider should be a plus pitch that misses bats, but his 82-85 mph curve is also an effective breaker. His firm 87-88 mph changeup is behind, but he has feel for that pitch as well.While he’s a good athlete who tunnels his pitches well, scouts wonder about Volchko’s ability to throw anything with arm-side run from that slot. His strike-throwing has been inconsistent at times, though improvements in that area this spring are a big reason for his helium. Scouts were streaming in to see whether it might be worth trying to sign him away from his commitment to Stanford.

Yes, it is that Bonds. Peyton is the son of Bobby Jr., who played 11 years of pro ball with the Padres, Giants and in indy ball, grandson of Bobby, whose big league career spanned 14 years, and nephew of Barry. Also an outfielder, Peyton Bonds went from Franklin HS in New Jersey -- where he was a teammate of A's prospect Joshua Kuroda-Grauer -- to Campbell University for his freshman year. He then returned to the Garden State to play for Rutgers, where he had a solid sophomore season, then turned his performance up a notch this spring, though a hamstring injury suffered in an April series against UCLA kept him off the field for a spell.At 6-foot-5, 230 pounds, Bonds looks like he was sent down from central casting, even if his current offensive profile doesn't quite fit the body type. He has a really good feel to hit, with excellent in-zone bat-to-ball skills. There's plenty of bat speed and high exit velocities, but it hasn't translated to power just yet, with some thinking there could be more unlocked with some bat path adjustments at the next level.While Bonds has average speed, maybe a tick better, he might not be a pure base stealer. But he can really play center field, with outstanding instincts to stay up the middle for a long time. He gets high marks for his plus makeup and passion for the game, perhaps handed down from his father, who grinded it out in the Minors for more than a decade.

The toolsy Broussard has flown under the radar more than he should, going undrafted out of a suburban Houston high school in 2023 and again after batting .409 with 17 steals in 36 games at San Jacinto (Texas) JC a year later. He's still overshadowed a bit in a deep crop of Texas college outfielders but is beginning to shed his anonymity. He stood out with his athleticism last spring after transferring to Houston and won co-MVP honors in the Cape Cod League All-Star game last summer.Broussard's best tool is his plus-plus speed, and he looks to get the most out of it by making consistent contact from the left side of the plate. He has a reasonably disciplined approach and hits hard line drives from gap to gap. He features bat speed and wiry strength, and he generates some surprising exit velocities along with occasional opposite-field power for a player listed at 6 feet and 170 pounds. He added muscle before his junior season and won't turn 21 until two months after the Draft, so he has more projection remaining than most college players.An accomplished basestealer, Broussard swiped 41 bags in 45 attempts (91 percent) between Houston and the Cape in 2025. His speed also plays well in center field, where he's a quality defender. With average to solid arm strength, he's capable of fitting anywhere in the outfield.

Holloway has revamped his mechanics after transferring from Nolensville (Tenn.) HS to Nashville's Christ Presbyterian Academy, where his pitching coach is former Cy Young Award winner R.A. Dickey. He's generating one of the best fastballs and some of the most effortless velocity in this Draft, though his lack of feel for a breaking ball is a concern. His stuff backed up briefly when he was hit in the head by an errant throw during practice in early April, but he was back to touching the upper 90s by the end of the month.After working with a 92-96 mph fastball that lacked life last summer, Holloway now owns a 94-98 mph heater that has peaked at 100 with improved command and tumbling action. He's still not displaying much aptitude for spin, flashing some decent mid-80s sliders but most often working with a below-average breaker. He has much more trust in a slightly harder changeup with depth.The 6-foot-5 Holloway has tightened up his delivery and is doing a better job of keeping his long limbs in sync and providing consistent strikes. Lowering his arm slot has helped the Vanderbilt commit improve the shape of his fastball and his ability to harness it. He's one of the more intriguing left-handers available, though he comes with significant reliever risk and plenty of divergent opinions on where he belongs in the Draft.

Riojas won 15 games in two years as a reliever at Texas-San Antonio before transferring to Texas, where he worked his way into the rotation by mid-2025. He was pitching himself toward the top three rounds before coming down with the flu and bronchitis, costing him 20 pounds and sapping his stuff as he surrendered 27 runs in 14 2/3 innings over his final starts. After going unselected last July, he has emerged as one of the best senior prospects in the 2026 Draft.By regaining the weight he lost and adding another 10 pounds, Riojas boosted his four-seam fastball 2-3 mph to parking at 94-96 and peaking at 98 with carry. His heater has been unhittable while he has commanded it to both sides of the plate this spring. He uses a diving 82-85 mph splitter as his changeup and it has been just as untouchable. His upper-80s cutter can be a plus pitch at its best, and he also deploys a sweepier mid-80s slider and a low-80s curveball.Riojas will unleash four-seamers, curveballs, cutters and splitters from a high three-quarters arm slot, then mix in two-seamers and sliders from a lower angle. He pounds the zone with his entire arsenal, and all of his pitches save his slider elicit plenty of chases as well. The only real knocks on him are that he's not terribly deceptive and he'll turn 23 on Draft Day, but he's advanced enough to make his pro debut in Double-A and could help a big league rotation in a hurry.

There has never been a high schooler from Vermont to get drafted and make it to the big leagues in the six decades since the Draft was instituted. Only two have ever been selected in the top five rounds, most recently when the Pirates took Owen Kellington in the fourth round of the 2021 Draft. McCarthy moved up his own timetable to join this select group when he reclassified in January to join the Class of 2026.While McCarthy is a bit undersized at 6-foot even, he's strong and extremely athletic on the mound and has shown at least glimpses of four potentially plus future pitches. He'll throw both a four-seam fastball up in the zone and a two-seamer with hard sink, popping some at 99 mph, sitting around 96 mph. His changeup might be his best secondary offering at present, sold with very deceptive arm speed and featuring solid late fade. He'll show off two distinct breaking pitches, with a sweeper of a slider at around 88 mph and a power downer low-80s curveball.With a super fast arm and his athleticism on the mound, McCarthy repeats his delivery well and throws a decent amount of strikes. The one thing he lacks is track record; he wasn't on the 2025 summer showcase circuit with his new class because he didn't reclassify until the winter. He opened a ton of eyes with an early start in Florida, then tried to do too much in his first start up north, with evaluators trying to figure out a bit of an irregular pitching schedule for the Tennessee recruit as the weather warmed up.

The main attraction at UCLA for 2026 is undoubtedly Roch Cholowsky, the No. 1 prospect in the class. But teams not picking at the very top of the Draft will still have good talent to evaluate, starting with Levu, who spent most of his first two years with the Bruins as their starting first baseman. After a solid freshman campaign (.809 OPS), Levu took a nice step forward as a sophomore in 2025, finishing with a .320/.389/.522 line to go along with a dozen homers, finishing things off by playing for the U.S. National Collegiate Team over the summer.A strong left-handed hitter, Levu has a fast bat and a good swing, with a ton of raw power to tap into. He can drive the ball to all fields with ease with at least plus raw pop. His impressive bat-to-ball skills and plate coverage can work against him at times; because he can get to everything, he swings at everything. He cut down on his swing-and-miss in 2025, but he’ll need to dial down the chase. If he does that, he could be among the nation’s leaders in home runs. Levu has incredible hand-eye coordination, proving it as an excellent pickleball player and in his experience as a flame-twirler as part of his Samoan culture.While most feel Levu will likely be destined for first base (where he is a solid defender) at the next level, there is some athleticism for him to work with. He can throw and his hands work fine, albeit with long actions, so some don’t want to rule out the possibility of playing third as a pro. Regardless of his defensive home, his bat could fly off the board early, especially if he tones down his approach at the plate.

Sims has the most electric arm in the high school class and repeatedly pushed his fastball to 100 mph on the showcase circuit last summer. He also has a 6-foot-8, 205-pound frame with plenty of room to add strength, so there may be more velocity to come. After winning an Oklahoma state 6-A championship at Edmond Santa Fe HS last spring, he transferred to Prestonwood Christian Academy (Plano, Texas) to play his senior season for James Vilade, a former scout and pro and college coach.Sims previously worked from a three-quarters arm angle and topped out at 97 mph with more sink on his four-seamer. After raising his slot in the past year, he now operates at 94-97 and reaches triple digits with more carry. He generates outstanding velocity with arm speed and little effort, another reason to project future velocity gains. The rest of his arsenal needs work, because he lacks consistency with his shapes and control with his mid-80s slider and changeup.Sims still is learning to keep his extra-long limbs in sync in his delivery, and while he throws his heater for strikes, his fastball command is a work in progress. He'll require time to develop and comes with reliever risk but also immense arm talent. He'll turn 19 a month before the 2026 Draft and would be sophomore-eligible in 2028 if he follows through on his Oklahoma commitment.

Peterson recorded an immaculate inning during his first college start in 2024 and has been Sam Houston's Friday-night starter the last two seasons. Big league clubs had interest in signing him as a sophomore-eligible last year, but he turned down their overtures and went undrafted. His feel for spin and delivery metrics could allow him to join 1984 second-rounder Kevin Renz as the highest-drafted pitchers in Bearkats history.Peterson owns one of college baseball's best curveballs, a high-spin 79-82 mph hammer, and ratchets up the rpms on a solid low-80s slider as well. He also imparts high spin rates on a 91-94 mph fastball that tops out at 96, and it plays up a bit thanks to shape, command and deception. He barely uses a mid-80s changeup that needs a lot of work.If Peterson threw harder, he might be a first-rounder. He's an athletic mover who gets down the mound well, creating plenty of extension from a low release height. He not only throws strikes with all three of his primary offerings, but he also locates them well, enhancing his mid-rotation profile.

More often than not, intriguing high school arms in Colorado go on to college and then come to the pro game, like Kevin Gausman or Kyle Freeland. There have been some high-octane arms who pop up late, as the weather warms up in the Centennial State, often getting above-slot deals to sign them away from a college commitment, like Trey Gregory-Alford getting seven figures to join the Angels in 2024. Wachsmann was getting some TGA comps as a pop-up guy with arm strength who was taking a big step forward step and results wise after a relatively pedestrian summer.Coming out of the same Grandview HS program that produced Gausman, Wachsmann is a 6-foot-4 right-hander who can light up the radar gun but is still a bit raw as a complete pitcher. He routinely gets his fastball up to 97 mph and can comfortably sit in the mid-90s. He does have both a curve and a slider, with the former the better of his two breaking balls, occasionally flashing plus. That pitch was improving as the spring wore on, though evaluating his ability to spin the ball is still more projection than anything else. He'll flash a changeup that could be average in time to round out his arsenal.Like with a lot of prep throwers over pitchers, Waschmann is control over command, with more of a fringy feel for the strike zone. Teams that are interested will be buying the arm strength and velocity to go along with the projectable and athletic frame. They'll also have to contend with a commitment to Wake Forest.

One of the smallest and least projectable prep pitchers in MLB Pipeline's Draft rankings, Sullivan also has one of the quickest arms and most explosive fastballs. He ran his heater up to 97 while dominating at the Area Code Games, though he struggled at the U.S. 18-and-under national team trials. He also has a pair of promising secondary offerings and his total package could land him a seven-figure bonus.Sullivan's fastball sat at 94-95 mph on the showcase circuit and has parked at 92-94 in the colder Ohio weather this spring, and its carry makes it difficult to hit and to command. His low-80s curveball with downer break shows the potential to become at least a solid weapon, as does his mid-80s changeup with fade and sink. He also has a short mid-80s slider.Sullivan has so much action on his pitches and some effort in his delivery that he sometimes struggles to find the strike zone. His quality athleticism eventually could translate into at least average control, though some scouts wonder if his lack of physicality or smooth mechanics will make it tough to hold up as a starter. Similarly built Jacob Miller hasn't lived up to expectations since the Marlins made him a supplemental first-round pick out of an Ohio high school in 2022, but he also didn't have Sullivan's power. A Tennessee commit, he'll be Draft-eligible as a sophomore in 2028 if he makes it to college.

Scouting grades: Fastball: 65 | Slider: 55 | Changeup: 50 | Control: 45 | Overall: 45Like his older brother, Jaxon, who was selected in the second round by the Cubs in 2023 despite missing his junior season at Arkansas following Tommy John surgery, Wiggins has a big body and an electric arm. He's more advanced at the same stage than his sibling, though his stuff stands out more than his polish. He skipped several events on the showcase circuit during the summer but was the most dominant pitcher at both the High School All-American Game and Perfect Game National.Wiggins generates premium velocity with relative ease, dealing at 93-96 mph and hitting 99 with late run and good carry on his fastball. He also has a power breaking ball -- a mid-80s slider with depth that repeatedly gets chases out of the zone. His changeup features similar velocity and nice tumble, showing the potential to become at least an average third offering. Wiggins is athletic and throws with little effort, but he has yet to prove he can command his fastball or land his secondary pitches in the strike zone. There's still projection remaining in his 6-foot-5 frame, so he could add more velocity in the future. The Arkansas recruit has a high ceiling as a potential No. 2 starter, but he'll need time to get there and comes with a good deal of reliever risk.

Teams searching for a combination of big tools and the confidence of having grown up around the game have been heading to California and high school powerhouse Harvard-Westlake to evaluate Gasparino. The son of Dodgers scouting director Billy Gasparino, Will Gasparino was a mainstay on the summer showcase circuit and is hoping to join Harvard-Westlake alums like Max Fried, Lucas Giolito, Jack Flaherty and, most recently, 2020 first-rounder Pete Crow-Armstrong as an early-round selection.Gasparino screams projection from his 6-foot-6 body, with raw tools aplenty and a high baseball IQ. His bat is a little bit of a work in progress, with long levers that can lead to some swing-and-miss. It often takes guys this size a bit longer to figure it out, but he has tremendous raw power that could be plus in time, and when it’s clicked at the plate, he’s shown he can do real damage from the right side.A plus runner, Gasparino moves like a much smaller guy, with the potential for him to be a threat on the bases and be a plus defender. His performance over the summer was uneven, but he’s been watched carefully this spring in SoCal as the Texas recruit has tremendous upside with the chance to be a five-tool standout.

Tinney earned All-America honors as a Notre Dame sophomore last spring, slashing .348/.498/.753 with 17 homers to stamp himself as one of the top catching prospects for 2026. His stock took a hit when batted .165 with a 40 percent strikeout rate in the wood-bat Cape Cod League and struggled during fall practice after transferring to Texas, but he has rebounded with a strong junior season. Few players in this Draft can match his combination of raw power and arm strength.At 6-foot-4 and 240 pounds and with a right-handed stroke geared to crush balls in the air to his pull side, Tinney is designed to hit home runs. He generates high-end exit velocities, but there are concerns about his hitting ability and how much of his double-plus raw pop he'll be able to get to in pro ball. He takes a lot of offspeed pitches for strikes and swings through a lot of offerings of all types in the zone, though he mitigates his excessive strikeouts with a healthy amount of walks.Tinney can flash well-above-average arm strength, though it plays more as plus and he needs to improve his transfer and the accuracy of his throws. He lacks quickness and agility behind the plate, though he has become a better receiver and framer this spring and perhaps can become average in both regards. His best-case scenario is that he becomes a larger version of Hunter Goodman.

Rutgers' regular catcher as a freshman in 2024, Natili has improved offensively and defensively in each of the last two years after transferring to Cincinnati. He ranked second in the Cape Cod League with six homers last summer and has continued to display some of the best power among the 2026 college catching crop this spring. He could become the Bearcats' highest pick since Ian Happ went ninth overall in 2015.Natili makes the most of his plus raw pop by launching balls in the air, and he has the ability to drive pitches out of the park to all fields. His right-handed swing can get too long, leading to swings and misses at an alarming number of fastballs, including in-zone heaters. He does mitigate that flaw with a relatively selective approach but always will be a power-over-hit guy.A well-below-average runner, Natili moves better behind the plate than he does on the bases. He's getting better as a receiver and blocker, leading to hope that he can become an average defender. His strong arm and leadership skills are assets.

In 2022, Xavier Isaac was a big 6-foot-4 left-handed-hitting first baseman coming out of the Florida high school ranks, one who ended up being a surprise first-round selection of the Rays. Isaac has since reconfigured his body and proven the Rays right by being one of the better power-hitting prospects in the Minors. Could his path help Bailey, another 6-foot-4, lefty-swinging corner infielder also from the Florida prep scene? In some ways, Bailey is ahead of where Isaac was in his senior season. While Bailey is very physical, he’s more athletic than you might think when looking at him, while Isaac had to commit to conditioning after entering pro ball. Similarly, Bailey has tremendous raw power, though there’s work to be done for him to tap into it consistently. Some scouts feel he’s too stiff in the box and needs to change a setup that leads to swing-and-miss, while others do see some hit tool, but perhaps with a barrel that tends to be a bit late. Bailey’s surprising athleticism does give him the chance to be more than just a first baseman, though most feel that’s where he will eventually land. The Florida State recruit has seen time in left field and third base, where his above-average arm plays well. But it’s the power potential at the plate that could have teams interested in keeping the Tallahassee native from staying home for college, though some teams’ models will not like that he will be 19 when the Draft rolls around.

Atoka, Okla., has a population of 3,188 and has produced one big leaguer (Ted Blankenship, who debuted in 1922) and one draftee (Patrick Wyrick, an Expos 19th-rounder in 1999). Ritchie is the town's best prospect in years, though he was hard for scouts to get a read on. He didn't participate much on the showcase circuit and didn't face much in the way of high school competition, so he rarely has faced a fastball better than 80 mph. Ritchie does have an easy left-handed swing but just hasn't been tested much. The best pitching he has faced came at the Midwest Scouts Association Showcase last July, and he drew three full-count walks after putting on a show in batting practice. He generates plus raw power with his bat speed and strength. Ritchie has good athleticism for a 6-foot-2, 210-pounder, though he'll probably lose a bit of his present speed as he continues to mature physically. A shortstop in high school, the Oklahoma State recruit projects as a third baseman at the next level. He should become an average defender at the hot corner with arm strength to match, and he should also be able to handle left field.

Rabe starred as a South Carolina prepster but blew out his elbow before debuting at Mississippi and redshirted in 2024 while recovering from Tommy John surgery. After pitching sparingly last spring, he has gone from dominant piggyback reliever to effective starter in 2026. He could ride one of the best fastballs and some of the best strike-throwing in college baseball into the top three rounds.Rabe's fastball elicits plenty of chases, empty swings and groundballs thanks to its combination of velocity (averaging 96 mph, topping out at 100), carry, command and deception. Both his upper-80s cutter and mid-80s slider have improved over the course of the season, and they were untouchable when he struck out 13 in six shutout innings in a Southeastern Conference tournament start against Alabama. He hasn't shown much aptitude for an upper-80s changeup with some depth.An athletic 6-foot-5, 200-pounder, Rabe repeats his delivery so easily that he issued just one unintentional walk to 79 batters faced a year ago before logging a 3 percent walk rate this spring. Not only does he pound the strike zone, but he's adept at working around the edges and rarely leaves balls over the plate to get pounded. He could raise his ceiling to potential mid-rotation starter if he can improve his secondary pitches.

Schaffner earned all-Summit League honors in both of this seasons at North Dakota State and was the Summit defensive player of the year in 2025 before transferring to North Carolina. He has been a revelation with the Tar Heels, displaying more impact and quickness than anticipated. His on-base ability and scrappy play prompt comparisons to former UNC outfielder Kane Kepley, a Cubs second-rounder last year, though he won't go quite as high in the Draft.A left-handed hitter, Schaffner has good feel for the barrel and focuses on lashing line drives from gap to gap. He has a disciplined approach and rarely swings and misses, especially against fastballs. While he won't be a slugger, he's hitting the ball harder than ever this spring and could produce 10-12 homers annually with a healthy amount of doubles.Schaffner's front-foot hitting style leads to plus-plus home-to-first times, but he's more a plus runner with good instincts on the bases and in the field. He's a rangy and extremely reliable defender at shortstop, though his arm likely will dictate a move to second base in pro ball. He has below-average to fringy pure arm strength that plays as average because he has a knack for running through balls and using his momentum to create carry on his throws.

Eight Illinois prep pitchers have received seven-figure bonuses in the previous four Drafts, and Slightom could be the next in line. After quarterbacking Lyons Township to the state 8-A football playoffs during the fall, he has added velocity this spring and has more projection remaining.Slightom worked with an 88-91 mph fastball as a junior, touched 95 at the World Wood Bat Association World Championship in October and has reached 98 with carry and armside run this spring. His 77-81 mph slider could use some power and lacks consistency, yet he'll spin some solid breaking balls at times. He doesn't use his changeup very often but shows some aptitude for the mid-80s offering, which flashes some nice tumble and fade.Slightom works with a side-step delivery and a low arm slot that provides some deception. A Cincinnati recruit, he's athletic and should add some needed polish once he focuses on baseball. With room to add plenty of strength to his 6-foot-4 frame, he could develop an upper-90s heater and a mid-80s slider.

Missouri State has produced big league batting champions (Bill Mueller), home run leaders (Ryan Howard, Luke Voit) and Rookie of the Year Award winners (Drake Baldwin). Now the Bears feature one of the most physically imposing and powerful hitters in the 2026 Draft. Bogenpohl broke Howard's school freshman home run record with 20 in 2024 and tied another Missouri State mark with 55 walks last spring.The 6-foot-6, 245-pound Bogenpohl offers plus-plus raw power from the left side of the plate and can crush pitches out of the park from foul pole to foul pole, yet he only homered six times in 53 games as a junior entering the NCAA tournament. He employs a disciplined approach and won't chase when pitchers refuse to challenge him, but he's also leaking open early and not doing damage on offerings on the outer half of the plate. His extra-large frame results in an extra-long stroke, too much in-zone swing-and-miss and groundball contact, as well as difficulties against non-fastballs.Bogenpohl has surprising athleticism for his size and shows solid speed once he gets going. He has played a capable center field in college but likely is destined for an outfield corner in pro ball. After showing well-above-average arm strength in the past, his throwing grades more as average to solid now.

Few players in the high school class offer a better power/speed combination or spark more debate about their hitting ability than Wilson. He generates high exit velocities and can get from the left side of the plate to first base in less than four seconds. He could go in the second or third round to a club that believes in his bat.Wilson had difficulty making contact on the showcase circuit, especially against non-fastballs, and some scouts think his high back elbow and uphill left-handed stroke will limit him as a hitter. Other evaluators think he has the hand-eye coordination, patience and rhythm to develop at least average bat-to-ball skills once he tones down his setup. No one questions his bat speed or strength, which combine to give him plus raw pop that plays from gap to gap.Wilson owns plus-plus speed but is still learning to get the most out of it. He's raw as a basestealer and stands out more with his quickness than his instincts in center field. He can improve his below-average arm strength if he learns to incorporate his lower half better on his throws. He'll turn 19 a month before the Draft, which will bother age-minded clubs, and will be a sophomore-eligible in 2028 if he makes it to Vanderbilt.

Sdao had the best stuff on Texas A&M's 2024 College World Series runner-up team and looked like a lock to go in the top three rounds of this year's Draft. But he injured his elbow in the Super Regionals and when rehab didn't cure him, he had Tommy John surgery last fall. Despite missing the entire season, he still might command a seven-figure bonus.Before he got hurt, Sdao worked at 92-94 mph and reached 97 with a fastball that carried past bats up in the zone. His best offering was a sweeping low-80s slider that carved up both lefties and righties. His mid-80s changeup featured some sink but will need improvement to become an average pitch.Though Sdao has mid-rotation potential, he has started just seven games in three years of college and worked more than five innings in an outing just twice. His wiry 6-foot-2 frame has more projection remaining and he generates his stuff with arm speed rather than effort. He provides plenty of strikes with all three of his pitches and commands his fastball well.

Aptos High School in Northern California has had five players drafted but none since 2008. That year right-hander Kevin Eichhorn went in the third round, though he never reached the big leagues. Gaeckle, who jumped out on the summer showcase circuit at events like the Area Code Games, has the chance to break his school’s dry streak in the Draft this July.A bit undersized at 5-foot-11, Gaeckle is a strong and athletic right-hander with some interesting pitch ingredients. His fastball has averaged around 92 mph this spring, up to 95, and he was up a tick or two higher over the summer. He has a live arm, and while the fastball tends to have a true plane, there’s a little hop to it. His hard curve, averaging around 76 mph, is his best pitch, and it could be plus, with high spin rates and a lot of depth. He started mixing in a mid-80s slider that could be effective, but it’s further away, and while he doesn’t throw it a ton, he has shown the ability to sell his changeup with arm speed, though it lacks consistent shape.Gaeckle tends to be around the strike zone and with his athleticism, he should have solid command in the future. The biggest question teams might have when deciding whether to try to sign him away from his Arkansas commitment is that he had Tommy John surgery when he was 15 years old.

Lynch's live arm and physicality attracted scouts during his New Jersey high school days, but he didn't make a jump as a senior and went undrafted before heading to North Carolina. As a freshman last spring, he dominated out of the bullpen before logging a 2.25 ERA in three postseason starts, including seven strong innings against Arizona in the NCAA super regional finals. He has been more steady than spectacular in a full-time rotation role this spring and scouts aren't sold on him as a starter.Lynch's best pitch is a power sinker that sits at 94-96 mph and tops out at 98 with plenty of horizontal action, making it difficult to lift. His slider operates at 83-85 mph with both depth and sweep, sometimes breaking so much that it's tough to control or induce chases with it, and it has been much more hittable as he has worked longer stints. He has developed an upper-80s changeup but struggles to throw it for strikes and it gets whacked when he does.Lynch has a strong 6-foot-3 frame built for rotation work, and the extension and low release height in his delivery create plenty of deception. He's throwing more strikes this year as a starter, though he still needs to refine his command. He was dynamic coming out of the bullpen last year and has closer upside if he returns to that role.

Most evaluators believe that Koeninger has a brighter future as a right-handed pitcher than as a power-hitting infielder. He prefers to play shortstop but didn't have a good summer with the bat on the showcase circuit. The U.S. national team used him strictly as a reliever while winning the 18-and-under World Cup in Japan in September.Koeninger works primarily with a 92-95 mph fastball that touches 97 and a low-80s curveball that can be a hammer at its best. He barely uses a low-80s changeup that he'll need to refine to succeed as a starter. He looks more strong than fluid on the mound but provides strikes and could improve significantly if he became a full-time pitcher.Koeninger is a physical right-handed hitter with plenty of raw power, but he comes with questions about his ability to hit quality pitching. His right-handed swing can get too long and the Tennessee recruit has trouble staying back on breaking balls and changeups. A plus runner with well-above-average arm strength, he projects as a solid defender at shortstop and a plus glove at third base.

Sides was a premium athlete in northern California, playing both basketball and baseball at Red Bluff High School about two hours north of Sacramento. Wanting to face better competition, improve his game and up his profile, it was announced in the middle of a summer showcase tour that included stops at Perfect Game National, the Area Code Games and MLB’s High School All-American Game, that Sides had decided to move south to powerhouse Orange Lutheran, forming a potentially dominant pitching combination with fellow right-handed 2026 Draft prospect Gary Morse.Sides is a big, physical 6-foot-5 hurler with the chance to have a legitimate four-pitch mix. He throws his fastball in the 92-95 mph range, showing the ability to throw the pitch with good life and carry up in the zone and sink down. His 83-85 mph slider flashes above-average with sweeper shape and he’ll also flip in a more fringy 78-80 mph curve to provide a different look. There’s also a harder, 85-87 mph changeup with late depth in his arsenal.Sides showed better pure stuff than his new teammate Morse, over the summer, with better feel to spin, though with not as sharp command. There are those who think he could end up being one of the best prep arms from California in the class, with SoCal scouts who lack history with the LSU recruit eager to see more.

Six big leaguers have come out of Florida Gulf Coast University, none bigger than Chris Sale, a first-round pick back in 2010. Since then, only eight Eagles have been taken in the top 10 rounds, with Justin Henschel going in the seventh round a year ago to the Reds most recently. Dempsey, who has been a productive two-way player since he set foot on campus, has a chance to become the first FGCU product to go in the top five rounds since 2023, most likely as a pitcher.Dempsey’s athleticism definitely plays on the mound with an intriguing combination of an electric fastball and a pair of breaking balls as his go-tos nearly all of the time. He’s been sitting around 92-94 mph more often than not, touching 95 at times. His velocity has been down a touch with fatigue from double duty as an outfielder playing a part and leading evaluators to believe there’s a few ticks to come when he focuses on pitching only. He gets elite spin rates north of 3,000 rpm with his breaking stuff, leading with an 80-82 mph curve that has 11-to-5 shape and misses bats when it’s on. He can change his arm slot at times to get on top, something that will have to be addressed at the next level because good hitters will pick that up. He also spins a harder slider, and while he’s shown a fringy changeup, he doesn’t use it much. He’s been a solid strike-thrower.At the plate, Dempsey has a good feel for hitting from the left side of the plate, taking professional at-bats, working counts and making a ton of contact, though he’s yet to show much impact. There’s more ceiling with him on the mound, reminding some of a Luke Weaver-type, who should get a chance to start out of the gate, but could eventually land in the bullpen.

The Pittsburgh area high school ranks have produced some solid bats over the years, from Neil Walker back in 2003 to Alex Kirilloff in 2016 and, most recently, Cole Young in 2022. All three of those hitters were first-round picks, and Williams, who plays for Franklin Regional High School just east of the city, doesn’t appear to be joining that trio that high on Draft boards. But after missing the summer showcase circuit with an ankle injury, Williams’ ability to show off his premium athleticism this spring had him popping up in early-round conversations.While he’s a bit raw all-around, Williams is as twitchy an athlete as there is in this class. He’s not without tools, with elite bat speed and some definite raw power that he’s still learning how to tap into from the right side of the plate, though it remains to be seen if he can handle premium pitching. Some scouts put the maximum 80 grade on his speed, and it was completely back this spring with no ill effects from the injury. He runs with reckless abandon, with singles and walks quickly turning into triples.It remains to be seen if Williams can slow the game down enough to play shortstop long-term. He has more than enough arm, showing the ability to crank throws in the 90s across the diamond, and his speed and athleticism help him cover a lot of ground. He plays with his hair on fire at all times, largely a good thing, but he’ll need to show he can stay within himself. He gets high marks for his passion for the game, gaining kudos from the scouting industry for showing up to DH at fall events even though he wasn’t at full strength just because the Vanderbilt commit wanted to compete.

It’s a fairly regular occurrence for some high school bats from the Mid-Atlantic states to pop up as the Draft approaches. Jackson Merrill, a first-rounder in 2021 from Maryland is the big name, while Jonny Farmelo (2023) and Griffin Burkholder (2024) were Virginia products who went in the top two rounds of their Drafts. In some ways, Yow is ahead of his predecessors because none of that trio landed on MLB.com’s December Draft Top 100. It’s still hard to pinpoint where the Virginia prep shortstop fits because he didn’t do much on the summer showcase circuit, though he certainly opened eyes in Jupiter, Fla., this fall at Perfect Game’s WWBA World Championship.All conversations about Yow start with his speed. He might be the fastest player in the class, a true 80 runner, one who some scouts clocked doing a 6.15 60-yard dash at the Border Battle this fall. But while he has a slight build, he’s a super-twitchy athlete with some projection, he makes a ton of contact and he gets to some impact, even with a wood bat in his hands. He may never be a slugger, but no one predicted Trea Turner would hit 20-plus homers in the big leagues, either.That’s relevant because Yow does give off some Turner vibes. Like Turner from his amateur days, scouts aren’t sure if Yow and his average arm can stick at shortstop long-term (Turner has played over 1,100 games at the position), so teams might not want to look away from him at the premium position. After his pop-up performance in Jupiter, Yow decommitted from Richmond and signed on with Virginia for college in his Charlottesville backyard.

A two-way player with some promise on the mound, Adams has cemented his future as a position player this spring. The consensus is that he owns one of the prettiest left-handed swings in the 2026 high school class, though there are some mixed opinions how much power he'll ultimately have. A club that believes he could get to 25 homers per season while remaining on an outfield corner could take him as early as the second round.Adams employs a patient approach and has a history of making consistent contact to all fields. In the past, he would get too passive at times and didn't tap into his raw power on a regular basis. He's doing a better job of being selectively aggressive and turning his quick stroke loose as a senior, enabling him to drive balls in the air to his pull side more often.While Adams can display average speed at times, he figures to be no better than a fringy runner once he's physically mature. The Louisiana State recruit has decent range but is still refining his reads and routes as a right fielder, while some scouts believing he's destined to wind up at first base, where he's a solid defender. His arm isn't a question because he reaches 91 mph with his fastball while showing feel for an upper-70s changeup and slider.

Lewis broke into college baseball by winning the Big East Conference batting title (.377) and freshman of the year award in 2024. He transferred to Wake Forest last year and has posted similar numbers against better competition, while also earning All-Star honors in the Cape Cod League. His hitting ability is unquestioned, but how much a club believes he can offer beyond that will determine how high he climbs in the Draft.Lewis employs a simple setup and a flat left-handed swing geared for contact. He controls the barrel very well, focusing on lacing line drives from gap to gap, and he destroys fastballs perhaps better than any hitter in college baseball. While he's 6-foot-2 and 220 pounds, he may not have more than average bat speed or raw power, though his bat-to-ball skills should help him get to most of it.If Lewis winds up at first base, which is where Wake Forest plans to deploy him this season, his relative lack of pop may become an issue. He played mostly third base in 2025, but he's an erratic defender with just average arm strength there. He's a fringy athlete and could get a look in left field after he turns pro.

Because he's one of the older legitimate prospects in the 2026 high school class -- 19 years and five months old on Draft Day -- Bowen creates some mixed opinions among evaluators. Some believe his mix of tools and potential to stay at shortstop could merit a third-round pick if he's signable. In large part because of his age, others would rather see him prove himself for two years at Oklahoma State before making a significant investment.Bowen has a simple right-handed stroke that he repeats well while focusing on producing line drives from gap to gap. He has the bat speed to catch up to quality velocity and makes consistent contact. He doesn't show much present raw power and generates a lot of grounders, so he may top out at 12-15 homers per season.Bowen has plus speed that translates into base-stealing ability and range at shortstop. He has solid arm strength and should be able to remain at short. His athleticism and arm give him the versatility to play almost anywhere on the diamond.

Ken Dugan won 1,137 games and two NAIA national titles in 37 seasons at Lipscomb (Tenn.), which is now an NCAA Division I program that plays at a field bearing his name. His grandson Jack has rebounded from a rough showcase circuit performance to emerge as the best hitter among Tennessee high schoolers with a strong spring. He helped his cause with an opposite-field homer off a 98-mph fastball from possible second-rounder Bo Holloway at a heavily scouted matchup in late March.Dugan uses a quick left-handed stroke to produce hard contact to all fields. He currently focuses on driving the ball from gap to gap but has the bat speed and strength for 20-homer power once he learns to launch more balls in the air to his pull side. He has settled down and made nice adjustments after trying to do too much at the plate last summer, when he struggled to catch up to velocity and couldn't solve secondary pitches.Though Dugan has solid speed and athleticism, he lacks the actions to stick in the middle infield. He has the arm strength and offensive upside to profile at third base, though some scouts believe he's destined for an outfield corner. He draws rave reviews for his makeup and will be Draft-eligible as a sophomore in 2028 if he follows through on his commitment to Tennessee.

In 2025, California prep power Corona High School saw three players get taken in the first 32 picks of the Draft. Murphy was arguably the best offensive performer on the team that saw right-hander Seth Hernandez and shortstop Billy Carlson go in the top 10 and Brady Ebel go No. 32 overall, with the outfielder hitting over .400 and slamming 11 homers as a junior. Murphy raised his profile at MLB Develops events like the Hank Aaron Invitational and Breakthrough Series, while shining at MLB’s High School All-American Game and winning a gold medal with Team USA at the WSBC U-18 World Cup in Japan.A strong, physically mature right-handed-hitting outfielder, Murphy’s two standout tools are his raw power and his defense. He has at least plus raw pop and has shown he can get to it in games in high school, but there are questions about his hit tool moving forward. He likes to change his swing and fiddle with his setup a lot, and there’s been a good amount of swing-and-miss and chase as a result, though there’s some conviction that finding consistency in his mechanics should allow him to find the barrel enough.A plus runner, Murphy is considered by some area scouts to be one of the best defensive outfielders they’ve seen in recent memory. He can play center field and if he slows down and moves to right, he has more than enough arm for the spot. All of the LSU recruit’s tools play up because of his full-throttle, hard-nosed play, giving more confidence he’ll maximize his ability as a pro.

Jesuit High School in Tampa has had a strong record of churning out professional-level talent, though the last one to sign from the school and make it to the big leagues was Lance McCullers Jr., a supplemental first-round pick back in 2012. Andersen, who participated over the summer in showcase events like Perfect Game National, PG’s All-American Game and USA Baseball’s 18U trials, has had a bit of an ascendant spring, potentially putting him in a position to be the first Jesuit product to get drafted since 2018.Over the summer, the 6-foot-4 Andersen sat around 93 mph and touched 96-97, and there’s been more consistent velocity during his senior year, routinely up to 97-98 with his heater. It can have good carry at the top of the zone, and he can get downhill at times. He has a solid mid-80s breaking ball that registers as a slider, though some feel it’s more of a power curveball shape-wise. Andersen showed off a very good changeup over the summer and in the fall, but it has sat on the shelf this spring because of the increase in velocity and overall stuff.Andersen has a very in-line delivery with his elbows tucked in, coming at hitters from a high slot. His command has suffered a bit as he’s learning to adjust to the firmer stuff, but he has the chance to be a dependable strike-thrower in the future. Not all evaluators are convinced he’s ready for pro ball, but plenty of teams will consider paying him now rather than wait to see how good he becomes after pitching at Mississippi State.

As a redshirt sophomore at Southwestern Illinois CC last spring, Brock slashed .462/.565/.870 and ranked third among national junior college players in hitting, on-base percentage and OPS (1.435) and fourth in slugging, and he was the only 20-20 player at that level, going deep 20 times and swiping 27 bases. After turning down over-slot offers from the Brewers as a 14th-rounder, he took his uncommon speed and athleticism for a catcher to Oklahoma. He has second-round tools but likely will last another round or two because of his struggles in Southeastern Conference play.Brock has a patient approach, quiet setup and a relatively compact right-handed stroke, but he also swings through a lot of strikes and mis-hits too many pitches. He crushes fastballs but really struggles against changeups and has problems with breaking balls as well. He possesses plus raw power and is at his best when he's driving balls from gap to gap.Brock combines plus-plus speed with basestealing aptitude. He'll slow down some if he remains behind the plate, where he moves well, is improving as a receiver and displays solid to plus arm strength. He also has started games at all three outfield positions for the Sooners, and a team wanting to maximize his bat and tools could opt to deploy him in center field.

Back trouble limited DeVaughan's exposure on the showcase circuit last summer, but he made up for lost time by reaching 96 mph with his fastball and 92 with his cutter at the World Wood Bat Association World Championship in October. He came out throwing even harder this spring while allowing no runs and just two hits while striking out 40 in his first 16 innings. He has emerged as one of the best high school pitching prospects in the Midwest and should become the first player ever drafted out of Mooresville (Ind.) HS, he missed the final month of the season with a scapular issue.With plenty of arm speed and little effort, DeVaughn produces four-seam fastballs that sit at 93-95 mph and top out at 98 with riding action, and he also owns an 89-91 mph two-seamer that sinks and a cutter with similar velocity. He shows feel for spinning a pair of breaking balls, a 78-80 mph curveball and an 82-84 mph slider with similar shape. He does a nice job of killing spin on a mid-80s changeup that fades, though he hasn't needed it much at this point.DeVaughan has a very projectable 6-foot-5 frame, so there should be more velocity to come. He has no problem throwing strikes while delivering pitches from a high three-quarters arm slot. If he follows through on his commitment to Alabama, he'll be Draft-eligible as a sophomore in 2028.

Parkview HS (Lilburn, Ga.) is one of the most successful high school programs in the country, winning nine state titles and three national championships in the last three decades while producing talents such as Jeff Francoeur, Jeff Keppinger and Matt Olson. The latest Panthers prospect is Washington, who tied for ninth nationally with 15 homers in 37 games last spring. He has a track record of performing at showcase events but still comes with some questions about his bat.Washington is solidly built at 6-foot-1 and 195 pounds with strong hands and wrists. He has a quick right-handed swing and generates impressive exit velocities, but he also gets overly aggressive at the plate and can struggle against breaking balls. He shows the ability to drive the ball to all fields yet makes more groundball contact than is desired.Washington's speed and arm strength both earn solid to plus grades. The Louisiana State recruit has a good chance to remain in center field in college or pro ball, and his arm and power would fit the right-field profile if he has to shift to a corner. He earns praise for his work ethic as well as his tools.

Dunlap sustained a break in his right femur near his knee that required two surgeries that cost him most of his sophomore season at Crown Point (Ind.) HS. He made a full recovery and since has developed into one of the top prep catchers in the 2026 class. Bigger and athletic than most backstops, he stands out with his power and arm strength.Dunlap's bat speed and the strength in his 6-foot-3, 205-pound frame give him plus raw pop. He'll need to make some adjustments at the plate to make the most of his power because his right-handed swing can get long and he'll chase pitches looking to do damage. He does focus on driving balls from gap to gap, showing some understanding that he doesn't have to turn and burn to drive balls out of the park.Despite his size and position, Dunlap can post plus times in the 60-yard dash and is an average runner on the bases. The Tennessee commit moves well behind the plate for such a tall catcher and should be able to stay there, though his receiving needs more polish. He blocks well and has solid arm strength but could tighten up his release.

It appears that Dodgers third base coach Dino Ebel knows how to help create good ballplayers. Last year, older son, Brady, went No. 32 overall to the Brewers, signing for $2.75 million. This year it’s Trey’s turn. Hailing from the same Corona High School powerhouse, the younger Ebel has been an integral part of the school’s success, including a 2024 National High School Invitational title, though he often spent it in Brady’s shadow. He’s now in the spotlight and has been creating a name for himself.There are some area scouts who think Trey is a more complete player than his brother at the same point in his career. He has more quality at-bats than just about any high school hitter in the area. Unlike his brother, he swings from the right side and has shown he can handle velocity as well as good breaking stuff, making solid swing decisions and exhibiting good bat speed. He’s hit over power now, using the whole field, but there’s some raw pop for him to tap into. Being an Ebel comes with a now-renowned work ethic, as well as access to high level instruction, and Trey has followed his brother in taking it and getting better. He’s shown a desire to improve deficits in his game, like working out with Brice Turang to improve his speed. The Texas A&M recruit is a better pure shortstop than Brady, with good instincts and a strong arm, even if he eventually needs to move to third and potentially be a plus defender there.

Johnson has been a famous prospect for years and won Gatorade's Georgia high school player of the year award as a junior last spring, but he presents a conundrum for scouts. They like the player more than his tools and his profile, and he regressed on the showcase circuit last summer after standing out there in 2024. It's unclear whether he'll get drafted high enough to divert him from a Vanderbilt commitment.Johnson takes better at-bats and makes contact more easily than most high school players. His right-handed swing can get too rotational at times, and he didn't do much damage against quality pitching during the summer, still putting the ball in play but producing too many rolled-over grounders. He does feature plenty of bat speed and strength, and his biggest proponents hope he can make adjustments and turn into a solid hitter with average power.As he has matured physically, Johnson has slowed down and now has below-average speed at best. He nevertheless has a chance to remain at shortstop, where his fluid actions and advanced instincts translate into surprising range. He has quick hands and solid arm strength, which would allow him to make an easy transition to third base if needed.

Cuvet was a mainstay on the high school summer showcase circuit in 2022, showing off his bat at events like the PDP League and the Perfect Game All-American Classic, where he was a finalist in the Home Run Derby. He continued to show off his carrying tool during his senior year at the Elite Squad Baseball (ESB) Academy in south Florida this spring.At 6-foot-4 and 225 pounds, Cuvet has the kind of frame and build that points to a corner infield profile. That carrying tool is his raw power and it’s definitely plus, with a ton of strength and the ability to drive the ball a long way. With that pop, however, comes a good amount of worry about swing-and-miss. There are holes in his swing, especially against breaking stuff, so he will have to make some adjustments in order to make consistent enough contact to tap into that power.While Cuvet has good hands and a plus arm that fired throws north of 90 mph from third base at the Draft Combine in June, his lack of footwork and quickness -- he’s a well below-average runner -- makes it less likely he can stick at the hot corner. First base is the most likely home for him, but any team trying to sign him away from his commitment to Miami will be interested in buying the bat, not the glove.

The nephew of former Georgia basketball standout and WNBA player Angel Robinson, Phelps was the top in-state recruit in the Bulldogs' 2023 class. It took him a couple of months to earn regular playing time as a freshman but he slashed .353/.441/.699 with 12 homers in 42 games, including a total of 34 starts at all four infield and outfield corners, as well as DH. He also was the top performer on the U.S. collegiate national team's four-game summer tour, furthering his case as a potential 2025 first-rounder, but a disappointing sophomore season has torpedoed those chances before he pulled out of the Draft in mid-July.After standing out with the ability to make both consistent contact and hard contact as a freshman, Phelps has regressed in both regards this spring. He's trying to do too much at the plate, chasing too many pitches and letting his right-handed swing get too long and uphill. He showed the possibility of becoming a solid hitter with power to match a year ago but has struggled mightily with non-fastballs in 2025.Phelps displays fringy speed out of the batter's box but is closer to solid once he gets going. He moves well in the field and shows soft hands and a strong if sometimes inaccurate arm, but he loses focus much too often. He can play an adequate corner outfield or first base but scouts no longer give him any hope of handling third base.

St. John Bosco High School in California has been a nationally ranked team this spring, with a talented roster typically led by infielder James Clark. Scouts could often double up on days that Garcia was pitching, and there have been times this year that the 6-foot-3 right-hander has stolen the headlines. Garcia has excelled under the spotlight, landing in the top five in average velocity and average breaking ball spin rate in his start at USA Baseball’s National High School Invitational, then outpitching Orange Lutheran’s Gary Morse back home in California.At his best, Garcia can show off two plus pitches in his fastball and curve. While he’ll pitch anywhere in the 92-97 mph range, he’s answered questions about maintaining his stuff by carrying mid-90s heat deep into starts, and there could be even more consistent velocity down the road. He throws it with 18-19 inches of ride, a four-seamer with true carry. He backs it up with a nasty 12-to-6 curve that has been known to register 3,000 rpm spin rates. He has a changeup and has shown some feel for it, but he doesn’t need it given how good the fastball-curve combination is.Making Garcia’s current two-pitch mix play up is his ability to throw it consistently for strikes. He often commands both offerings like someone at a more advanced level. He pitches with a bulldog mentality, but controls his effort down the mound and has shown that he maintains his composure in big moments. Concerns about some reliever risk have dissipated, which should only help the Long Beach State recruit’s Draft stock.

Dan Giese was a late-round pick of the Red Sox in the 1999 Draft and spent parts of three years in the big leagues with the Giants, Yankees and A’s before eventually entering the world of scouting, at one point serving as the Yankees director of professional scouting. His son, Gavin, had some serious helium as the spring progressed in Southern California and has a good chance to be the first player drafted from Dana Hills High School since Hans Crouse was a second-rounder in 2017.Giese offers a very intriguing combination of size -- he’s 6-foot-3 -- projection and, of course, MLB family ties. All of his stuff has taken a step forward this spring, starting with a fastball that sits around 93-94 mph currently.
