Not only does it play up because he throws it with high spin, but it’s easy to dream on more velocity to come as he fills out his athletic frame. His go-to secondary is his changeup, thrown with good late action to it in the 81-83 mph range. His breaking stuff is still a work in progress. He’ll show an average slider up to 83 mph and a typically upper-70s curve, but they currently lack the consistent bite you’d like to see.In addition to a potential four-pitch mix, Giese fills up the zone with all of his offerings and has a real feel to pitch that belies his years. Add in the ease of operation coming down the mound and how clean his arm action is and it’s obvious why cross-checkers were streaming in to see him this spring, enhancing the possibility that he won’t head to the University of San Diego, his father’s alma mater, for college ball.

After high school ball in Virginia outside of Washington D.C., Renfrow stayed in-state and headed to Virginia Tech for college ball. He stepped right into the Hokies' rotation and was the first freshman to pitch on opening day since the school moved to the Atlantic Coast Conference for the 2005 season. He’s been a mainstay since and has pitched twice for USA Baseball’s Collegiate National Team, with uneven results as a junior making it a little bit tougher to pinpoint exactly where in the Draft he might hear his name called.From a size and pure stuff standpoint, Renfrow’s name belongs in the early rounds with some of the top college arms in the class. The 6-foot-3 right-hander can run his fastball up to 97 mph, sitting around 94, and it plays really well at the top of the zone. At times, Renfrow will show distinct upper-80s sliders and cutters, with the former featuring harder break with more depth than the shorter cutter, though they routinely blend together. He can fold in an 80-mph 12-to-6 curve effectively and will flash a solid, average mid-80s changeup, though he doesn’t incorporate the cambio as frequently as his other offerings.While Renfrow has limited the walks this spring, it has been more control over command. When he’s locked in, like he was in a late April start against North Carolina State, tossing eight shutout innings and fanning nine, he misses bats with all of his repertoire. Often this year, however, he’s missed with his fastball in the zone too much, and it’s gotten hit. That’s made him a bit of an enigma, with his ceiling perhaps being in the second round, where former Hokies right-hander Drue Hackenberg went in 2023 after a comparable inconsistent season.

Stringer has starred for the West Marion HS (Foxworth, Miss.) varsity since he was an eighth-grader, though his stuff would fluctuate because he was worked hard while also pulling double duty as a shortstop. He wasn't a regular on the showcase circuit, another reason he didn't register on scouting radars until he started popping mid-90s fastballs this spring. When he sustained that velocity and showed a pair of interesting secondary pitches, he became a candidate for the top three rounds.Working out of a high slot, Stringer produces impressive carry on a heater that ranges from 92-97 mph with the potential for more velocity to come. His arm angle allows him to stay on top of a 78-82 mph curveball with downer break that gives him a second plus offering, as well as a low-80s splitter with similar promise. He'll mix in an occasional mid-80s slider that lags behind the rest of his repertoire.Stringer has a strong 6-foot-3, 205-pound frame that could add more muscle. He creates some deception with good extension and some crossfire, and he shows the ability to move his fastball around the zone and deliver his secondary pitches for strikes. Originally committed to Pearl River (Miss.) CC, he signed with Louisiana State in late April.

In a down year for high school right-handers, Rice has a better combination of a deep arsenal and polish than most. He'd be a lock to go in the top two or three rounds if not for his lack of physicality and his commitment to Auburn, but he still has plenty of believers among evaluators. He's built along the lines of Tim Hudson and his style is reminiscent of the four-time All-Star.Rice's best pitch is a tight mid-80s slider with high spin rates, but it's just one of four weapons at his disposal. His fastball sits in the low 90s, touches 95 and misses bats with its carry and armside run. His feel to spin extends to a low-80s curveball that he lands in the zone, and he also shows advanced aptitude for a fading mid-80s changeup.Rice has an up-tempo delivery but the athleticism to repeat it well and throw strikes with all four of his offerings. He generates his stuff with a quick arm and has a track record of performing against top competition. Though he's undersized at 5-foot-11, he has the potential to become a mid-rotation starter.

While Georges has long had size and arm strength, his path to being a high-level Draft prospect was anything but certain. As a sophomore at Clovis High School outside of Fresno, California, Georges blew out his elbow and required Tommy John surgery. He returned to the mound in time for league play as a junior in 2025, then put the injury further in his rearview by throwing at a myriad of summer showcase events, including Major League Baseball’s All-American Game, Perfect Game National and the Area Code Games.While Georges’ senior year has had some ups and downs, he still brings an ideal 6-foot-5 pitching frame to the mound with the chance to have a quality three-pitch mix with excellent metrics under the surface. He’ll throw his fastball in the 92-96 mph range; it can have carry and ride, and he also has a two-seamer with good sink. He’ll flash a plus low-80s slider that can miss bats, giving him an effective sinker-sweeper combination at times. He’s shown glimpses of a very solid 84-85 mph changeup with sink and fade he’ll throw to hitters on both sides of the plate.Georges hasn’t always thrown quality strikes this spring, and at times, things can snowball for him on the mound. He’ll also be 19 come Draft time, which might ding him in some teams’ models. Even so, his stuff and the data behind it could be enough to get teams interested in signing him away from his commitment to Texas Christian.

Morris likely first jumped on anyone’s radar back in 2023 when he played well, going 5-for-10, for USA Baseball’s 15U team that went a perfect 5-0 in an international friendly series held in the Dominican Republic. A teammate with players like potential top-of-the-Draft prospect Grady Emerson, Morris has continued to make strides in all aspects of his game, showing off skills at multiple positions along with some ability at the plate.Morris’ twitchy athleticism stands out wherever he is on the field, whether it’s in the right-handed batters box, behind the plate or at shortstop. He’s a hit-over-power type now, showing the ability to make consistent contact and shoot the gaps with line drives generated from a short, compact swing. He needs to add strength, but scouts have seen enough impact to think he’ll get to some more power in the future.The 6-foot Morris has seen time as a backstop and up the middle at short. Scouts might prefer him more as a middle infielder, with a solid to a tick-above-average arm and the ability to transfer the ball quickly and throw from all angles. He’s learned to slow the game down some this spring, making strides defensively at short from the summer. The Oklahoma State recruit moves well bend the dish, but a slightly undersized right-handed-hitting high school catcher is a tougher profile.

Servite High School in Anaheim has had close to two dozen players drafted over the years, though none since 2012 (Cole Irvin went on to the University of Oregon). Martin has a very good chance to end the drought after being a mainstay on the summer showcase circuit, then slashing .381/.548/.619 for USA Baseball’s 18U team in the WBSC U-18 Baseball World Cup Americas Qualifier in November.At times over the summer, Martin did look like one of the better high school prospects in the country on both sides of the ball, swinging the bat well at the start of the Area Code Games, for example. But his swing went backwards at times as his hands would get too deep and stuck in his load. There is bat speed, and he has a level path, so he could have an average bat in the future. His overall stock could be determined by how much impact he’ll have in terms of learning to turn on pitches and backspin balls, rather than just using the middle of the field.There are fewer questions about his defensive ability. While he’s an average runner, he has the chance to be an above-average to plus defender at shortstop to go along with a strong arm. He officially withdrew from the Draft in July, so he'll head to UCLA.

As a New York high schooler, DeCaro reclassified from the 2024 Draft to '23 and got an early jump on his college career at North Carolina. He looked like a potential 2026 first-rounder when he cracked the Tar Heels rotation as a 17-year-old freshman, but scouts aren't sure what to make of him because his stuff has leveled off since. His feel for pitching has allowed him to thrive in college and make the U.S. Collegiate National Team each of the last two summers.While DeCaro gets outs, he doesn't miss a lot of bats or land his secondary pitches in the zone consistently. His fastball has ordinary velocity (91-94 mph, peak of 97) and shape and relies on generating weak contact. His fading low-80s changeup has regressed a bit during his junior season and is now more of an average offering, as are his tight low-80s slider and upper-70s curveball.DeCaro keeps batters off balance by sequencing his pitches well and he throws consistent strikes from an upright delivery. Unless he improves the velocity and quality of his arsenal, he profiles more as a durable high-floor starter than someone who will pitch in the front half of a rotation. He's extremely young for a college junior (20 years, three months on Draft day) but doesn't have a lot of projection remaining in his strong 6-foot-5 frame.

A solid high school prospect from Dunedin, Florida, Beard began his college career in state at Florida Atlantic University, stepping right into the Owls rotation as a freshman. He took a big step forward as their sophomore ace in 2025, earning All American Athletic Conference First Team honors in the process before making a couple of Cape Cod League appearances and participating in USA Baseball’s Collegiate National Training Camp over the summer. A transfer to Florida State and the ACC could help his profile.Tall and lean, Beard is the quintessential pitchability college lefty with a strong feel for his craft and an unusual delivery to help it all play up. Everything comes from a very high slot that creates extension and deception. Velocity-wise, his fastball is fringy, sitting around 90-91 mph and topping out at 94, but he does a nice job of elevating it up and hitters chasing it out of the zone. His best pitch is his 75-77 mph changeup that gets “Bugs Bunny” comments from scouts, sold with outstanding arm speed. He has feel for spinning his breaking stuff with some depth, both a newer low-80s slider and a softer curve he’s had since his prep days, though they trail the cambio so he uses them a bit more strategically.Beard has made excellent strides with his ability to fill up the zone and limit walks. His ceiling might be a bit limited, but he’s reminded some of Guardians lefty Logan Allen, a second rounder out of Florida International in 2020, in terms of the path he could potentially follow.

A high school product from outside of Athens, Georgia, Jones began his college career at Stetson University, opening a lot of eyes by posting a .935 OPS with 23 steals and earning ASUN Conference Freshman of the Year honors. His move to the University of Florida as a sophomore was cut short after just four games by season-ending shoulder surgery. He was swinging the bat well upon his return as a redshirt sophomore with the Gators this spring, though a lack of impact, especially in SEC play, was somewhat hampering his Draft stock.A 6-foot-3 athletic right-handed hitter, Jones had been as advertised for much of the 2026 season. He has a solid approach at the plate with good bat-to-ball skills, walking nearly as often as striking out in his college career. While he hasn’t been driving the ball, being more of a gap-to-gap guy, there is some projection in his frame, and some think he could get to power, at least to his pull side, if he can learn to get behind the ball better. Jones is a premium athlete but wasn’t clocking in plus run times at the start of the spring, though he was showing more effort and stealing more bags as the season unfolded. He also has the ability to play a very good center field, especially when he’s going full throttle. Some of the energy-related issues could have been him just feeling himself out following last year’s left shoulder labrum surgery, and a strong finish to his SEC campaign could help push him up a bit on Draft boards.

For the second time in three years, Jackson Prep (Flowood, Miss.) has a potential 30-30 center fielder. Because of questions about his bat, he won't go ninth overall like Konnor Griffin did to the Pirates in 2024, but Roberts has one of the highest ceilings in the high school class. He also has one of the best bodies (6-foot-5, 215 pounds) and is one of the youngest players, not turning 18 until two weeks after the 2026 Draft.With his combination of bat speed and the strength and leverage in his huge frame, Roberts can produce tape-measure shots and high exit velocities. He has well-above-average raw power but did little damage against high-level competition on the showcase circuit last summer. He loses his timing at the plate, chases fastballs out of the zone and has to prove he can handle quality breaking pitches.A three-sport star who also has played forward in basketball and ran the 200-meter dash and long jumped in track, Roberts has plus speed and should be able to handle center field. The Florida recruit has been clocked at up to 94 mph on the mound, but he didn't pitch last summer after sustaining a shoulder injury and will focus on hitting going forward. His arm now is playing more as average in center, which is more than enough for the position.

Johnson's athleticism and potential to play shortstop could make him the first player drafted from a talented Oklahoma roster. A transfer from Wichita State, he's one of the few Sooners whose performance hasn't dropped precipitously in Southeastern Conference action. He's one of the twitchiest and fastest players in the college ranks.Johnson displays good feel for the barrel from the left side of the plate but chases too many non-fastballs out of the zone. He doesn't miss strikes and employs a line-drive approach. He has average raw power and is driving the ball more than ever, but he hasn't shown much pop with wood bats in summer leagues and may top out at 10-12 homers per season.A consistent plus-plus runner, Johnson can get from home to first in less than four seconds at his best and is an accomplished basestealer. He shows average arm strength at third base, where his fluid actions lead to the belief that he could handle shortstop in pro ball after seeing occasional action there for the Shockers. He also could become a dynamic center fielder or valuable super utilityman.

LeBlanc helped Louisiana State- Eunice win the Division II Junior College World Series in 2024 before batting .385/.488/.583 with 11 homers and 39 steals as a sophomore last spring. This year, he has been one of the most dangerous hitters in the Big 12 Conference and led Kansas to its first regular-season league championship since 1949, followed by its first conference tournament title since 2006. He lacks a standout tool but his high-level performance makes him one of the better college shortstops available.With a strong 6-foot, 200-pound frame and a simple right-handed swing geared to launch balls in the air, LeBlanc tied the Kansas single-season record when he hit his 21st homer at the Big 12 tournament, where he earned MVP honors. While most of his power plays to his pull side, he does have opposite-field pop as well and 20-homer upside at the next level. He has decent plate discipline and a knack for making contact, and he proved himself with wood bats in the Northwoods League last summer.LeBlanc's basestealing prowess results more from savvy than pure speed, as he's an average runner. His solid arm strength is his lone better-than-average tool and helps him make plays at shortstop, where he's a reliable if not flashy defender. He'll get a chance to show what he can do at short in pro ball but profiles better at second or third base.

The most underrated player in Georgia Tech's deep lineup, Kerce has modest tools but does a little bit of everything for the Yellow Jackets. He has improved throughout his college career, becoming one of the best contact hitters in NCAA Division I and proving he can handle shortstop this spring. He's a high-floor utility type who could fit in the top three or four rounds.Kerce employs a simple right-handed swing and disciplined approach to produce a lot of line drives and hard-hit grounders from gap to gap. His exit velocities have increased this year, though he makes a lot of groundball contact and rarely pulls balls in the air. That will probably cap his power ceiling around 10 homers per season, though he should provide his share of doubles. He ranked second in Division I with 28 doubles this spring entering NCAA tournament action.Kerce's instincts allow him to make the most of his average speed, though it plays up more in the field than on the bases. Primarily a third baseman during his first two college seasons, he's getting to plenty of balls at shortstop and making plays with solid arm strength. He has a chance to be an everyday middle infielder but profiles better in a utility role.

Scouts rave about Kozeal's hitting ability and high baseball IQ. He rarely swung and missed on the showcase circuit and didn't strike out in four starts while helping Team USA win the 18-and-under World Cup in September. None of his other tools grade as better than average, but his bat and makeup may make him the first Nebraska prepster to land in the first three rounds since Cole Stobbe in 2016. Gatorade's Nebraska player of the year as both a junior and senior, Kozeal consistently puts together quality at-bats and has an extremely advanced approach for a high schooler. He has a compact left-handed swing, controls the strike zone and uses the entire field. While he's a bit undersized, he has the strength and bat speed for 15-20-homer power and can drive the ball to the opposite field, as evidenced by a home run to left field when he stayed back on a changeup from Illinois right-hander Zander Mueth at the PDP League. Kozeal has some twitchy athleticism that helped him account for 26 touchdowns in eight games as Millard South's (Omaha) quarterback in the fall. The Vanderbilt recruit has fringy speed and average arm strength, yet he finds a way to make plays at shortstop. He'll move across the bag at the next level and profiles well as an offensive second baseman.

Murdock headed from the Utah high school ranks to UC San Diego, redshirting his freshman year and then spending his first real season on the mound in 2025 making eight Sunday starts and seven other relief outings, with mixed results. He was a Cape Cod League All-Star last summer, racking up eight saves as a closer for Orelans. He returned to the Tritons’ rotation as a redshirt sophomore this spring and has shown glimpses, creating a buzz by outpitching Santa Barbara’s Jackson Flora with an eight-inning, 17-strikeout masterpiece in early April, but then failed to build on that momentum in his next couple of outings.At his best, Murdock is a strong power pitcher who can maintain his stuff deep into starts, one who could pitch with two plus pitches at the next level. In that start against UCSB, he sat 95 mph and missed bats with the pitch, but while he’s touched as high as 99 mph, he typically sits around 92-94 and it gets squared up. The slider is typically a legit out pitch, an 84-85 mph breaker that gets a ton of swings-and-misses along with chases. There’s a mid-80s changeup in there, but he rarely goes to it, throwing his fastball-breaker almost all of the time. More often than not, Murdock’s lack of command (well over five walks per nine in his college career), not to mention relatively low extension and effort in his delivery, leads to thoughts of reliever risk. A team interested in making him just the second Tritons pitcher to go in the top five rounds in over 50 years, joining Astros prospect Ryan Forcucci, could send him out as a starter while remembering just how good he looked coming out of the pen on the Cape.

One of the more athletic and projectable pitchers in the 2026 high school crop, Putnam got a late start on the diamond this spring because he had to finish the basketball season first. His fastball touched 97 mph in his second outing, and while his breaking ball and control have been inconsistent, his arm talent is undeniable. He could take off once he focuses on baseball full-time and gets pro instruction, and his upside could land him in the top three rounds.When he's on, Putnam can work at 94-96 mph with armside run on his fastball, which has ranged from 88-98 during his senior season. He can flash some solid upper-70s sliders with quality depth, though his breaker lacks reliability and needs more power. His mid-80s changeup has nice fade and is a more dependable offering.Putnam has a quick arm and a 6-foot-5 frame with plenty of room to add strength, so his pitches should gain more velocity in the future. The Tennessee recruit moves down the mound very well, creating extension that allows his stuff to get on hitters quicker than they expect. He'll scatter his pitches at times but has the ingredients for at least average control as he gains more experience.

Santarelli emerged as one of the best power hitters in the 2026 high school class while topping Wisconsin prepsters with 11 homers in 31 games last spring (despite drawing 44 walks), leading St. Joseph (Kenosha) to the state Division 3 championship. He rose to the occasion on the showcase circuit as well, winning MVP honors at the MLB High School All-American Game, putting on a show during the Home Run Derby and during game action at the Under Armour Next All-America Game. He has the ceiling of a more athletic version of Kyle Schwarber.Physically imposing at 6-foot-2 and 230 pounds, Santarelli has a relatively compact left-handed swing that unleashes high exit velocities and tape-measure shots to all fields. Though he knows his plus-plus raw power means he doesn't have to sell out to crush home runs and he has the discipline to take walks when pitchers decline to challenge him, he's a streaky hitter who can lapse into strikeouts. He's still working on pitch recognition and maintaining a consistent stroke.Faster than most players his size, Santarelli can flash solid straight-line speed and plays as a fringy runner. His defense needs a lot of work, because he's rough at first base and has below-average arm strength. The Louisiana State recruit has the work ethic to improve his glove and throwing and could merit a look in left field.

Murdock headed from the Utah high school ranks to UC San Diego, redshirting his freshman year and then spending his first real season on the mound in 2025 making eight Sunday starts and seven other relief outings, with mixed results. He was a Cape Cod League All-Star last summer, racking up eight saves as a closer for Orelans. He returned to the Tritons’ rotation as a redshirt sophomore this spring and has shown glimpses of dominance, creating a buzz by outpitching Santa Barbara’s Jackson Flora with an eight-inning, 17-strikeout masterpiece in early April, but then failed to build on that momentum in his next couple of outings.At his best, Murdock is a strong power pitcher who can maintain his stuff deep into starts, one who could pitch with two plus pitches at the next level. In that start against UCSB, he sat 95 mph and missed bats with the pitch, but while he’s touched as high as 99 mph, he typically sits around 92-94 and it gets squared up. The slider is typically a legit out pitch, an 84-85 mph breaker that gets a ton of swings-and-misses along with chases. There’s a mid-80s changeup in there, but he rarely goes to it, throwing his fastball-breaker almost all of the time. More often than not, Murdock’s lack of command (well over five walks per nine in his college career), not to mention relatively low extension and effort in his delivery, leads to thoughts of reliever risk. A team interested in making him just the second Tritons pitcher to go in the top five rounds in over 50 years, joining Astros prospect Ryan Forcucci, could send him out as a starter while remembering just how good he looked coming out of the pen on the Cape.

Orange Lutheran has a well-deserved reputation as one of the best high school programs in not just Southern California, but the entire country. Gerrit Cole really put it on the map when he was a first-rounder in 2008 before going on to UCLA, but a pitcher hasn’t been taken from the school since the Rangers took Cole Winn in the first round of the 2018 Draft. In 2026, OLU has a pair of right-handers who might compete to be the first off the board in Cooper Sides and Morse, who threw at summer showcase events like Perfect Game National and the Area Code Games.Standing at 6-foot-8, Morse looks like a future workhorse-type starter. He relies mostly on a very good fastball-changeup combination right now, with a heater that’s up to 97 mph, sitting around 95 over the summer. His mid-80s changeup -- thrown with splitter-like action -- has tumble and depth and is a true bat-misser that could be a plus pitch in the future. His ability to spin is behind those two offerings. He’ll show a 75 mph curve without much action to it and a slightly harder three-quarter-type slider that lacks the necessary power. Committed to Tennessee, Morse does a solid job repeating his delivery and finding the strike zone consistently. He’s going to keep adding velocity, and if he can find new grips and cues for his breaking stuff to make them closer to average, he has the chance to fly off boards in the top two rounds and reach his ceiling as a mid-rotation starter.

Bishop Moore High School in the Orlando area has had one player get drafted to become a big leaguer, though long-time backup catcher Drew Butera went on to college first. Davis hopes to be the first from the program to be drafted at all since 2010, putting his name on the map at a number of MLB Develops events, including back-to-back Dream Series in 2024 and 2025, the 2025 Breakthrough Series and Hank Aaron Invitational, as well as the Area Code Games and MLB’s All-American Game. He’s gone on to establish himself as one of the more intriguing and most athletic prep hitters in Florida in this class.A left-handed hitter and thrower, Davis can do a lot of things well on the baseball field. He displays solid bat speed with an ability to get the bat through the zone quickly to make hard contact. While some evaluators are concerned about impact at the plate as he progresses, others have seen some ability to drive the ball with some sneaky raw power to his pull side thanks to a rotational swing. Davis is a plus runner who can use his speed to impact the game on both sides of the ball. He’s a basestealing threat and can go get the ball in center field, where he should be able to stay for a long time, with a chance to develop into a plus defender in time. He’ll head to Louisiana State for college ball if the Draft doesn’t work out.

As the spring high school season in Northern California approached, most scouts probably thought that shortstop Tyler Spangler would be the main attraction when they headed to De La Salle High School. While Spangler hasn’t played this season, Schlicht has made the trip worthwhile whenever he’s been on the mound. The big right-hander, who impressed with his feel for pitching at the Area Code Games last summer, has been exceptional during his senior year, not allowing a run over his first five starts to make teams seriously ponder whether he’s signable.At 6-foot-3 and 195 pounds, Schlicht screams projection while showcasing a very solid four-pitch mix. While there was hope he might have more of a velocity jump this spring, he was getting his fastball up to 94 mph early this season -- sitting more 90-91 as the year went on -- and the pitch plays because it has good finish coming from a low release point. He can really spin two breaking balls, both of which could be plus in time. His slider might have a slight edge with super high spin and good tilt to it over his 11-to-5 curve. He has solid feel for an above-average straight changeup.Schlicht is a solid athlete who gets down the mound well. He repeats his delivery consistently and fills up the strike zone with all of his offering while showing a huge competitive streak when he’s on the bump. He could be the type of prep arm a team with multiple picks makes a play at early, though there is a commitment to Stanford to contend with.

The younger brother of Chris McElvain, who pitched at Vanderbilt before signing with the Reds as an eighth-rounder last July, Ethan also has committed to the Commodores. But he may not make it to college because he has the best stuff and physicality among a deep crop of Tennessee high school left-handers. Matthew Dallas (Briarcrest Christian HS, Eads) entered the year with more acclaim, but McElvain passed him this spring. McElvain's fastball velocity has ticked up a bit during his senior season, and he's now operating at 91-93 mph and reaching 96. He can throw his heater with riding action at the letters or with downhill plane lower in the strike zone. His slider sits around 80 mph with two-plane break, and it can be a solid pitch when he doesn't overthrow it. McElvain has yet to show much feel for a changeup and almost never uses it. He has a strong 6-foot-4 frame but throws with some effort and can struggle to find a consistent release point, costing him control and command. He'll need time to add polish but could turn into a mid-rotation starter.

Sheerin broke into college baseball by winning Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference Rookie of the Year accolades and setting Mount St. Mary's records for strikeouts (109), strikeouts per nine innings (14.0) and opponent average (.197) in 2024. One of the top transfers on the portal market that year, he moved to Louisiana State but tore an anterior cruciate ligament while playing basketball that summer and missed all of 2025 following left knee surgery. He has returned this year throwing 3 mph harder than he did as a freshman and some teams rate him as the best reliever available.Sheerin's fastball now sits at 95-97 mph and tops out at 100, exploding on hitters with carry and armside run. He does a nice job of throwing his tight mid-80s slider for strikes, though it doesn't miss as many bats as it should. He'll flash a low-80s curveball with depth and an upper-80s splitter, but he relies almost exclusively on his heater and slide piece.Sheerin has a 6-foot-5 frame and gets down the mound well, creating good extension, and his low arm slot and crossfire delivery provide plenty of deception. With his mechanics and effort, he's definitely a reliever. If he can upgrade his slider and fringy control, he could be a late-inning option as a pro.

One of the better sophomore-eligible prospects in the 2026 Draft, Wentz offers power from both sides of the plate and has helped his cause by improving his conditioning and athleticism. But he also has flummoxed scouts with his inability to stop chasing or do much damage against breaking balls and changeups. Viewed as a potential second-round choice entering 2026, he may have to wait a couple of more rounds to hear his name called.Wentz's combination of bat speed and strength gives him at least plus raw power, and he could tap into more of it if he became more selective and pulled more balls in the air. More effective from the left side, he destroys fastballs but scuffles against everything else, and his aggressiveness leaves him especially vulnerable to changeups. He draws his share of walks because pitchers are reticent to challenge him, but he needs to tone down his approach.The 6-foot-2, 215-pound Wentz is moving better as sophomore and displaying fringy speed. He has split his college career between third base and second base, and his limited range and solid arm strength fit better at the hot corner. He's an erratic defender at both positions, so he may wind up on an outfield corner.

Hernandez registered on scouting radars as a Georgia prepster in 2024, but not enough for clubs to consider buying him away from Georgia Tech. He earned Atlantic Coast Conference freshman of the year accolades in 2025, starting games at six different spots (including one as a pitcher) and breaking Matt Wieters' freshman school record with 69 RBIs. Draft-eligible as a sophomore, he could factor in the top three rounds but also has struggled in ACC play and hasn't shown as much power this spring.Hernandez combines strength with good swing decisions to produce impressive exit velocities. But he also comes with some concerns at the plate, most notably his struggles to make contact against breaking balls. There's some arm bar in his right-handed stroke, which can get long and uphill, and he makes too much ground-ball contact while rarely pulling the ball.Though Hernandez has played a variety of positions, he's a below-average runner who's overmatched at second or third base. He's a fringy defender on the outfield corners and average at first. He does possess the arm strength for right field and pushed his fastball to 93 mph last year before concentrating on hitting as a sophomore.

Tiroly spent his first two years of college ball at Rider University in New Jersey, earning Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference Player of the Year honors as a sophomore. He entered the transfer portal and landed at Virginia, but a foot injury suffered at the end of the fall kept him from showing the Cavaliers or scouts checking him out in his new environment for the first time what he could do. A lack of prep time because of the injury, as well as not being 100 percent to start the spring led to a very slow start, but he was growing on evaluators as he heated up in ACC play.After having so much success in the MAAC, it was unclear how Tiroly would perform against a higher level of competition in a top conference. The right-handed hitter literally couldn’t put his best foot forward, not facing live pitching until opening weekend and wearing a boot between games. As he put the injury behind him, he started showing off a much better feel to hit, with more pop in conference play, answering that question about his ability to handle ACC pitching.Tiroly wasn’t a runner before the injury, so that part of his game wasn’t impacted in any real way. He’s a capable defender at both second and third, with enough arm for the hot corner. Some feel that might be his better position because of a lack of overall range, though playing third would put more pressure on him to keep showing an impact bat at the plate.

Ford grew up in the shadow of the University of Virginia and spent his first two years of high school in Charlottesville, but then moved to the Baylor School in Tennessee, where he was teammates with Henry Godbout. Ford followed Godbout back to Virginia and had a tremendous freshman season, finishing with 17 homers and a 1.006 OPS, enough to earn first-team freshman All-American honors from D1 Baseball and making him one of the more intriguing Draft-eligible sophomores in the class, thought he hasn’t been as productive power-wise in 2025.Ford is a big 6-foot-5 right-handed hitter whose value is tied almost entirely to his bat. It looked like it would play after getting to his pop right out of the gate, but he was having trouble lifting the ball as frequently as a sophomore. It’s mostly been to his pull side, but given that Virginia’s home park hasn’t always been conducive to power, it was an impressive debut. While he draws walks and makes a good amount of contact given his power potential, he does have a tendency to chase, especially softer stuff.Almost exclusively a first baseman as a freshman, Ford did start seeing some time in right field in this fall and has shown to be serviceable in right field this spring, which could make him a more interesting prospect if that works. He’s a below-average runner with a fringy arm, so he might be limited to that infield corner, but wherever he lands defensively, it’s his run-producing bat that will get him drafted early and continue the assembly line of hitters to come out of the Cavaliers' program.

The son of former big league outfielder and current Oklahoma associate head coach Reggie Willits and the older brother of 2025 No. 1 overall pick Eli Willits, Jaxon could give his family a third potential big leaguer. He doesn't have a true standout tool but his high baseball IQ allows him to get the most out of his physical ability. Hitting .318 with more walks than strikeouts during two seasons in the wood-bat Cape Cod League helps bolster his case for going in the first three rounds.A switch-hitter, Willits makes more contact and hits the ball with more authority from the left side of the plate. He recognizes pitches well, doesn't chase and makes regular contact to all fields. He has average raw power and doesn't lift balls consistently, so he won't hit more than 12-15 homers per season.Willits is an opportunistic baserunner with average speed and quickness. He's more of a dependable than especially rangy shortstop, which along with his average arm strength makes him better equipped for second base. He's a high-floor utility type who draws universal praise for his intelligence and work ethic.

A 20th-round pick out of a Virginia high school by the Guardians in 2023, Marohn pitched for the U.S. collegiate national team last summer and allowed just two earned runs in his first four starts this spring. He was building momentum toward the second round, but he missed his first start in March with biceps soreness and didn't pitch again after coming down with a forearm strain in mid-April. He didn't dominate as much in Atlantic Coast Conference action in between, further clouding his status.Marohn's fastball operates at 90-93 mph and peaks at 95, playing better than its velocity thanks to carry, command and deception. It works well with his best offering, a mid-80s changeup that induces a lot of chases with its tumble. His mid-70s curveball and low-80s slider flashed as solid pitches in 2025 but haven't been nearly as effective this year.Marohn's control has regressed as well, though he has continued to thrive while missing more bats than ever. He throws off hitters' timing with some crossfire in his delivery, funk in his arm action and wide angle. While his floor is more impressive than his ceiling, he could be a useful southpaw starter.

When Chris Pollard left Duke to take over as the head coach at the University of Virginia in the summer of 2025, seven players followed him via the transfer portal. The marquee name was first-round candidate AJ Gracia, but it also included Johnson, a two-way player with more upside on the mound. A strong fall had scouts in the area excited about what he might be able to do as a pitcher for the Cavaliers, but he was slowed out of the gate by what was termed a kinetic chain issue, and he didn’t make his first appearance until March 20. As he has throughout his college career, he put up inconsistent numbers on the bump.Despite a less-than-pretty statline, scouts are still bullish on Johnson as an athletic left-hander who has yet to focus on pitching full-time. He has as many as five pitches to work with, starting with a fastball that averaged a touch over 93 mph but topped out at 97-98 mph this spring, and it some ride to it when he’s at the top of the zone. His slider, thrown up to 83-84 mph, can be a 1-to-7, hammer-like breaker with a ton of swing and miss. He has a distinct upper-70s curve and can also throw a harder, 86-88 mph cutter. He used his low-80s changeup more than any other secondary pitch, throwing it downhill with armside sink and run, a cambio he’ll throw to hitters on both sides of the plate.Johnson has never been a consistent strike-thrower, carrying a career 4.6 BB/9 (5.1 per nine in 2026) rate into NCAA Regional play this year. But there are some raw ingredients to work with, and there were teams who felt he was worthy of top five round consideration, with perhaps another gear to reach when he puts the bat down for good.

Weinstein spent his first three seasons of high school at powerhouse Huntington Beach, a program that produced players like 2023 first-rounder Ralphy Velazquez and won a pair of National High School Invitational titles. Weinstein moved over to Orange Lutheran -- owner of four NHSI championships and Gerrit Cole's alma mater -- for his senior season so that he could get the chance to play shortstop.Weinstein has hit wherever he's been, whether it’s over the years as a mainstay in the Huntington Beach lineup, this spring for OLu or at showcase events like the Area Code Games and PG National. He has a nice, balanced left-handed swing geared for contact and an advanced approach at the plate, never giving away at-bats. He’s hit-over-power but he does have some ability to drive the ball in the air to his pull side.While the ultra-competitive Weinstein is a below-average runner, he’s shown he has very good range on the dirt. He's looked solid defensively at short this spring, with solid actions and enough arm where scouts feel he should be allowed to begin his pro career at the premium position. He might be best suited eventually as a hit-tool driven second baseman at the next level, but the biggest question surrounding the infielder is if he is signable away from his commitment to Louisiana State.

Though McCoy recorded a 7.11 ERA in his first two seasons at South Carolina, a brief but dominant stint in the Cape Cod League last summer left some evaluators wondering if he might be the best college left-hander in the 2026 Draft. He struck out 25 of the 54 Cape batters he faced while issuing just three walks in three starts. He has the stuff and metrics to back up that performance and will have to show that he can sustain it, but he won't get the chance to do so this year after blowing out his elbow in January and requiring Tommy John surgery.McCoy's fastball gained a couple of ticks of velocity on the Cape, parking at 94-95 mph and peaking at 98 with natural cutting action. He also threw a nasty mid-80s slider with plenty of sweep and mixed in some true cutters at 87-91 mph. He doesn't have much trust in a fading upper-80s changeup that he struggles to locate.McCoy's low three-quarters arm slot and resulting low release height create pitches with an unusual combination of velocity and shapes that are hard for hitters to deal with. He lacks size, has effort in his delivery and doesn't have much track record of success or strikes, so he might be more reliever than starter. He looked like he could pitch in the front half of a rotation while on the Cape, and he has the arsenal for a high-leverage role if he does wind up in the bullpen.

Nawrocki spent most of his junior season at Aledo (Texas) HS as a DH, helping the Bearcats win the state 5-A Division I title by batting .532 and winning championship game MVP honors by going 4-for-4. But he opened eyes with his pitching during the summer, including a dominant turn at the Area Code Games where he struck out five of the 12 hitters he faced. Though his listed height of 5-foot-11 is generous, and he's almost certainly a reliever, his pure stuff could land him in the top three rounds.Nawrocki may have the best slider in the Draft, an 82-85 mph beast that can top 3,200 rpm and can feature either tremendous depth or sweep, depending on what he wants. His four-seam fastball ranges from 91-95 mph with high spin rates that create quality carry. He doesn't use his firm 85-88 mph changeup much, but its fade and his ability to land it for strikes give it the potential to become at least an average offering.While he's small, Nawrocki has a strong, athletic frame. It's impossible to project him as a starter, but it he can throw consistent strikes and maintain the stuff he displayed during the summer, he could become a high-leverage lefty reliever. Clubs may let him prove he can do that in college and he's committed to Louisiana State.

An all-state pitcher, wide receiver and wrestler as a Missouri high schooler, LaPour flashed mid-90s fastballs but was so raw on the mound that he went undrafted and received minimal recruiting interest. He quickly added polish in college, cracking Wichita State's rotation as a freshman before transferring to Texas Christian for 2025, when he became the first Horned Frog to record six straight quality starts since Nick Lodolo in 2019. He's one of the most physical and hardest-throwing pitchers in the college class, though he missed two and a half months early in the season with elbow soreness.At his best, LaPour works at 95-98 mph and reaches 101 with heavy sink on his fastball, though it's not as dominant as its velocity might indicate because its shape and command are ordinary. His mid-80s slider can be a plus pitch with two-plane depth at its best, though it breaks so much that he struggles to land it for strikes and rarely uses it when behind in the count. His fading upper-80s changeup is a more reliable offering and he also has tinkered with an upper-80s cutter.Built like a linebacker, LaPour has a physical 6-foot-4 frame and has learned to repeat his simple delivery well enough to develop average control. His biggest proponents see a durable three-pitch starter who might have snuck into the first round with a healthy junior season. Other evaluators think he doesn't miss enough bats and may be better suited for the bullpen, where his bulldog mentality would fit nicely.

Rangel grew up pitching and playing quarterback with the assumption that he'd play two sports at the Colony (Texas) HS, where his father Rudy had been the head football coach for two decades. When it became apparent that his future was brighter on the mound, he gave up the gridiron after his freshman year. He has an electric arm but also comes with some reliever questions.Rangel achieves high spin rates on his two best pitches. His 93-96 mph fastball reaches 98 with carry and armside run, and his low-80s curveball combines power and depth at its best. He'll flash a tighter version of his breaking ball and potentially could develop a distinct slider with more velocity. His upper-80s kick changeup is too firm and rarely used, but it does show some sink and promise.Though he's very athletic, Rangel is smaller than most pitchers and lacks physicality. Add in a slingy delivery with some effort that provides deception but hampers his strike-throwing, and the Texas recruit may be better suited for the bullpen than the rotation. Scouts praise his intelligence and competitiveness, and he could become a high-leverage reliever if he's not a starter. Some teams may hold his age (19 years, three months on Draft day) against him, and he’ll be sophomore-eligible for the 2028 Draft if he makes it to college.

Hill has been a major contributor to Greenwich Country Day since his freshman year at the Connecticut high school. He threw well as an underclassman at events like the Area Code Games in 2024, then after a solid junior season, opened a lot of eyes early in the summer in a Cape Cod League workout game at Fenway Park. That was it in terms of his summer work, however, as he didn’t pitch at the East Coast Professional Showcase or return to the Area Code Games as an incoming senior.At 6-foot-5, Hill is a projectable prep right-hander with a potential four-pitch mix. He can consistently pump his fastball up to 95-96 mph with some sink, with more in the tank to come. His best secondary offering is his hard slider that can miss bats, but he showed at Fenway he also has a distinct curve with big downer action. He can also drop in a solid changeup with some tail, a cambio he turns over well. Committed to Stanford, Hill’s combination of size and pitch mix point to a future starting pitcher at the next level, especially as his command improves. Because he wasn’t seen more over the summer, scouts will be eager for things to warm up in Connecticut and see if Hill can join the likes of Hiro Wyatt and Frank Mozzicato (both of whom were drafted by the Royals) as recent Nutmeggers to go in the top three rounds.

Lascelles has been establishing himself as the top Canadian position player in the 2026 Draft class for some time now, playing for the Toronto Mets in the Canadian Premier Baseball League and the Canadian Junior National Team, while also displaying his tools at premier events like the Canadian Futures Showcase last fall and Prep Baseball Report’s Super 60 this past winter. But he really gave his Draft stock a boost with visits to both Arizona and Florida this spring, facing almost entirely professional pitchers.The left-handed swinging Lascelles has pretty much hit wherever he’s been. This spring was no different, when he hit a combined .389 with four extra-base hits in 54 at-bats against Minor League arms. It’s a little bit of an unorthodox setup at the plate, with his elbows far apart and a steep bat path when he swings, but he routinely sends line drives up the middle with a left- to-right-center field approach. He doesn’t always get into his legs, leading to some questions about how much power he’ll have.Lascelles also runs track for his high school and he’s a borderline plus runner who knows how to steal a base. The jury is out whether he can stick at shortstop long term, with work to be done on his first step and range and an average-ish arm that might be better suited for second base. It’s the bat that teams will be buying if they take him early enough to sign him away from his commitment to Yale.

A legitimate two-way player from the New Jersey high school ranks, Weingartner didn’t register all that much on the summer showcase circuit, playing sparingly at the East Coast Pro Showcase and both pitching and hitting some at the Area Code Games. He started generating some buzz this winter when he broke the record with a 6.24-second 60-yard dash at the Prep Baseball Report Super 60 and continued to build a case for himself, more as a position player than a pitcher, as the top prep player in the Garden State this spring.While he can crank his fastball up to 94-96 mph on the mound at times, it’s Weingartner’s athleticism and tools as a position player that was garnering more attention this spring. He can put on a show in batting practice, with clear raw power from the right side of the plate. He’s shown some feel in games, but there is some length to what looks like a bit of a manufactured swing that some evaluators think might struggle with premium velocity from professional pitching.It’s obvious that Weingartner has plus speed, and the arm that can touch mid-90s as a pitcher works from shortstop. He could be the type of high school athlete who might need a couple of summers in a complex league, and it remains to be seen if a team will be willing to spend to keep him from honoring his commitment to Penn State.

Amalbert really jumped on the Draft scene with a huge showing at last summer’s Area Code Games, when several evaluators singled him out as the best hitter at the event. He started cutting his teeth before that at MLB Develops events like the Hank Aaron Invitational, in 2023 and 2024, as well as the 2025 Dream Series. His offensive potential and tools were getting plenty of attention back home in New Jersey, though his performance wasn’t as consistently electric as it was over the summer.Amalbert’s plus bat speed stands out from the right side of the plate. It’s a swing that some long-time scouts in the Northeast say reminds them of the high school version of Manny Ramirez. And at times, Amalbert looks like he could be an outstanding hitter with elite exit velocities, one who will impress with blast numbers in workouts. Then there are times he struggles with breaking stuff and finding the barrel, like he did over his first several games this spring. When he’s locked in, he shows the potential to be an above-average hitter with above-average power.A plus runner, Amalbert didn’t appear to have a true position. Initially a shortstop, that didn’t really fit, but it seems like he’s found a home in center field, where he’s shown pretty good instincts to go along with that speed. After the Area Codes, scouts may have pegged him to easily be a third-rounder, and he’s going to be crosschecked enough for teams to have a better feel whether his tools are worth trying to draft away from his commitment to Oklahoma.

After going just 4-for-22 as a George Mason freshman in 2024, Hull slashed .367/.474/.557 and ranked fourth in NCAA Division I in runs (83) and 10th in steals (42) last spring. He has posted similar numbers at the plate after transferring to North Carolina and batted .432/.504/.658 in Atlantic Coast Conference play. At 6-foot-4 and 215 pounds, he offers one of the best combinations of physicality and athleticism in the college ranks.The complicating factor with Hull is that scouts don't like his unorthodox left-handed swing, which can get rigid and features so-so bat speed. He controls the strike zone well and makes contact with ease, but he also hits a lot of balls on the ground. He did start to hit for more power later in the season and has enough raw pop to produce 15-20 homers annually as a pro if he can make adjustments.An average to solid runner out of the batter's box, Hull can flash well-above-average speed once he gets going and is a threat to steal. A right fielder at George Mason, he has shifted to center field this spring. He's still learning the position but has the quickness to stay in center, and his fringy arm is playable there.

The best player to ever come out of Butler Area High School north of Pittsburgh is right-hander Matt Clement, a 1993 third-rounder who spent parts of nine years in the big leagues, but there hasn’t been a player drafted from the program since 2003. This year, the Golden Tornado have a pair of right-handed pitchers who have intrigued scouts. The scouting industry might have had Nolan Stefaniak ahead of Casteel entering the spring, but that’s likely flipped as the latter has made some big strides this spring.Casteel’s combination of size, projection and improved pitchability had teams running to western Pennsylvania to get more looks at him.The 6-foot-4 hurler typically sits around 92 mph and tops out at 95 mph, but he can maintain the low-90s velocity deep into starts, and it’s easy to dream about more to come as he fills out his frame. He has a slider that has the chance to be a true out pitch, mostly with three-quarters shape, depth and bite, though he can manipulate it into more of a slurve or a sweeper as he needs it. He doesn’t use his changeup much, but it has the chance to be a solid third offering with some fade.In addition to Casteel’s stuff ticking up, he also showed a better feel for finding the zone, with solid command and control compared to looks from the fall. The feel for pitching and pure arm strength point to some ceiling here, with some evaluators talking about the West Virginia recruit in the top five rounds.

Harris entered the start of the summer showcase circuit last year considered one of the best high school position players in the country. And he showed off glimpses of his considerable tools at nearly every event possible, including the Perfect Game National, both PG’s and MLB’s High School All-American Games, the East Coast Pro Showcase and the Area Code Game. With his skills, however, came some questions about his hit tool. When the Jacksonville, Florida prep standout continued to have swing-and-miss issues during his senior year, his Draft stock took a hit, leaving his status up in the air.At 6-foot-2 and hitting from the right side, he’s reminded some area scouts in Florida of a young Dylan Crews, who played his high school ball in Florida before heading to LSU. There is definite raw power in his game, and he can get to it at times when he keeps his barrel in the zone for a long time and creates leverage. But he has struggled to find consistency at the plate, with strikeouts still plaguing him this spring, especially having difficulty connecting against breaking stuff.Harris does have above-average speed and uses it well on both sides of the ball. When he combines it with his outstanding instincts in center field, he looks like a potentially plus defender up the middle in the future. The arm would play in a corner, and he might have the raw power to profile there if need be, but it might not be until he spends some years in the University of Florida lineup before he moves on to pro ball.

When Sosa first entered Viera HS (Fla.), he was around 5-foot-5 and weighed maybe 160 pounds, not exactly the kind of physique that screams future catching prospect. Four years and close to 7 inches and 40 pounds later, Sosa’s name has been steadily rising up Draft boards. He really put himself on the map during summer showcase circuit events like Perfect Game National, East Coast Pro and the Baseball Factory All-America GameScouts love the combination of Sosa’s strong body, offensive potential, chance to stick behind the plate and blue-collar approach to the game. Teams love left-handed-hitting catchers, and Sosa has the chance to be a good one, showing an ability to control the zone well, drawing a ton of walks and rarely trying to do too much. He has a simple and repeatable swing and can handle righties and lefties. It’s more of a line-drive approach right now, but there’s solid raw power for him to tap into in the future.Sosa does show a little stiffness with too wide of a base behind the plate. That said, he does a decent job receiving and blocking, with the agility to improve on that front, and his arm is solid and accurate. The high school catching demographic is a risky one, but Sosa could be enticing enough to draft and sign away from his commitment to North Carolina State.

One of the biggest wild cards in the Draft, Jelkin got dismissed from Nebraska as a freshman in 2022 and teams are still wary of his makeup. He spent 2023 at South Mountain (Ariz.) CC before blowing out his elbow at Houston the following spring, requiring Tommy John surgery that sidelined him for all of last season at Kentucky. He turned down the Dodgers as a 14th-rounder in 2023 and the Mets as a ninth-rounder in '24. Though he'll be 23 years and seven months old on Draft Day, he also has top two rounds stuff and earned second-team All-Southeastern Conference honors as a redshirt senior.Jelkin operates with three solid pitches with high spin rates, the best of which is a low-80s curveball with quality depth. His fastball has sat at 93-96 mph and reached 98 with power sink this spring, and it touched 100 during fall practice. His upper-80s cutter features late break that keeps it off barrels.Jelkin also shows some aptitude for a mid-80s changeup with fade and sink. A lanky 6-foot-5, 195-pounder with room to add more strength, he slings his pitches from a low three-quarters slot that adds deception. While he provides consistent strikes, he does catch the heart of the plate too often and will need to upgrade his command.

Morton damaged tendons in his throwing wrist while hitting fungoes as a Minnesota high school senior in 2023, so he made just two starts at the beginning of that season -- and reached 97 mph with his fastball and 91 with his cutter. He turned down a lucrative offer from the Brewers in the 19th around to attend Texas A&M, where he worked just 19 1/3 innings in two years while battling the strike zone and an oblique injury. After transferring back home, he became Minnesota's best pitching prospect since 2020 No. 3 overall pick Max Meyer before blowing out his elbow in his final regular-season start and requiring Tommy John surgery.Morton generates premium stuff from a sidearm slot and low release height, creating plenty of run and sink on a fastball that operates at 93-95 mph and touches 97. He produces premium spin rates on a mid-80s slider with excessive sweep and a cutter that parks in the low 90s. His firm upper-80s changeup features some fade but doesn't fool hitters.While Morton displayed much better control than he did with the Aggies, he caught the heart of the plate too often. None of his offerings elicit many chases because he throws so many strikes, and only his slider qualifies as a swing-and-miss pitch (while his sinker is valuable because it generates so much groundball contact). He doesn't have a weapon to get lefties out, though better command could help.

Like his older brother Connor before him, Ethan Norby is an undersized star at East Carolina who draws rave reviews for his competitiveness. While Connor was a sweet-swinging infielder who went to the Orioles in 2021's second round en route to the big leagues, Ethan is a left-hander with one of the better sliders in college baseball. He didn't quite live up to expectations this spring but still could factor into the top three or four rounds.Norby's high-spin 79-82 mph slider features plenty of sweep and depth, and it was outstanding when he struck out a Pirates-record 19 against eventual College World Series runner-up Coastal Carolina last March, though it can get roughed up when it catches too much of the plate. He's throwing a tick harder this year, parking at 91-92 mph and topping out at 95 with his fastball, but it isn't missing as many bats with its carry as it has in the past. He's struggling to land his tumbling mid-80s changeup for strikes, while his mid-80s cutter lags behind the rest of his repertoire.The 5-foot-10 Norby leans into his lack of size by getting down the mound and using a three-quarters arm slot to create a low release height and good shape on his pitches. His slingy delivery and short arm action add to his deception without compromising his control or fastball command, though he hasn't been quite as sharp as he was as a sophomore. He profiles best as a slider-happy reliever but his aptitude and makeup could make him a back-of-the-rotation starter.

Jorgensen tore the ACL in his right knee during a baseball workout, leading to surgery in February 2025 that sidelined him for his junior season at Jesuit Prep (Dallas) as well as the summer showcase circuit. He returned with a strong inning at the World Wood Bat Association World Championship in October and has been unhittable for much of this spring. He's the grandson of Mike Jorgensen, who spent 17 years as a first baseman in the big leagues before a long post-playing career with the Cardinals that included nine years as farm director and a partial season as interim manager.Jorgensen creates plenty of backspin on his fastball, which sits at 92-94 mph and touches 97 with bat-missing carry. He also generates high spin rates on his upper-70s curveball, which features plenty of downer break. His low-80s changeup with splitter action gives him a solid third pitch, and some scouts believe it's his best offering.With his fastball characteristics and strong 6-foot frame, Jorgensen draws repeated comparisons to Logan Henderson. The Texas recruit gets down the mound well and produces a surprising amount of extension for his size from a high arm slot. Some evaluators worry about his lack of physicality and projection as well as the effort in his delivery, which results in inconsistent control.

Coming out of the Queens, New York high school ranks, Montesa began his college career with Division II Adelphi in his home state. He earned Northeast 10 Conference Pitcher of the Year honors as a sophomore, catching the attention of Division I programs. The right-hander transferred to West Virginia for his junior season and opened a lot of eyes early in the season to give himself some helium. He wasn’t able to maintain it consistently, especially in Big 12 play, but while he lost his rotation spot late in the year, scouts still liked his pure stuff enough to keep him in Day 2 conversations.Montesa is an athletic 6-foot-1 right-hander who has a four-pitch mix that could give him the chance to start at the next level. His fastball sits in the mid-90s and can touch 98 at times, and it’s a tough pitch to barrel -- with a decent amount of swing-and-miss -- when he’s up in the zone. Command of the pitch was iffy at best, and the heater can get hit when he’s down in the zone with it too much. He does have a very good feel for spin for both his preferred upper-70s curve that he can manipulate the shape of when he’s locked in and his lesser-thrown harder and shorter mid-80s slider, both of which miss plenty of bats. There is a firm upper-80s changeup in there as well, though it’s clearly his fourth pitch.The biggest thing holding Montesa back is his command. His walk rate climbed as the 2026 season wore on, and his 5.98 BB/9 rate in Big 12 play undoubtedly contributed to him being taken out of the rotation as postseason play loomed, though his good raw stuff and age (he’s still only 20 years old come Draft time) were enough for teams to be discussing in the top several rounds.

American Heritage HS in Plantation, Fla., has produced a good amount of big league talent, from Eric Hosmer to Mark Vientos. But all have been hitters except for one: right-hander Shaun Anderson, who went on to the University of Florida before emerging as a third-round pick in 2016. Snow hopes to be the first pitcher from the program to be drafted since lefty Brandon Barriera was a first-rounder in '22 and just the fourth arm to be taken from the school since Anderson was a late-round pick who didn’t sign back in '13.Snow is a slightly undersized right-hander who gives off some Sonny Gray vibes in terms of his body type. After missing some time earlier this spring with an oblique issue, he came back throwing extremely well. He’s been up to 97-98 mph as his senior season wore on, showing he can maintain his velocity deep into starts. He backs it up with what he might call two different breaking balls, but is really an effective 78-80 mph power slurve, one with tight three-quarters shape with late finish when he’s landing it well. He has feel for a firm 85-87 mph changeup that can have depth and fade, but he doesn’t throw it much in games.Because of his size and his occasionally spotty command, there are some reliever risk concerns that might cap his Draft stock a bit. Some scouts put his ceiling at around the third round, and it might take him being selected there -- or paid at that level when taken a bit later -- to keep him from following in Anderson’s footsteps to pitch for the Gators in college.

Harvard-Westlake High School in southern California has produced high-end Draft talent, from first-round quality arms like Lucas Giolito, Max Fried and Jack Flaherty to exciting hitters like Pete Crow-Armstrong and most recently, 2024 first-rounder Bryce Rainer. This year’s entry is Tronstein, whose Draft stock was up in the air after he scuffled some on the summer showcase circuit but turned things around this spring, particularly in front of a lot of eyes at USA Baseball’s National High School Invitational.An athletic 5-foot-11 right-handed bat, Tronstein is an aggressive hitter who is looking to do damage at all times. He’s able to hit the ball very hard, like he did with some elite exit velocities at NHSI, and can drive the ball to all fields with some sneaky pop. He had a tendency to chase too much out of the zone over the summer, leading to some swing-and-miss concerns, and he has a penchant for lunging against soft stuff too much, something that will have to be addressed at the next level.Tronstein is an above-average runner, plus underway, and his speed can help him on both sides of the ball. He’s played both shortstop and the outfield, and while he has an average arm, scouts tend to like him better in center, where his athleticism plays well, because his arm might be a bit shy for plays in the hole. The biggest hurdle for teams considering Tronstein might be his commitment to Vanderbilt.

When most baseball fans think about Notre Dame High School talent in recent years, Hunter Greene (2017) comes to mind, with Giancarlo Stanton ten years earlier. The program hasn’t had a position player taken in the top 10 rounds since 2009, but Madrid hopes to buck that trend this July. The catcher wasn’t seen all that much on the summer showcase circuit but was impressing both area scouts and cross-checkers during his senior year, particularly with his work behind the dish.Madrid’s calling card is his defense. His abilities behind the plate give him a very high floor, with some evaluators claiming he has the best arm of any young catcher they’ve seen this year. It’s not just pure arm strength, as he also has a quick release and is very accurate controlling the running game. His overall defensive package, including receiving and blocking, is quite advanced.His ultimate ceiling will be determined by how much he hits. More than one scout gave a report that included a version of, “if he hits at all, he’s a big leaguer.” The Oregon recruit does have some raw power that he’s gotten to in games at times in front of evaluators, and a team that saw enough of that might look past the typically tough right-right high school catching profile to sign him away from his college commitment.

Because Jasa was so raw when he arrived at Nebraska as a Colorado high school recruit, Cornhuskers coaches not only redshirted him in 2024 but also wouldn't allow him to work against teammates in practice for fear he might injure them with an errant pitch. After posting an 8.68 ERA with 19 walks in 18 2/3 innings in his college debut last year, he has taken a huge step forward in 2026. He emerged as the ace of a team that earned Nebraska's first NCAA regional host bid in 18 years and displayed the best arm in the program since Joba Chamberlain two decades ago.Jasa averages 96 mph and reaches 98 with his fastball, but it lacks life and command and tends to get hit harder than would be expected, so he has used it just 28 percent of the time this spring. He leans much more on a tight upper-80s slider that tops out at 91 and plays well off his best pitch, a downer 78-82 mph curveball. Though he doesn't employ his low-80s changeup very often and has difficulty landing it for strikes, it shows promising tumble and fade at its best.Jasa has much better feel for his breaking balls than his dead-zone fastball, and while his control has improved, it's still below average. That means he comes with a good deal of reliever risk, though he also has huge upside as a starter if a team can help him improve his ability to shape and locate his heater. He's athletic and has improved tremendously in college, leading to hope he can continue to do so at the next level.

Nevada's best prep pitching prospect in 2023, Mercurius spent his first two college seasons at Nevada-Las Vegas before transferring to Oklahoma to reunite with his brother Xander, the Silver State's top high school arm in 2025. L.J. can look like a second-round talent at his best and opened this spring by allowing one earned run in four non-conference starts. After getting rocked by Southeastern Conference hitters, he fits more in the fourth round.Mercurius operates with a 92-94 mph fastball that peaks at 97 with improved carry but not much running action, so it gets in trouble when he doesn't locate it well. The same is true with his mid-80s changeup, a plus offering with fade and sink that was victimized for five homers in his first seven SEC starts. He relies primarily on those two pitches while mixing in some tight low-80s sliders.The Sooners have raised Mercurius' arm slot to get more backspin on his fastball, similar to what they did with first-rounder Kyson Witherspoon in 2025. He's providing more strikes in his second year as a college starter than he did in his first but his ability to improve his command will determine his future success. If he can't, he'll be more middle reliever than No. 4 starter.

A product of the Oregon high school ranks, Molony stayed in state for college ball. He made an immediate impact at University of Oregon, earning freshman All-American and first-team all-Pac 12 honors after finishing with a .969 OPS. He duplicated that OPS effort as a sophomore, finishing with 15 homers and 10 steals. A rough stint in the Cape Cod League didn’t help his overall profile, but he’s still the best prospect in the Pacific Northwest.While he’s not in the same category as Aiva Arquette, the Oregon State shortstop who was the No. 7 overall pick in the 2025 Draft, Molony has plenty of attributes to like. The right-handed hitter produces plenty of bat speed coming from a strong and athletic 6-foot-2 frame. The power continues to come, especially to his pull side, though his inability to get to it with a wood bat on the Cape concerns some scouts. He rarely swings and misses at in-zone offerings, but not everyone is sold on his pure hit tool to get to that power.Most evaluators give Molony the chance to stick at shortstop. He’s average, maybe a tick above, as a runner, but he has solid range, good hands and more than enough arm for the premium position. He’s unlikely to reach Arquette levels come Draft time, but a strong junior season with the Ducks could vault him into first-round conversations.

A Tucson native who attended Suaharita HS south of the Arizona city, Kramkowski stayed put to play for the University of Arizona in college. He pitched sparingly as a freshman in 2024, then spent his sophomore year in the Wildcats’ weekend rotation. There was some buzz this fall as his velocity ticked upward, but he hasn’t maintained the better stuff as Arizona’s Friday starter during his junior year this spring.With his tall, thin frame and glimpses of an easy fastball, Kramkowski has drawn some comps to T.J. Nichols, a former Arizona hurler and current Rays prospect. The 6-foot-3 right-hander relies mostly on his sinking fastball-slider combination. It’s a bowling ball heater that was up to 98 mph this fall, but was back around the 92-93 range he threw it in as a starter in 2025, and it does elicit very high ground-ball rates. He backs it up with an upper-70s slider that has decent three-quarter traditional tilt to it. He started going to his average mid-80s cutter more frequently this spring. Kramkowski threw a ton of strikes last year, walking only 1.8 per nine. He repeated that in 2026, but it’s control over command, and at times, he can get too much of the plate and get hit. Teams might pick apart his lack of a put-away heater or his lack of secondary pitch metrics, but he has been posting every week and the sinker-slider combination gives him some building blocks to work with on the pro side.

Sandefer’s college passport has been well-stamped in Florida. He began his post-prep career at Division II St. Leo before moving to Central Florida and pitching in a bit of a swing role as a sophomore. He hit the transfer portal again and headed to the University of Florida, presumably for the chance to start. A minor injury had him working out of the pen to start the year and he didn’t look comfortable, though he got in a groove over a couple of mid-week starts to earn a spot in the weekend rotation. That’s been an up-and-down experience, especially in SEC play, and he seemed to dodge a bullet when x-rays came back negative after a comebacker hit him on his pitching arm during the conference tournament.While not the biggest right-hander in the world at 6-foot-1, Sandefer’s stuff could give him the chance to start. He’s seen his fastball tick upwards a bit this year, now sitting over 93 mph and touching 98 mph, and he backs it up with an interesting array of secondary offerings. He can miss bats with his low-80s slider with high spin, a breaker that can be a super-wide sweeper with angle, though it’s inconsistent at times. He sells his low-80s changeup well with his arm speed and it features good fade and depth. He mixes in a cutter at times as well.All of Sandefer’s repertoire comes from a really whippy arm and a low slot, with movement to everything he throws, and he’s generally around the plate. His inconsistencies in conference play might have halted his helium a bit, but a team taking him in the top several rounds could send him out as a starter with the option to shorten him up for a life in the bullpen if that role doesn’t stick.

Brother Rice High School in Michigan has produced three big leaguers. One, Nick Plummer, signed out of the prep ranks as a first-rounder in 2015. The other two, pitcher Karl Kauffman and its best alum, DJ LeMahieu, went on to college before re-emerging in the Draft. Dowd is following the path of those two, first spending two productive years at Southern California before transferring to Florida State for his junior season, during which he slowed down as ACC play progressed.Dowd is more of a “sum of his parts” kind of outfielder rather than one with loud tools. Scouts do like the way he can handle the bat from the left side of the plate. He repeats his swing well, making a ton of contact and spraying the ball around the whole field. He might never be a huge power guy, but he will take his chances when the opportunity arises, showing the ability to get the ball in the air when he does. He controls the zone very well, drawing more walks than strikeouts, giving him a possible table-setting role in the future.While he’s not a burner, Dowd is very smart on the basepaths, showing the ability to take extra bags, even if he’s never a threat to steal. While patrolling the grass, he’s displayed the ability to get good jumps in center field and has shown he can handle the corners as well. Some see him as a bit of a tweener profile-wise, one who might be more of a fourth outfielder, but his high floor and plus makeup are assets.

From the minute he arrived on campus, Hansen has been a member of the UC Irvine weekend rotation, making 16 starts as a freshman and 16 more as a sophomore in 2025 en route to earning First-Team All Big West honors. He was the Anteaters’ Friday night starter once again this season, with mixed results, but his consistency and durability have him in position to join Andre Pallante (2019 fourth round) and Riley Kelly (2025 fourth round) as Irvine pitchers taken recently in the top several rounds.With a strong 6-foot-2 frame, there's nothing glamorous about how Hansen gets things done on the mound, but he has a legitimate three-pitch mix with an idea of how to use it and the ability to routinely go deep into starts. His fastball is generally in the 90-95 mph range, though he was showing a tick more velocity this year sitting 93-94. He commands it well and has shown he can get to more later in games, giving some hope there could be more consistent mph to come.
