He has an above-average 81-84 mph curve, and his 84-85 mph changeup flashes plus; combined, the two secondary offerings missed bats at a 39-percent clip in 2026.Hansen is around the zone with all of his offerings, but while his compact arm action adds deception, hiding the ball well, some scouts don’t love his below-average extension. There is strong consensus that he has plus makeup and a nasty competitive streak, which could give him the chance to start long-term, albeit with a more limited ceiling.

The TNXL Academy was formed in 2014 to provide baseball players in Florida an alternative high school experience that would provide more instruction and better competition. Since then, the program has produced 10 draftees, though none have made it to the big leagues to date. There are a pair of arms at TNXL this year getting plenty of attention in lefty Spencer Evans and Brady, who moved from the Buffalo, New York, area to train in Florida and raise his Draft profile considerably.Brady offers excellent size at 6-foot-5 and projection, showing off a four-pitch mix at summer showcase events like Perfect Game National, the East Coast Pro Showcase and the Area Code Games, before putting together a solid senior season. The fastball is average right now, though he commands the heater he can get up to 95 mph well. He has both a low-80s slider with good angle and an upper-70s curve that’s improved. Some scouts like his changeup better and he has very good feel for his 85-mph cambio.The big right-hander uses a cross-fire delivery that creates some deception and adds some quality to his breaking ball. He moves easily down the mound and fills up the strike zone, with upside to all of his offerings and a good chance to be a starter long-term. Teams also like that he’ll still be just 17 years old come Draft time, though they’ll have to contend with his Louisiana State commitment.

Part of a Blair Oaks (Wardsville, Mo.) program that spawned Tom Henke, Libbert impressed on the showcase circuit last summer and consistently displayed quality stuff as a senior. He climbed Draft boards all spring and looked headed for the top three rounds despite his commitment to Missouri. He struck out 18 in six innings during his final start in early May, but he injured his elbow after slipping on artificial turf while making a throw and required Tommy John surgery.Libbert regularly works with a low-90s fastball that reaches 97 mph and plays better than its velocity because of its significant carry up in the zone. He has precocious feel for a low-80s changeup that can be a plus pitch at its best. He also has aptitude for spinning the ball, though his upper-70s curveball and slider with similar velocity can blend together at times. Libbert's 6-foot-1, 205-pound frame is more strong than projectable, but he already has the makings of three solid or better offerings. Though there's some effort in his delivery, it's also deceptive and doesn't prevent him from throwing strikes. He repeats his mechanics well and stands out with his competitiveness.

Because the Iowa high school season runs deep into July, LaFavor didn't get seen on the showcase circuit last summer. He has intrigued some evaluators this spring with his ultraprojectable 6-foot-7 frame and delivery metrics, and he'll get scouted more heavily in June and July. There are some parallels to Jacob Misiorowski at the same stage of their Midwest prep careers, though no one is predicting that LaFavor will explode to that extent.LaFavor's fastball can touch 95 mph early in games before settling in at 90-93 with some armside run and carry. His low-80s slider features sweeper action, and he also will turn it into an upper-80s cutter at times. He shows some aptitude for killing spin on an upper-70s changeup with fade.LaFavor's size and ability to get down the mound creates 7 feet plus of extension in his delivery, while his low arm slot produces wide angle and a low release height. Though the Iowa recruit's mechanics create deception, some scouts have concerns about his long arm action, effort and crossfire. He's still growing into his body and needs more strength so he can do a better job of repeating his delivery and locating his pitches, though he's more polished than Misiorowski was as a high school senior.

Thiel is one of the more projectable and most improved high school pitchers in the Midwest. The 6-foot-7 left-hander topped out at 88 mph during East Coast Pro tryout last summer and failed to make the team, but he has added about 5 mph to his fastball this spring. While many scouts believe he'd be best served by attending Ohio State and adding more strength and polish, there are enough interested teams that he may not get to Columbus.Thiel now reaches 94 mph early in games and operates at 89-91 mph throughout his starts, imparting carry and armside run on his heater. He gets good depth on a curveball that reaches the upper 70s and owns a slider with more power and horizontal action but less consistency. He also demonstrates feel for a changeup that parks around 80 mph and drops at the plate.After gaining significant strength during the last year, Thiel now carries 230 pounds on his tall frame and still has room to add more. He uses his size to create extension and angle that add deception. He throws more strikes than most teenage southpaws his size and projects to develop average control and command.

Torrey Pines High School in Southern California has had a dozen players get drafted over the years, with four eventually touching the big leagues. Two of them have been there this year in Carson Williams and Kyle Hurt. Jutronich has the chance to be the first left-handed pitcher ever selected from the school, and his combination of ceiling and athleticism had scouts touting him as a potential top-three-round pick, though missing two months of his senior season with an arm issue has halted his momentum.At 6-foot-4, 190 pounds, Jutronich screams projection from the left side. His fastball is below-average right now -- he’ll pitch at 89-90 mph, topping out at 92-93 -- but there’s strong conviction there will be more velocity to come when he adds strength to his frame. He has good feel to spin, with a slider that’s a bit ahead of his slower curve. He also exhibits feel for a changeup.Jutronich’s athleticism adds to an ease of operation on the mound, and he tends to be a reliable strike-thrower. He did make it back for two outings post-injury, with mixed results, but it helped assuage any health-related fears teams might have had. He’s committed to go to UC Santa Barbara, which has a very good reputation for developing pitching -- think Shane Bieber, Michael McGreevy, last year’s No. 2 pick Tyler Bremner or this year’s top pitching prospect, Jackson Flora -- so seeing Jutronich emerge in three years as a first-rounder might be in the back of some evaluators’ minds.

The state of Georgia doesn't have its usual first-round high school pitcher in 2023, but the state's best mound prospect is extremely polished for his age and could factor into the top three rounds if he's signable. McNeillie hails from a Milton HS program that has spawned three significant big leaguers in Kyle Farnsworth, Dexter Fowler and Dylan Cease. He has a lot of upside and projection remaining and could develop into a first-rounder in '26 if he follows through on his college commitment to Florida. McNeillie's fastball currently parks at 88-91 mph and tops out at 94, but it plays better than its velocity. He uses both two-seamers and four-seamers while working all four quadrants of the strike zone, and his three-quarters delivery creates impressive life, angle and deception. He should throw harder once he adds some much-needed strength to his thin 6-foot-2 frame. McNeillie likes to attack hitters with his heater and will have to use his secondary pitches more often against more advanced hitters. He has the potential for three solid offerings as he achieves good sweep on a mid-70s slider and tough sink on a low-80s changeup. He has a quick arm and an easy, repeatable delivery that allow him to pound the strike zone.

José Veras was a big right-handed pitcher who carved out a nine-year big league career as a reliever for eight different teams after signing out of the Dominican Republic. His equally large son Genson wants to go into the family business, albeit in a different division. Veras has gone to TNXL Academy in Florida for high school and offers some serious offensive upside, though swing-and-miss on the summer showcase circuit really hurt his Draft stock.The younger Veras stands at 6-foot-6, just like his dad, and is a lean and strong 225 pounds. A right-handed hitter and thrower, he possesses the tools of an everyday outfielder if he can get to them consistently. After posting a large strikeout rate over the summer, he made some adjustments during his senior year, both in his approach and in simplifying his swing, helping him get to his huge raw power a bit more consistently while facing a lot of junior college pitching.Veras runs well for his size, clocking solid 60-yard dash times and showing he can steal a base. He’s played center field for TNXL, and a team could send him out there, but he’s probably best suited for right, where his above-average arm would play well. The team deciding to try to sign him away from his Florida State commitment will be the one who felt they saw enough this spring to believe he can make enough contact to get to the impact necessary for an outfield corner at the next level.

The Oregon high school baseball scene has produced some serious early-round talent in recent years, though most of it has come on the mound, with first-rounders like Mick Abel, Noble Meyer and Kruz Schoolcraft. The 2026 prep class in the Beaver State is a bit thinner, but there are some interesting hitters to consider in the top several rounds. Nelson is among the best of the group, a name that was moving up boards the more scouts saw him this spring, giving him the chance to be just the second Oregonian high school hitter, joining 2025 draftee Slater de Brun, to go in the top five rounds since 2012.Nelson is a left-handed-hitting shortstop who you might have to see more than once to fully appreciate. He has some feel for the barrel, with some looseness to his swing and some bat speed, to go along with solid pitch recognition. He lacks impact right now, with a tendency to have too much pull-side roll over contact. There’s a little juice to that side and given his clean bat path and ability to carry the barrel through the zone, there’s some hope that there will be some power to come as he fills out his frame.A plus runner, Nelson is an aggressive baserunner who can steal bags. His athleticism plays on the dirt as well, and he couples it with a good game clock and instincts to give him the chance to play shortstop for a long time, with more than enough arm for the premium position. He’s committed to Loyola Marymount for college ball.

Hailing from Oakton High School in Virginia, Lavey stayed in the region to head to George Washington University to extend his baseball career. He made an immediate impact as a freshman, earning second-team All Atlantic 10 honors, repeating the feat again as a junior. In between, he was a Cape Cod League All-Star. His solid showing on the Cape, carrying that over to the 2026 season and constant need for decent all-around catchers had teams considering Lavey in the top several rounds.Lavey checks off an oft-coveted box as a left-handed-hitting catcher. Nothing he does is flashy, but he can do everything pretty well. He has an easy swing, with a line-drive, up the middle approach, and he has the strength to drive the ball at times, with solid exit velocity data to back that up. He doesn’t swing and miss or chase much and walked more than he struck out as a junior.Behind the plate, there’s work to be done, but he has the makings of a solid defensive backstop, one with an above-average arm and pop times under 2.00 seconds. He’d receive better instruction on his glove work on the pro side, so there’s some ceiling to reach there. That and his steady bat had some believing he could go in the top five rounds come Draft time.

Lujo won four Florida state 7-A titles and three national championships in four seasons at Stoneman Douglas HS (Parkland, Fla.). After batting .361 as a freshman at Dayton in 2025, he transferred to Georgia and became one of the best hitters in the Southeastern Conference, posting a .394 average in league play as the Bulldogs won the regular-season and tournament crowns. He's the grandson of Rennie Stennett, who won two World Series rings with the Pirates and is the only modern big leaguer to collect seven hits in a nine-inning game.A right-handed hitter, Lujo has quality bat-to-ball skills and a patient approach. He rarely ventures out of the strike zone and makes repeated line-drive contact against all types of offerings, though he doesn't walk very often. He tends to work fastballs from gap to gap and turn on secondary pitches, and he could enhance his 12-15 homer power if he learns to launch more balls in the air.Lujo uses his plus speed to beat out infield hits and steal bases. A third baseman at Dayton, he has the hands and feet to play the middle infield but throwing issues prevented him from claiming that role at Georgia. He shifted to center field, where he's still honing his instincts but could develop into an average to solid defender with fringy arm strength.

Wright starred in two seasons at Bowling Green State, winning Mid-American Conference Freshman of the Year honors in 2024 and Defensive Player of the Year accolades in 2025. He also led the MAC with a .503 on-base percentage in his first season and with a .406 batting average and .513 OBP before transferring to Tennessee. Though a broken hamate in his left hand required surgery and cost him the first three weeks of the season, he has been the Volunteers' best hitter this season and is one of the more athletic catchers available.Wright combines bat-to-ball skills with an extremely aggressive approach, chasing a ton of pitches yet making a lot of line-drive contact and rarely missing fastballs. The right-handed hitter slugged just .243 with wood bats in the Cape Cod League last summer and doesn't do a lot of damage against premium velocity, so he may not have more than 10-15 homer power. While his swing-at-everything mindset cuts into his walk totals, he enhances his on-base ability by getting hit by an excessive amount of pitches.Unlike most catchers, Wright can flash plus speed and is capable of playing center field. He has spent more time in center than behind the plate this year while recovering from his hamate injury but offers more value as a backstop. He's an effective receiver and blocker with solid arm strength.

Ferraro began his college career at Texas A&M in the fall of 2023 before leaving for Blinn (Texas) JC and winning a Junior College World Series championship as a freshman in 2024. He batted .376/.497/.733 with 36 homers in two seasons with the Pirates, then transferred to Texas Tech and slashed .374/.481/.604 with eight longballs this spring. He comes with some of the best hitting data in this college crop but doesn't offer much value outside of the batter's box.Few college hitters combine the ability to execute good swing decisions, make consistent contact and generate premium exit velocities as well as Ferraro. A left-handed hitter, he has flipped his profile from power-over-hit at Blinn to hit-over-power with the Red Raiders. He owns plus raw pop but prioritizes drilling line drives from gap to gap rather than trying to crush balls to his pull side.Ferraro is a well-below-average runner who's conservative on the bases. He has spent most of this spring at DH with sporadic starts in right field, where he has limited range. He was a substandard defender at first base in junior college, though he does have average arm strength and displayed an upper-80s fastball when he pitched as a freshman.

In 2020, Jacob Berry was a solid high school prospect in Arizona who went on to spend two years in college -- one at Arizona and one at Louisiana State -- en route to being a first-round pick of the Marlins in '22. Now it’s his younger brother Jet’s turn to follow in the family business -- dad, Perry, played pro ball and sister Jade is an infielder on Stanford’s softball team -- as one of the more talented high school hitters in the Four Corners.Like his brother, Jet is a switch-hitter with abilities from both sides, though that’s where the sibling similarities end. The younger Berry is smaller, twitchy and athletic, and while he does have a chance for a little impact from both sides of the plate, he’s more of a hit-over-power kind of guy. His best tool is his easily plus speed, sometimes recording 70 run times, making him a constant threat on the basepaths.Aside from future offensive impact, the other main question around Berry is where he’ll play at the next level. Some scouts won’t rule out his ability to play shortstop, with decent hands and actions, but some feel his arm is short for the left side of the infield. He could be an average defender at second base or center field, though teams might have to wait for two years when he’ll be a Draft-eligible sophomore at Arizona State.

Scolari was playing his high school ball in Reno, Nev., when Tommy John surgery knocked out his senior year. He spent his true freshman year at San Diego rehabbing from the injury and didn’t throw a competitive pitch. Now a Draft-eligible redshirt freshman, Scolari settled in as the Toreros’ Friday night starter in 2025, showing off some arm strength and projection while earning West Coast Conference Pitcher of the Year honors, though his long-term role remains in question.Scolari has added some size and strength to his frame and now stands at 6-foot-4 and 200 pounds. He mostly relies on his fastball and slider combination to get outs. His fastball is up to 96 mph, typically sitting 92-93, but he misses a fair amount of bats with the lively heater. His low-80s slider can be an out pitch, but it’s inconsistent. He does have some developing feel for an upper-80s changeup, though it’s behind the other two offerings.While he has a starting pitcher’s frame and solid delivery, Scolari’s fringy command -- to go along with his lack of a dependable third pitch -- does lead to some starter vs. reliever questions. There also is a bit of a lack of track record here given that the first pitch he threw this season was the first he’d thrown since his junior year of high school, but that also means there could be some intriguing upside potential to reach.

Harvard-Westlake High School in California can boast alumni arms like Lucas Giolito, Max Fried and Jack Flaherty, but they haven’t had one drafted since Flaherty was a first-round pick by the Cardinals in 2016. That could change this year, and scouts could get a 2-for-1 deal when checking out first-round talent Bryce Rainer at shortstop and Marsten on the mound. The right-hander had Tommy John surgery as a sophomore and missed the 2022 season, but he’d been showing enough stuff, including in front of a lot of evaluators at USA Baseball’s National High School Invitational, this spring to generate early-round buzz.The 6-foot-3 right-hander made a big statement right out of the gate, showing a 95-99 mph heater in his first start while flashing a plus breaking ball and changeup. It backed up a little since then, though he was still consistently up to 94 mph with his heater at the NHSI. When it’s on, his slider is up to 85 mph, and it’s short and tight. Some area scouts think his changeup might be even better, thrown 82-84 mph with excellent depth. He seems to have scrapped a slower curve he’d used in previous seasons, but there’s feel to spin here.Marsten is strong and athletic on the mound with a sound and repeatable delivery that reminds some of Gerrit Cole, pointing to a future of strike-throwing with a north-south arsenal. Some teams may pause because of the injury history or the fact he’ll be 19 at the Draft, but he could be a very exciting Draft-eligible sophomore coming out of Wake Forest’s pitching lab should he head to school.

Shelar is the latest prospect out of a Marist School (Atlanta) program that has produced seven big leaguers, most notably Kyle Farmer, and won 14 Georgia state championships. He's a physical player with an interesting combination of raw power and solid speed, and a team that believes in his bat could pay him a seven-figure bonus. If that doesn't happen, he'll head to Mississippi State.Already 6-foot-2 and 205 pounds, Shelar projects to have well-above-average raw power thanks to his combination of bat speed, strength and leverage. There are mixed opinions about his bat, with some scouts worrying about his ability to make consistent contact against pro pitching. He takes aggressive hacks with an unorthodox right-handed stroke that can get uphill.If he does hit enough to tap into his pop, Shelar has the solid speed to become at least a 20-20 player. A center fielder in high school, he doesn’t have a quick first step but covers ground once he gets going. He likely will wind up on a corner in the long run and has the arm strength and offensive profile to fit in right field.

TNXL Academy in Florida has a trio of Draft prospects who could all go in the top several rounds. Genson Veras is the bat scouts like, while evaluators can pick between a pair of different arms in Coleton Brady and Spencer Evans. While Brady’s feel for pitching is better, Evans has showcased better pure stuff from the left side, starting at summer showcase events like East Coast Pro Showcase and USA Baseball’s 18U National Team Camp and continuing into this year, albeit with less strikes overall.Evans offers a combination of size (6-foot-3), projection and arm strength. Combine that with the fact that he's left-handed, and that’s sure to intrigue teams. At times, he can look like a first-round caliber talent, with a fastball routinely up to 95-96 mph and flashing two above-average secondary offerings. He’s added velocity to what is now a low-80s slider and can miss a lot of bats with it, throwing the breaker with good angles. His mid-80s changeup continues to improve.As good as that package sounds, Evans is often stuff over performance, with his command and control the biggest culprit in keeping him ineffective. He’s prone to walks and comes with a little left-handed reliever risk, but he’s athletic and moves easily on the mound. A team that sees enough future starter in him could try to sign him away from his commitment to Louisiana State.

Every year there are players who take a large step forward in their Draft season, pop-up prospects as they’re generally known. Barker has been one of those players in the Four Corners. After not being seen much on the summer showcase circuit, Barker started showing off his raw tools as his Casteel High School made it to USA Baseball’s National High School Invitational semifinals, and that continued this spring as Casteel advanced to the second round of the Arizona state playoffs.Barker is a super-athletic left-handed hitter who plays the infield in high school. Everything about him is a little raw, but there are glimpses of him doing everything well. It’s a hit-over-power approach, and those who like him see an average hit tool in there, though he’ll have to continue to add strength to his 6-foot-1 frame to get to more impact, even if he’s never a huge power guy. The one purely plus tool he has is his speed, and some scouts have seen 70-grade run times from him occasionally.Where Barker best profiles defensively is also a question. He has some abilities to play short but had been seeing most of his time on the dirt at third base this spring. His bat might not profile well for the corner infield spot, and his overall defense needs work, with some thinking he might fit in a 3B/2B/LF kind of role. Those who like him are all about projection, but some think he’d be better off going to Texas Tech, where he’d be a Draft-eligible sophomore in two years.

High school players in the northeast are often late risers simply because it takes a while for the weather to warm up and for them to get going. Add in a winter sport like basketball to delay being seen as another reason for a player to be a bit of a pop-up guy. And when that player didn’t play on the summer showcase circuit, they can really seem like they come out of nowhere. Plunkett checks off all three boxes, but the New York prepster’s name was coming up more frequently in scout chatter as the season moved closer to the Draft.An athletic 6-foot-3, Plunkett certainly looks the part. The right-handed hitter has a smooth swing that is currently line-drive oriented, but it’s easy to project plenty more raw power to come because his frame should be able to add plenty of strength as he matures. He’ll show it off in batting practice at times, but there’s not that much impact in games to date.Plunkett’s athleticism also helps him defensively. He looks like he could stick at shortstop for a while, with enough arm and more to come, good actions and solid instincts. He might look like a collection of 50 grades, but that’s a solid big leaguer, and there could be a lot more in the tank. He’s committed to Binghamton for college, but there’s been some buzz that he could be like a Matthew Ferrara type, who was an over-slot ninth-round signing by the Phillies in 2025, or even Michael Oliveto, a surprise supplemental first-round pick by the Tigers last year.

A two-way star in high school whose future is on the mound, Willoughby scored the game-winning run in extra innings in the Kentucky state championship game last June before performing well on the showcase circuit. He's a projectable athlete with the potential for three solid or better pitches once he's fully developed. A Kentucky commit, he'd be eligible for the Draft as a 21-year-old sophomore in 2028.Willoughby's fastball sits at 93-95 mph and tops out at 97 with armside run in short stints, though he works more in the low 90s when he ventures deeper into games. He displays advanced feel for a mid-80s changeup with fade and sink, and his cambio could become a plus pitch. He trusts it more than an upper-70s slider that features promising sweep but needs more power.Willoughby has a clean three-quarters delivery that translates into strikes with all three of his offerings. He already can spot his fastball to both sides of the plate and should add more velocity as he fills out his 6-foot-2 frame. He might blossom into a mid-rotation starter if he can refine a harder, more reliable slider.

Gallaher has established himself as one of the most clutch playoff performers in college baseball, earning MVP honors at NCAA regionals in each of his first two seasons while leading North Carolina to the Men's College World Series in 2024 and a victory shy of a return last June. Though he was the Tar Heels' best hitter as a sophomore, he hasn't performed as well this spring and scouts aren't quite sure what to make of him. He has slugged just .316 with two homers in 52 games during two summers with wood bats in the Cape Cod League, which hurts his cause.A right-handed hitter, Gallaher doesn't have any obvious weaknesses at the plate. He manages the strike zone well enough, shows the ability to handle lefties and righties and all types of pitches and makes hard flyball contact from gap to gap. His lack of power with wood is a concern, especially with a player who may not be able to handle a challenging defensive position.A below-average runner with average arm strength, Gallaher manned third base in his first two college seasons but the game sped up on him too much there. UNC shifted him to second base this year and he has played better than scouts expected. Some still question whether he has the actions and quickness to man the keystone in pro ball, so he could wind up in left field.

Scouts have been trying to figure out Vigue since his days as Massachusetts high schooler, when he could be unhittable at times and unable to find the strike zone others. That pattern continued in two years at Michigan, where he went undrafted as a sophomore-eligible in 2025. After switching schools, he has had an up-and-down season as Georgia's No. 2 starter and missed a turn in the rotation in late April with forearm tightness.Vigue gets more horizontal separation between his fastball and slider than almost any pitcher in the college crop. His 93-95 mph sinker reaches 98 with significant armside run, while his mid-80s slider has huge sweep in the opposite direction. He has utilized those two offerings 95 percent of the time this spring, rarely using a low-90s cutter and upper-80s changeup.Vigue generates plenty of swings and misses with his slider and weak contact with his fastball, but he has great difficulty landing either pitch in the zone on a regular basis. He rarely works past five innings, running up high pitch counts when hitters lay off his slider or he can't command his sinker. He has a strong 6-foot-3, 230-pound frame and a delivery that should provide more strikes, giving hope that he can add a third pitch and succeed as a starter in pro ball, but realistically he's more of a reliever.

Jorvorskie Lane Sr. rushed for a Texas A&M-record 49 touchdowns before spending three seasons in the National Football League. His son punishes baseballs rather than linebackers and is one of the more intriguing athletes and youngest players in the 2026 prep class. He projected as a first-round pick and drew Andrew McCutchen comps earlier in his high school career, though struggles during the summer and his senior season have dropped his stock.Lane has the potential for four solid-or-better tools but the key to his success will be the fifth. He creates impressive bat speed and raw power but his right-handed swing gets too long and uphill. He has difficulty making contact against quality pitching and does little damage when he doing so.Lane features plus speed and some evaluators give him the same grades for his arm and center-field play. There are some mixed reviews about his defense and thoughts that the Arkansas recruit may wind up on an outfield corner. He has also seen time at catcher and in the middle infield.
